So instead of shuffling millions of raw rows, it shuffles a handful of partial sums per key. This is why sum/count/avg (associative aggregations) are cheap, but collect_list (must gather every raw value) is expensive — it can't be partially aggregated.

5.5 dropDuplicates

dropDuplicates(["user_id"]) is wide — Spark must compare rows across the whole dataset, so it shuffles by the key columns to co-locate potential duplicates, then keeps one per group. On large data this is a full shuffle; if you only need approximate distinct counts, approx_count_distinct avoids it.

5.6 explode

explode(col) turns one row containing an array into multiple rows (one per element) — the SQL "unnest". Physically it's narrow (per-partition, no shuffle), but it can massively inflate row count: a row with a 1000-element array becomes 1000 rows. Watch the downstream memory and partition sizes after an explode.

5.7 pivot

groupBy("country").pivot("month").sum("amount") turns distinct values of a column into new columns. It's wide (a grouped aggregation) and requires Spark to know the distinct pivot values — if you don't supply them, Spark runs an extra job just to find them. Always pass the value list (pivot("month", ["Jan","Feb",...])) in production to skip that scan.

Common pitfall: Chaining dozens of withColumn calls (common when translating step-by-step pandas code) bloats the logical plan and can slow analysis/codegen dramatically. Collapse into a single select/selectExpr. Also, calling inferSchema=True on huge CSVs in production silently doubles your read cost — declare the schema.
Key takeaways:
  • Explicit schema > inference (inference costs an extra data pass and can guess wrong); Parquet/ORC carry schema for free.
  • Columns are lazy expressions, not data — that's what makes them optimizable.
  • select/withColumn/filter/when are narrow (no shuffle, fused by codegen); groupBy-agg, dropDuplicates, pivot are wide (shuffle).
  • Associative aggregations (sum/count/avg) get cheap map-side partial aggregation; collect_list does not.
  • explode is narrow but inflates rows; supply pivot values to avoid an extra scan.

6. Joins & Shuffle Mechanics

The shuffle is the single most important performance concept in Spark. It is the operation of redistributing data across the cluster so that related records land together, and it is expensive because it involves disk writes, network transfer, and disk reads. Almost every "why is my Spark job slow?" answer comes back to a shuffle. This chapter dissects it, then applies it to joins.

6.1 What a shuffle actually IS

Suppose you groupBy("country"). The rows for "US" might currently be scattered across all 200 partitions on all executors. To sum per country, all "US" rows must reach one place. A shuffle achieves this in three phases:

  1. Map-side write (shuffle write). Each task reads its partition and, for every row, computes a target partition (usually hash(country) % numShufflePartitions). It writes rows into per-target buckets and spills them to local disk as shuffle files. (Disk write #1.)
  2. Exchange (the transfer). Every reduce task must fetch, over the network, the bucket destined for it from every map task. This is an all-to-all transfer — N map tasks × M reduce tasks connections. (Network #2.)
  3. Reduce-side read (shuffle read). Each reduce task reads all its fetched pieces, merges them, and now has all rows for its set of keys, ready to aggregate/join. (Disk read #3.)
Shuffle: all-to-all redistribution by key hash 
   MAP SIDE (write to local disk)          REDUCE SIDE (read over network)
   partition 0   partition 1                 reduce 0        reduce 1
   +--------+   +--------+                    (keys hash 0)  (keys hash 1)
   | US, US |   | US     |                    +----------+   +----------+
   | UK     |   | UK, UK |                    |  all US  |   |  all UK  |
   +---+----+   +---+----+                    +----+-----+   +----+-----+
       |            |                              ^              ^
    hash&bucket  hash&bucket                       |              |
       |            |        =====EXCHANGE=========+              |
       +----[US->r0][UK->r1]-----(network fetch)------------------+
            [US->r0][UK->r1]

   Cost = disk write + all-to-all network + disk read.  THIS is the shuffle.

6.2 Wide vs narrow transformations & stage boundaries

This is the theoretical backbone:

  • Narrow transformation — each output partition depends on one input partition (map, filter, select, withColumn). No data movement; can be pipelined together.
  • Wide transformation — each output partition depends on many input partitions (groupBy, join, distinct, repartition). Requires a shuffle.

Spark breaks the DAG into stages at every wide transformation. A stage is a set of narrow operations that can run pipelined without shuffling; the boundary between stages is a shuffle. So the number of shuffles = number of stage boundaries − 1, roughly. Minimizing shuffles = minimizing stages = the core of Spark tuning.

6.3 Join strategies — mechanics and selection

A join needs matching keys together, which normally means a shuffle — but not always. Spark chooses among three main strategies:

StrategyMechanicsChosen when
Broadcast Hash JoinThe small table is copied in full to every executor; each executor builds a hash table from it and probes it with its local partitions of the big table. No shuffle of the big table.One side is small enough to fit in memory (default under spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold, ~10 MB). The single best case.
Shuffle Hash JoinBoth sides shuffled by key; each reduce task builds a hash table from the smaller side's partition and probes with the other. No sort needed.Neither side is broadcastable but one side is much smaller and fits in memory per partition.
Sort-Merge JoinBoth sides shuffled by key, each partition sorted by key, then merged in a single pass like a zipper.The default for two large tables. Robust (doesn't need a side to fit in memory) but pays for sorting.
Worked example — force a broadcast: 
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
big.join(broadcast(small_dim), "product_id")
Physically: small_dim is collected to the driver, then broadcast to every executor (one copy each). The huge big table is never shuffled — each executor joins its local partitions against the in-memory hash table. A 5 TB×5 MB join that would have been a monstrous sort-merge shuffle becomes an almost-free narrow operation. This is the highest-leverage join optimization in Spark.

6.4 Bucketing theory

If you repeatedly join two big tables on the same key, you pay the shuffle every time. Bucketing pre-shuffles the data at write time: you write both tables bucketed by the join key into the same number of buckets (hash(key) % numBuckets). Then at read time, matching keys are already co-located in corresponding buckets, so the join can be a sort-merge join without a shuffle — the expensive redistribution was done once, at write, and amortized over many reads. The cost is the upfront bucketed write and the rigidity (bucket count is fixed). It's a warehouse-style optimization for known, repeated join patterns.

Common pitfall: Relying on the auto-broadcast threshold to "just work". Spark estimates table size from statistics; if stats are missing or stale (common after transformations), it may not broadcast a table that would fit, defaulting to an expensive sort-merge join. When you know a side is small, wrap it in broadcast() explicitly. Conversely, broadcasting a table that's actually large OOMs every executor at once.
Key takeaways:
  • A shuffle = map-side write to local disk → all-to-all network exchange → reduce-side read; it's the dominant cost in most jobs.
  • Narrow = one input partition per output (no shuffle); wide = many inputs per output (shuffle). Stages break at every wide op.
  • Join strategies: broadcast hash (small side copied everywhere, no big-side shuffle — best), shuffle hash (both shuffled, hash probe), sort-merge (both shuffled + sorted — default for two large tables).
  • Explicit broadcast() is the highest-leverage join optimization; bucketing pre-shuffles at write time to avoid the shuffle on repeated joins.

7. Partitioning Theory

"Partition" is used for several different things in Spark, and conflating them causes endless confusion. This chapter separates them cleanly: in-memory partitions (parallelism units), shuffle partitions (config), and on-disk partitioning (directory layout at write). Master these distinctions and a whole class of Spark bugs disappears.

7.1 Where the partition count comes from

  • On read, partition count is driven by input splits: Spark divides input files into chunks of roughly spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes (default 128 MB). A 12.8 GB dataset → ~100 partitions. Many tiny files, however, tend to become many tiny partitions (the small-files problem, 7.4).
  • After a shuffle, the partition count is set by spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (default 200). So every groupBy/join output has 200 partitions unless you change it or AQE coalesces them (Chapter 9). 200 is a mediocre default — too many for small data (tiny tasks, overhead), too few for very large data (huge tasks, spills).
Worked example — partition math: You read a 40 GB dataset with default 128 MB splits: 40,000 MB / 128 MB ≈ 313 partitions. Good — that's plenty to keep a few hundred cores busy. But then you groupBy, and output jumps to exactly 200 shuffle partitions regardless of data size. If the grouped result is still 40 GB, each of the 200 partitions is 200 MB — fine. If it's only 1 GB, you now have 200 tiny 5 MB partitions and 200 tasks of pure overhead. This is why you tune spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to the data (or let AQE do it).

7.2 repartition vs coalesce

repartition(n)coalesce(n)
Directionincrease OR decreasedecrease only
Shuffle?YES — full shuffle, evenly balanced resultNO — merges adjacent partitions without moving data across the network
Balanceeven (hash/round-robin)can be uneven (merged blindly)
Use forfixing skew, increasing parallelism, repartitioning by a columncheaply reducing partition count before write (e.g. many→few output files)

The mechanism: coalesce avoids a shuffle by simply grouping existing partitions together on the same executors — cheap, but if you coalesce from 200 to 1, all data funnels through one task (no parallelism, likely OOM). repartition pays for a full shuffle to guarantee balanced, arbitrary partition counts. Rule: reducing partitions for a smaller write → coalesce; needing even balance or more partitions → repartition.

7.3 partitionBy (on write) vs in-memory partitioning — the constant confusion

These share a word but are completely different things:

  • In-memory partitioning (repartition, shuffle partitions) controls how data is split across executors during computation — a runtime, performance concern. Invisible on disk.
  • On-disk partitioning (df.write.partitionBy("date")) controls the directory layout of the output files: Spark writes /date=2026-01-01/, /date=2026-01-02/, ... folders. This is a storage concern that enables partition pruning on future reads: a query filtering date='2026-01-01' reads only that one folder, skipping the rest.
Worked example — how they interact and go wrong: 
df.write.partitionBy("country").parquet("out/")
If df currently has 200 in-memory partitions and 100 countries, then each of the 200 tasks may write a small file into each country folder → up to 200×100 = 20,000 tiny files. The fix is to align in-memory partitioning with the write partitioning first: df.repartition("country").write.partitionBy("country") so each country's data is in one in-memory partition and produces one file per folder. Confusing the two "partitionings" is exactly how the small-files disaster below is born.

7.4 The small-files problem

Genesis: distributed engines write one file per (in-memory partition × on-disk partition). Streaming micro-batches, over-partitioned writes, and high shuffle.partitions all multiply file count. Millions of tiny files are toxic: each file has fixed open/metadata overhead, listing a directory of millions of files is slow, and reading them wastes I/O and defeats columnar efficiency. Fixes: coalesce/repartition before write to control output file count; choose a sensible partitionBy granularity (partition by date, not timestamp); run periodic compaction jobs (Delta/Iceberg OPTIMIZE) that rewrite many small files into few large ones; target ~128 MB–1 GB files.

Common pitfall: "I'll partitionBy the high-cardinality column so queries are fast." Partitioning by something with millions of distinct values (like user_id) creates millions of tiny folders — catastrophic. On-disk partitioning is for low-cardinality columns you filter on (date, region, category). For high-cardinality access patterns use bucketing or sorting instead.
Key takeaways:
  • Read partition count comes from input splits (~128 MB each); post-shuffle count comes from spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (default 200 — tune it).
  • repartition = full shuffle, balanced, up or down; coalesce = no shuffle, decrease only, possibly uneven.
  • In-memory partitioning = runtime parallelism (invisible on disk); partitionBy on write = directory layout enabling partition pruning. Different concepts, same word.
  • Small files come from too many (in-memory × on-disk) partitions; fix with coalesce/repartition, sane partition columns, and compaction. Never partitionBy a high-cardinality column.

8. Memory Model & Spills

Spark's speed comes from working in memory, so understanding how an executor's memory is divided — and what happens when it runs out — is essential to avoiding the most common production failure: the OutOfMemory error. This chapter maps the executor's memory regions and the mechanics of spilling.

8.1 Executor memory regions

An executor's JVM heap (set by spark.executor.memory) is divided by Spark's Unified Memory Manager into regions:

  • Reserved memory — a fixed sliver (~300 MB) Spark keeps for itself.
  • User memory — for your data structures, UDF objects, etc. (roughly 40% of the non-reserved heap).
  • Unified region (execution + storage) — the remaining ~60%, split into two cooperating sub-pools:
    • Execution memory — working space for shuffles, joins, sorts, aggregations (building hash tables, sort buffers).
    • Storage memory — holds cached/persisted DataFrames and broadcast data.

The "unified" part is the clever bit: execution and storage share one pool and borrow from each other on demand. If no data is cached, execution can use the whole unified region. But execution memory has priority: it can evict cached blocks to reclaim space (because cached data can be recomputed from lineage), whereas storage cannot evict execution memory (that would fail a running task). This dynamic sharing beats the old fixed split.

8.2 Spill-to-disk mechanics

When a task needs more execution memory than is available (e.g. a hash aggregation whose hash table won't fit, or a sort of more rows than fit), Spark doesn't immediately crash — it spills: it sorts/serializes the in-memory portion and writes it to local disk, freeing RAM to continue, then merges the spilled runs at the end. Spilling keeps the job alive but is slow (disk I/O), so heavy spilling is a red flag you'll see in the Spark UI ("Spill (Memory)" / "Spill (Disk)" metrics). Cause is usually too-large partitions (too few shuffle partitions) or skew (Chapter 9).

8.3 OOM taxonomy — driver vs executor

WhereTypical causesFix direction
Driver OOMcollect()/toPandas() on big data; broadcasting a too-large table; huge query plans; many tiny result files' metadatadon't collect big data; increase driver memory; avoid over-broadcast
Executor OOMpartitions too large; heavy skew (one giant partition); high-cardinality groupBy building a massive hash table; too little memory per coremore/ smaller partitions; handle skew; more executor memory; fewer cores per executor

The key diagnostic question is always which process died — the fixes are opposite. Driver OOM is almost always "you pulled too much to the driver"; executor OOM is almost always "a partition was too big to fit".

8.4 Caching levels & eviction

df.cache() (= persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK)) tells Spark to keep a computed DataFrame around so reuse doesn't recompute the whole lineage. Storage levels trade memory vs CPU vs disk:

  • MEMORY_ONLY — fastest, but if it doesn't fit, the missing partitions are recomputed on access.
  • MEMORY_AND_DISK — spill cached blocks to disk if RAM is short (the DataFrame default).
  • MEMORY_ONLY_SER / _SER variants — store serialized (compact) bytes: less RAM, more CPU to deserialize.
  • DISK_ONLY — for very large data reused a few times.

Eviction is LRU within storage memory, and remember execution memory can evict cached blocks under pressure. Only cache what you reuse — caching a DataFrame used once wastes memory and can push out useful blocks. And cache() is lazy: it only materializes on the next action.

8.5 Serialization

Whenever data crosses a boundary — shuffled between executors, cached in serialized form, or sent to/from the driver — it must be serialized (turned into bytes) and deserialized. This is pure overhead, so its cost matters. For RDDs, the Kryo serializer is much faster/compacter than Java's default. For DataFrames, Tungsten's binary format largely handles this efficiently already. The expensive serialization boundary you'll actually hit in PySpark is the Python↔JVM one in UDFs (Chapter 9).

Common pitfall: "Just cache everything to make it fast." Over-caching fills storage memory, triggers evictions and spills, and starves execution memory — making things slower. Cache deliberately, only DataFrames genuinely reused across multiple actions, and unpersist() when done. Also: cache() without a following action does nothing (it's lazy).
Key takeaways:
  • Executor heap = reserved + user + unified(execution ⇄ storage). Execution and storage share dynamically; execution has priority and can evict cached blocks.
  • Spilling writes execution data to disk to survive memory pressure — keeps the job alive but slow; heavy spill = a tuning signal.
  • Driver OOM = you pulled too much to the driver (collect/broadcast); executor OOM = a partition was too big (skew, too few partitions). Identify which process died.
  • Cache only what you reuse; pick a storage level for your memory/CPU trade-off; caching is lazy and evicted LRU.

9. Performance Engineering

This chapter is the toolbox of a Spark performance engineer: diagnosing and fixing skew, leveraging Adaptive Query Execution, tuning broadcasts, understanding the true cost of UDFs, and the interplay of file format with pushdown. These are the levers that turn a 3-hour job into a 15-minute one.

9.1 Data skew — theory and detection

Skew is when data is unevenly distributed across partitions — one key (or a few) has vastly more rows than others. After a shuffle by that key, one partition might hold 90% of the data. Since a stage finishes only when its slowest task finishes, one giant partition means 199 tasks finish in seconds and one task runs for an hour (and may spill or OOM). Detection theory: in the Spark UI's stage view, look at task duration and shuffle-read-size distribution — a huge gap between the median task and the max task (e.g. median 2s, max 40 min) is the skew signature.

9.2 Salting — the classic skew fix, step by step

Salting spreads a hot key across many partitions by adding a random suffix to the key, then reassembling. Mechanically:

  1. Add a random salt (say 0–9) to the skewed side's key: the key US becomes US_0 ... US_9. Now "US" rows spread across 10 partitions instead of 1.
  2. Explode the other side of the join: each US row on the small/other side is replicated 10 times, once per salt (US_0 ... US_9), so every salted key still finds its match.
  3. Join on the salted key. The hot key's work is now split across 10 tasks running in parallel instead of one.
  4. Strip the salt and, if needed, aggregate again to combine the 10 partial results back into the true per-key result.
Worked example — why it works: Before salting, US has 100 million rows all hashing to partition #7 → one task processes 100 M rows (30 min, spills). After salting with 10 buckets, those 100 M rows spread as US_0..US_9 → 10 tasks of ~10 M rows each, running concurrently on 10 slots → ~3 min, no spill. You traded a small amount of extra work (replicating the other side 10×) for parallelism on the bottleneck.

9.3 Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)

AQE (on by default in Spark 3.x, spark.sql.adaptive.enabled) re-optimizes the plan at runtime using actual statistics gathered after each shuffle — something the upfront optimizer can only estimate. Its three headline features:

  • Coalescing shuffle partitions. After a shuffle, AQE sees the real output sizes and merges tiny partitions together — so you don't need to hand-tune shuffle.partitions; set it high and let AQE coalesce down. Solves the "200 tiny partitions" problem automatically.
  • Switching join strategy. If a table turns out (at runtime) to be small enough to broadcast — even though the static estimate said otherwise — AQE converts a sort-merge join into a broadcast join on the fly.
  • Skew join handling. AQE detects skewed partitions after a shuffle and automatically splits the oversized partition into smaller sub-partitions, running them in parallel — an automatic, built-in version of salting for joins.

9.4 Broadcast thresholds

spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (default 10 MB) is the size under which Spark auto-broadcasts a join side. Raise it if your executors have memory to spare and you want larger dimension tables broadcast (e.g. 100 MB); set it to -1 to disable auto-broadcast entirely (useful when bad size estimates cause wrong broadcasts and OOM). And always remember you can force it with broadcast(df) regardless of the threshold.

9.5 The UDF cost model

A UDF (User-Defined Function) lets you run arbitrary Python in a query — but in PySpark it's expensive for a specific mechanical reason. Spark's engine runs in the JVM; your Python UDF runs in a separate Python process. So for every row, data must be serialized from the JVM, sent to Python, deserialized, your function runs, the result is serialized back, sent to the JVM, deserialized. That serialization boundary crossing per row is the killer, and Catalyst also can't see inside your Python function to optimize it (it's a black box — no pushdown through it).

  • Built-in first principle: always prefer built-in pyspark.sql.functions — they run entirely in the JVM/Tungsten, no Python boundary, and Catalyst optimizes them. A regex, date math, or conditional you were about to write as a UDF almost always exists as a built-in.
  • Pandas UDFs (vectorized UDFs) with Arrow: if you truly need Python logic, use pandas UDFs. They use Apache Arrow to transfer data in columnar batches (not row-by-row) and run your function on whole pandas Series at once. This amortizes the serialization cost over thousands of rows per transfer and vectorizes the computation — often 10–100× faster than a plain row-at-a-time UDF.

9.6 File format & pushdown interplay

Performance starts at the file format, because it determines what can be pushed down (Chapter 4). Parquet/ORC are columnar and carry statistics + schema, so predicate pushdown (skip row-groups) and projection pushdown (read only needed columns) both work — Spark can avoid reading most of the data. CSV/JSON are row-based text with no statistics, so Spark must read and parse every byte of every row regardless of your filter/select. Choosing Parquet over CSV is frequently the single biggest speedup available, before any Spark tuning. Compression (Snappy) and sensible row-group sizes further help.

Common pitfall: Reaching for a Python UDF as the first tool because it's familiar. A single row-at-a-time UDF can dominate an entire job's runtime and disable Catalyst optimization across it. Search the built-in functions first; if you must use Python, use a pandas/Arrow UDF; use a plain UDF only as a last resort. Similarly, don't assume AQE fixes everything — it helps after shuffles, but it can't fix a query that was written to shuffle needlessly.
Key takeaways:
  • Skew = one key dominates one partition; the stage waits on that one slow task. Detect via median-vs-max task duration gap in the UI.
  • Salting spreads a hot key across N buckets (replicating the other side N×) to parallelize the bottleneck.
  • AQE re-optimizes at runtime: coalesces small partitions, switches to broadcast joins, and splits skewed join partitions automatically.
  • PySpark UDFs pay a per-row Python↔JVM serialization tax and block Catalyst — prefer built-ins, then pandas/Arrow UDFs.
  • Columnar formats (Parquet/ORC) enable pushdown; CSV/JSON force full reads. Format choice is often the biggest single speedup.

10. Sizing & Configuration

Given a cluster of raw machines, how many executors, cores, and how much memory each? Getting this wrong wastes money (idle resources) or causes failures (OOM, GC storms). This chapter works the sizing math end to end and explains the famous "~5 cores per executor" rule and other key configs.

10.1 The executor sizing math, worked fully

Suppose your cluster is 6 worker nodes, each with 16 cores and 64 GB RAM. We want to carve this into executors.

  1. Reserve overhead per node. The OS + node daemons (e.g. YARN NodeManager) need ~1 core and ~1 GB. Usable per node ≈ 15 cores, 63 GB.
  2. Choose ~5 cores per executor. Empirically, ~5 cores per executor maximizes HDFS/throughput concurrency without too much contention — beyond ~5 concurrent tasks per executor, disk/IO and JVM contention give diminishing returns and hurt throughput. So set spark.executor.cores = 5.
  3. Executors per node. 15 usable cores ÷ 5 cores/executor = 3 executors per node.
  4. Total executors. 3 per node × 6 nodes = 18 executors. Subtract 1 for the driver (in cluster mode the driver takes a slot) → 17 executor instances.
  5. Memory per executor. 63 GB per node ÷ 3 executors/node = 21 GB gross. Now subtract off-heap overhead (spark.executor.memoryOverhead, ~max(384 MB, 7–10% of executor memory)) ≈ ~2 GB. So spark.executor.memory ≈ 19 GB.
Result of the worked example: 
spark.executor.instances = 17
spark.executor.cores     = 5
spark.executor.memory    = 19g
# total parallelism = 17 executors × 5 cores = 85 tasks at once
Contrast two bad extremes on the same cluster: Fat executors (1 executor per node, 15 cores, 60 GB) → excessive GC on the huge heap and poor HDFS throughput from 15 concurrent tasks hammering the same JVM. Tiny executors (15 executors per node, 1 core each) → each executor can't share broadcast/cache data with siblings, per-executor overhead multiplies, and broadcasts are copied 15× per node. The ~5-core middle is why the rule exists.

10.2 Why ~5 cores (the "why", not just the number)

Two forces bracket the choice. Too many cores per executor → many tasks share one JVM heap and one set of I/O channels, so garbage collection pauses lengthen and disk/network throughput per task drops (HDFS clients degrade past ~5 concurrent reads). Too few cores per executor → you have many small JVMs, each duplicating broadcast variables and cached data, with more scheduling and memory overhead, and less ability to share work. ~5 balances concurrency against contention and sharing.

10.3 Dynamic allocation

Dynamic allocation (spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled) lets Spark add executors when there's a backlog of pending tasks and release idle ones back to the cluster manager — so a job that's briefly huge and mostly small doesn't hog a fixed fleet. It needs an external shuffle service so shuffle files survive when an executor is removed. Great for shared clusters and bursty workloads; for a dedicated, steady job a fixed count can be more predictable.

10.4 Common configs, explained

ConfigWhat it controls
spark.executor.instances / cores / memorythe fleet size and shape (above)
spark.executor.memoryOverheadoff-heap memory for shuffle buffers, Python (PySpark!), native libs — undersizing this causes "container killed by YARN for exceeding memory" errors
spark.sql.shuffle.partitionspost-shuffle partition count (default 200) — set high, let AQE coalesce
spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThresholdauto-broadcast size cutoff (default 10 MB)
spark.sql.adaptive.enabledturn on AQE (default true in 3.x)
spark.driver.memorydriver heap — raise if you legitimately collect large results or have huge plans
Common pitfall: In PySpark, forgetting that the Python worker process lives in memoryOverhead, not the executor heap. Heavy Python UDFs or large broadcast dicts in Python can blow overhead and get containers killed even though the JVM heap looks fine. If you see "container killed for exceeding memory limits," raise memoryOverhead, not just executor.memory.
Key takeaways:
  • Sizing recipe: reserve OS overhead per node → pick ~5 cores/executor → executors per node = usable cores ÷ 5 → memory per executor = usable RAM ÷ executors/node − overhead.
  • ~5 cores balances GC/IO contention (too many) against duplicated overhead and poor sharing (too few).
  • Dynamic allocation scales executors with demand (needs an external shuffle service); good for bursty/shared clusters.
  • In PySpark, the Python worker lives in memoryOverhead — undersize it and containers get killed.

11. Structured Streaming

Spark can process not just static datasets but unbounded streams — data arriving continuously (Kafka topics, file drops, event hubs). Structured Streaming is Spark's streaming engine, and its brilliance is a single conceptual trick: you write the same DataFrame code you'd write for a batch, and Spark runs it incrementally forever. This chapter builds the streaming mental model from that idea.

11.1 The unbounded table model

The core abstraction: think of the stream as an unbounded input table to which new rows are continuously appended. Your query (a groupBy, a filter, a join) is defined against that table as if it were static; Spark's job is to keep the result table up to date as new input rows arrive. You reason about what the answer should be, not how to incrementalize it — Spark handles the incremental execution. This is why streaming code looks almost identical to batch code.

11.2 Micro-batching

Under the hood, the default engine is micro-batch: Spark repeatedly wakes up, grabs the new data that arrived since last time, processes it as a tiny Spark batch job, updates the result, and commits progress. So "streaming" is really a fast loop of small batch jobs. This gives you Spark's full batch power (Catalyst, joins, aggregations) on streams, at the cost of some latency (seconds, not milliseconds). (A low-latency "Continuous Processing" mode exists but is limited; micro-batch is the norm.)

11.3 Triggers

The trigger controls how often the micro-batch loop fires:

  • Default — fire the next micro-batch as soon as the previous finishes (lowest latency, continuous).
  • processingTime="1 minute" — fire on a fixed interval.
  • availableNow / once — process all currently available data, then stop (great for cheap, scheduled "incremental batch" jobs that use streaming's bookkeeping without running 24/7).

11.4 Output modes

The output mode defines what gets written to the sink each batch:

  • Append — only brand-new result rows (that won't change again) are written. For simple maps/filters, or aggregations once a watermark closes them.
  • Update — only result rows that changed this batch are written (for keyed aggregations that keep updating).
  • Complete — the entire result table is rewritten every batch (only feasible for small aggregate results; impossible for unbounded ones).

11.5 Event time vs processing time

A critical distinction. Processing time = when Spark handles the record. Event time = when the event actually happened (a timestamp inside the data). They differ because of network delays, retries, mobile devices going offline: an event that happened at 10:00 might arrive at 10:07. For correct analytics ("how many sales in the 10:00–10:05 window?") you must window by event time, which means Spark has to cope with events arriving late and out of order.

11.6 Watermarks — late data mechanics

If you group by event-time windows, Spark must keep each window's state in memory to accept possible late arrivals — but it can't keep all windows forever (unbounded state → OOM). A watermark is your promise: "I will not accept data more than T late." Spark tracks the max event time seen and, once (max_event_time − T) passes a window's end, it closes that window, emits its final result, and drops its state. Data arriving after the watermark for a closed window is discarded.

Worked example — a watermark timeline (5-minute windows, 10-minute watermark): 
event_time  arrives   window        state
--------------------------------------------------------------
10:02       10:03     [10:00-10:05]  open, counted
10:04       10:12     [10:00-10:05]  open, counted (8 min late, OK)
            (max event time seen = 10:12, watermark = 10:12 - 10m = 10:02)
10:22       10:23     [10:20-10:25]  open
            (max event time = 10:22, watermark = 10:12; window [10:00-10:05]
             end 10:05 < watermark? no yet... as time advances...)
max event   10:40     watermark = 10:30  -> [10:00-10:05] end(10:05) < 10:30
                                          => CLOSE it, emit final count, drop state
10:04 event 10:45     [10:00-10:05] already closed => DISCARDED (too late)
The watermark trades completeness (a longer watermark accepts later data) against state size / latency (a shorter watermark closes windows sooner, using less memory and emitting results faster). You pick T based on how late your data realistically arrives.

11.7 Stateful aggregation

Windowed aggregations, streaming joins, and deduplication all require Spark to remember information between micro-batches — the running count per window, the join buffer, seen keys. This is state, kept in a state store on the executors (and checkpointed). Watermarks are what bound this state so it doesn't grow forever. Unbounded state (e.g. a streaming distinct with no watermark) is the classic streaming memory leak.

11.8 Checkpointing & exactly-once

For a stream to survive restarts and failures without losing or duplicating data, Spark checkpoints its progress to durable storage (S3/HDFS): the offsets consumed from each source and the current state. On restart it reads the checkpoint and resumes exactly where it left off. Exactly-once processing is achieved by combining two properties:

  • Replayable source. The source (e.g. Kafka) must let Spark re-read from a known offset. On failure Spark rewinds to the last checkpointed offset and reprocesses the uncommitted batch — so no data is lost (at-least-once from the source side).
  • Idempotent / transactional sink. Because a batch might be reprocessed, the sink must not double-count it. An idempotent sink (e.g. writing with a deterministic file/txn id, or a transactional sink like Delta Lake that commits the batch atomically with its ID) ensures a reprocessed batch overwrites rather than duplicates.

Replayable source + idempotent sink + checkpointed offsets = end-to-end exactly-once, even though internally batches can be retried. This is the same source-replay + idempotent-sink pattern discussed in the Kafka section.

Common pitfall: Windowed aggregation with no watermark. Spark keeps every window's state forever, and the streaming job slowly consumes all executor memory until it OOMs days later. Any event-time stateful operation needs a watermark to bound state. Also: never delete or share a checkpoint directory between different queries — it stores query-specific offsets and state, and reusing it corrupts progress.
Key takeaways:
  • Structured Streaming models a stream as an unbounded input table; you write batch-style code and Spark runs it incrementally (micro-batch = a fast loop of tiny batch jobs).
  • Triggers control cadence; output modes (append/update/complete) control what's written each batch.
  • Window by event time for correctness; watermarks bound how late data is accepted, trading completeness vs state size, and close/emit windows.
  • Stateful ops need bounded state (watermarks) or they leak memory.
  • Exactly-once = replayable source + checkpointed offsets + idempotent/transactional sink.

12. Spark in the Ecosystem

Spark rarely runs "bare". In practice you meet it wrapped inside a managed platform, launched via spark-submit, and — importantly — you should know when not to use it at all. This chapter places Spark in the real-world landscape.

12.1 Where Spark runs: the platform landscape

PlatformWhat it isCharacter
Databricksthe commercial platform from Spark's creators; managed Spark + Delta Lake + notebooks + Unity Catalog + Photon (a native C++ engine that accelerates Spark SQL)the smoothest Spark experience; opinionated, feature-rich, cross-cloud
AWS EMRAWS-managed Hadoop/Spark clusters on EC2flexible, cheaper raw compute, more DIY ops; integrates with S3/Glue
Azure Synapse / Microsoft FabricMicrosoft's analytics suites bundling Spark pools with warehousing/BI (Fabric is the newer unified SaaS)tight Azure/Power BI integration; good for Microsoft-shops
Standalone / self-managedSpark on your own VMs or Kubernetes, no vendor layermaximum control and lowest license cost, maximum operational burden

These are covered in more depth in the Cloud & Platforms sections; the point here is that "learning Spark" means learning the engine, and the platform is a wrapper that handles provisioning, storage integration, and convenience.

12.2 spark-submit anatomy

spark-submit is the command that launches a Spark application. Its arguments map directly to concepts from earlier chapters:

spark-submit \
  --master yarn \                     # which cluster manager
  --deploy-mode cluster \             # driver inside cluster (Ch.2)
  --num-executors 17 \                # fleet size (Ch.10)
  --executor-cores 5 \                # cores per executor (Ch.10)
  --executor-memory 19g \             # heap per executor (Ch.10)
  --conf spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=400 \   # tuning (Ch.7)
  my_job.py --date 2026-07-05         # your script + its args

Reading a spark-submit line tells you exactly how the job is shaped: its cluster manager, deploy mode, and resource allocation. On Databricks/EMR you often set these through the platform UI/JSON instead, but the underlying knobs are identical.

12.3 When NOT to use Spark

Spark has real overhead: JVM startup, cluster coordination, shuffle machinery, serialization. For small data, that overhead dwarfs the actual work, and simpler tools win outright.

  • Data fits comfortably in one machine's RAM (up to tens of GB)? Use pandas or, better for analytics, DuckDB / Polars — an in-process columnar engine that runs SQL/dataframe ops on one machine at extreme speed with zero cluster overhead. For a 5 GB CSV, DuckDB will typically finish before a Spark cluster even finishes starting up.
  • Latency-critical single-row lookups? That's a database's job (OLTP), not Spark's.
  • Truly big data, iterative/multi-stage transforms, or you're already streaming from Kafka at scale? That's Spark's home turf.
Worked example — the decision: A daily job processes 3 GB of transactions and aggregates by merchant. On Spark: 30–60s of it is just cluster startup and JVM warmup for maybe 5s of real work, and you pay for a cluster. In DuckDB (a single Python process) the same job runs in a few seconds on a laptop with no infrastructure. Choose Spark when the data won't fit or the compute genuinely needs many machines — not by default.
Common pitfall: "We're a serious data team, so everything goes on Spark." Running small jobs on Spark burns money and adds latency and operational complexity for no benefit. The mark of maturity is matching the tool to the data size: DuckDB/Polars/pandas for small, Spark for genuinely large or distributed workloads.
Key takeaways:
  • Spark usually runs wrapped in a platform: Databricks (smoothest, + Delta/Photon), EMR (flexible/DIY), Synapse/Fabric (Microsoft-integrated), or self-managed (max control/burden).
  • spark-submit flags map straight to concepts: master (cluster manager), deploy-mode, num/cores/memory of executors, and configs.
  • Don't use Spark for small data — pandas/DuckDB/Polars finish before a cluster starts. Use Spark when data won't fit one machine or compute needs many.

13. Reading the Spark UI

Everything in this section becomes actionable through the Spark UI — the web dashboard (usually on the driver, port 4040, or the History Server after a run) where you diagnose real jobs. Learning to read it is how theory turns into "I found and fixed the slow stage." This chapter maps the UI to the concepts and gives a debugging flowchart.

13.1 The hierarchy: jobs → stages → tasks

The UI mirrors Spark's execution model. An action (collect, write) triggers a job. Each job is split into stages at shuffle boundaries (Chapter 6). Each stage runs many tasks (one per partition). So: the Jobs page shows each action; clicking a job shows its Stages; clicking a stage shows its tasks and their metrics. You navigate down this hierarchy to localize a problem.

13.2 Finding the slow stage

On the Stages page, sort by duration. The stage eating most of the wall-clock is your target. Open it and read its summary metrics table, which shows the distribution (min / 25th / median / 75th / max) of task duration, shuffle read/write size, and spill — the distribution is where the truth is.

13.3 Shuffle read/write metrics

Per stage, Shuffle Write = bytes this stage wrote for the next stage to read; Shuffle Read = bytes this stage pulled from the previous one. Large shuffle numbers confirm you're paying the shuffle cost (Chapter 6); if a "simple" query has surprisingly large shuffle bytes, you're moving more data than expected — a candidate for a broadcast join, better partitioning, or pushing filters earlier.

13.4 Skew signatures

Skew (Chapter 9) shows up unmistakably in the task distribution: median task duration tiny, max task duration huge (e.g. median 3s, max 25 min), and likewise a max shuffle-read far above the median. One straggler task, big spill on that task, and a stage that "hangs at 199/200 tasks" are all the same skew fingerprint. That's your cue to salt, or trust AQE skew handling.

13.5 GC and spill signals

The task table includes a GC Time column. If GC time is a large fraction of task time (say >15–20%), executors are memory-pressured — heaps too large (fat executors), too much cached, or partitions too big. Spill (Memory) and Spill (Disk) columns being non-zero mean tasks ran out of execution memory and spilled (Chapter 8) — usually too-few/too-large partitions or skew. High GC + high spill together = memory tuning needed (more/smaller partitions, right-size executors, cache less).

Debugging flowchart — "my Spark job is slow":
  1. Open the Jobs page → which job/action dominates wall-clock time? Go into it.
  2. Find the slowest stage (sort stages by duration). Open it.
  3. Look at the task duration distribution. Is max ≫ median?
    • Yes → SKEW. Check shuffle-read distribution (one huge task). Fix: salt the hot key, or enable/trust AQE skew join, or broadcast if one side is small.
  4. Uniform but slow tasks? Check shuffle read/write size.
    • Huge shuffle → can you avoid it? Broadcast a small side, filter earlier (pushdown), pre-aggregate, or bucket for repeated joins.
  5. Check Spill and GC columns.
    • High spill → partitions too big: increase shuffle.partitions (or let AQE coalesce), repartition.
    • High GC → executor heap pressure: smaller/more executors (~5 cores), cache less, right-size memory.
  6. Check the SQL / DAG tab & explain(). Is a SortMergeJoin where a BroadcastHashJoin should be? Are PushedFilters present? Are there needless shuffles (an accidental repartition, a wide op you could avoid)?
  7. Is the job even worth Spark? If data is small, the answer might be DuckDB, not tuning (Chapter 12).
Common pitfall: Reading only the total/average stage time and missing the distribution. Averages hide skew — a stage averaging 10s can contain one 25-minute straggler. Always look at the min/median/max task metrics, not just the headline number. Also, tuning blind (bumping memory randomly) without first reading the UI wastes hours; let the metrics point you to the actual bottleneck.
Key takeaways:
  • UI hierarchy mirrors execution: action → job → stages (split at shuffles) → tasks (one per partition). Drill down to localize problems.
  • Find the slowest stage, then read the task distribution (min/median/max), not the average.
  • Skew signature = max task ≫ median task (and lopsided shuffle read); one straggler / "199 of 200 done".
  • Non-zero Spill = memory-starved tasks (too-big partitions/skew); high GC = executor heap pressure — both are memory-tuning cues.
  • Use the SQL/DAG tab + explain() to catch wrong join strategies, missing pushdown, and needless shuffles before changing configs.

Apache Kafka — Complete Theory

From "what is an event?" down to partition offsets, ISR high-watermarks, idempotent producer sequence numbers, exactly-once transactions and Debezium CDC — the full mental model of the append-only log that powers modern streaming, built from zero.

1. The Event Streaming Paradigm

Before a single Kafka term makes sense, you must understand the shift in how systems talk to each other that Kafka embodies. Kafka is not a database and it is not a message queue in the classic sense — it is a distributed, append-only log, and everything it does grows out of that one idea. This chapter builds the idea from zero, because if you truly grasp the log, the rest of Kafka is just engineering details.

1.1 Request/response — the world you already know

Almost every program you have written works in the request/response style. Your code asks for something and waits for an answer: a SQL query, an HTTP call to an API, a function call. The caller and the callee are tightly coupled in time — both must be alive at the same moment, and the caller blocks until the answer comes back. This is perfect for "give me this user's balance right now." But it becomes a trap as a system grows. Suppose an order is placed and five things must happen: charge the card, reserve inventory, send a confirmation email, update analytics, notify the shipping partner. If the order service calls all five directly, it must know about all five, wait for all five, and if any one is slow or down, the whole order stalls. Add a sixth consumer of orders next month and you must edit and redeploy the order service. The order service has become a hub that knows everyone.

1.2 The event — a fact that happened

The event-driven alternative inverts the flow of knowledge. Instead of the order service calling everyone, it simply announces a fact: "OrderPlaced: order 8123, customer 42, amount €90, at 14:03:01." An event is an immutable record of something that already happened, in the past tense. The order service does not know or care who listens. Interested parties subscribe and react on their own schedule. This is decoupling: the producer of an event and its consumers are independent in identity (producer doesn't know consumers), in time (consumer can be offline and catch up later), and in rate (a slow consumer doesn't slow the producer). To add a fraud-detection service next month you deploy it and point it at the order events — you touch nothing that already exists.

Worked example — the fintech ledger: A payments company models money movement as events, never as "current balance" mutations. Every action is a fact: MoneyDeposited, PaymentAuthorized, PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued. The "balance" is not a column someone overwrites — it is the sum of all events for that account, replayed in order. This is event sourcing. Its power: the ledger is an audit trail by construction (you can prove exactly how a balance came to be), you can rebuild any downstream view (fraud model, monthly statement, regulatory report) by replaying the events, and you can add a brand-new view years later that reads history it was never present for. A traditional "UPDATE balance SET amount = amount - 90" destroys the very information regulators demand.

1.3 The log — the core abstraction, explained deeply

Now the central idea. A log in this sense is not application text logs — it is a data structure: an ordered, append-only, immutable sequence of records. Picture a notebook where you may only ever write on the next blank line, never erase or insert between lines. Each line gets the next integer as its position, called its offset: line 0, line 1, line 2, forever forward. Three properties make this humble structure extraordinarily powerful:

  • Ordered. Records have a total order (offset 5 came after offset 4). Order is often the whole point — "deposit €100" then "withdraw €90" must not be reordered into "withdraw €90" (bounces) then "deposit €100."
  • Append-only & immutable. You never modify or delete an existing record; you only add to the end. This is what makes a log safe to read concurrently by many consumers with no locking — nobody is mutating what you're reading.
  • Offset-addressable & replayable. Because every record has a stable position, a reader remembers "I am at offset 4" and simply asks for offset 5 next. Different readers sit at different offsets independently. A reader can rewind to offset 0 and reprocess the entire history — the data wasn't consumed and destroyed, it was merely read.

Contrast this with a classic message queue (like RabbitMQ in its default mode): a message is delivered to one consumer and then deleted. That's fine for a work queue, but it means the data is gone — a second team cannot also read those messages, and you cannot rewind history. Kafka's decision to make the log the primary abstraction — to retain records and let many independent consumers read at their own offsets — is precisely what makes it a backbone for both real-time reaction and later reprocessing. The log is simultaneously a messaging system, a storage system, and a source of truth.

Misconception: "Reading from Kafka removes the message, like popping a queue." No. Consuming does not delete anything. Records leave only when their retention period expires (Chapter 2), completely independent of who has read them. Ten different consumer groups can read the same records; each merely tracks its own offset.
Key takeaways:
  • Request/response couples caller and callee in time and identity; it doesn't scale to many reactors to one action.
  • An event is an immutable past-tense fact; publishing events decouples producers from consumers in identity, time, and rate.
  • The log — an ordered, append-only, immutable, offset-addressable sequence — is Kafka's core abstraction; order, immutability, and replay all fall out of it.
  • Consuming does not delete data; Kafka is storage + messaging at once, which is why replay and multi-consumer reads are possible.

2. Kafka's Data Model — Topics, Partitions, Offsets, and the Disk

Chapter 1 gave us one log. A single log lives on one machine and is read one record at a time, so it caps throughput at what one machine and one reader can do. Kafka's data model is the set of decisions that turn that single log into a horizontally-scalable, durable, replayable system. Every term here is a direct consequence of "how do we scale and persist the log?"

2.1 Topics and partitions

A topic is a named category of events — a logical stream, e.g. orders or payments. But a topic is not one log; it is split into partitions. A partition is the actual physical append-only log from Chapter 1. A topic with 3 partitions is 3 independent logs. This split is the single most important design choice in Kafka, because it delivers two things at once: parallelism (different partitions live on different machines and are read by different consumers simultaneously) and ordered scale (order is guaranteed within a partition but not across partitions). You trade a global order you rarely need for the ability to spread load.

Anatomy of a topic (3 partitions): 
Topic "orders"

Partition 0:  [ off0 ][ off1 ][ off2 ][ off3 ]--> new writes append here -->
Partition 1:  [ off0 ][ off1 ][ off2 ]---------> new writes append here -->
Partition 2:  [ off0 ][ off1 ][ off2 ][ off3 ][ off4 ]--> new writes append -->

- Each partition is an independent, ordered log.
- Offsets are per-partition: "offset 3" means nothing without a partition.
- Order is guaranteed WITHIN a partition, never ACROSS partitions.

2.2 Offsets — position, not identity

An offset is the integer position of a record within its partition, starting at 0 and monotonically increasing. It is assigned by the broker at append time and never reused within that partition. It is not a global ID and not a timestamp — it is purely "the nth record in this particular log." A consumer's entire notion of "where am I" is the tuple (topic, partition, offset). Committing an offset of 7 for partition 0 means "I have processed records 0 through 6; give me 7 next." This tiny integer is the whole basis of Kafka's replay and fault-recovery machinery.

2.3 Segments — how a partition actually lives on disk

A partition would be an unbounded file, which is impractical to manage or delete from. So Kafka breaks each partition into segments: fixed-size or time-bounded chunks (e.g. 1 GB or 1 week each). Only the newest segment (the "active segment") is being written; older ones are sealed and read-only. Alongside each segment's .log file sit index files (.index mapping offset→byte-position, .timeindex mapping timestamp→offset) so a consumer asking for "offset 5,000,003" can binary-search to the right segment and then jump to the exact byte without scanning. Deleting old data is now trivially cheap: drop whole sealed segment files. This segment structure is why retention is efficient.

2.4 Why sequential disk I/O is fast (the surprising performance secret)

Beginners assume Kafka is fast because it keeps everything in RAM. It does not — Kafka writes every record to disk, and yet handles millions of messages per second. The trick is that Kafka only ever does sequential reads and writes, never random ones. On a spinning disk, random access requires the head to physically seek (milliseconds each); sequential access streams contiguously and can hit hundreds of MB/s — often faster than random access to RAM once you account for how CPUs and disks are optimized for streaming. Because a partition is append-only, every write is "add to the end of a file" — the most sequential pattern possible. Two more mechanisms compound this:

  • The OS page cache. Kafka does not maintain its own in-process cache. It writes to the OS page cache, which the OS flushes to disk in the background. Reads by consumers who are "caught up" (reading recent data) are served straight from page cache — RAM speed — without Kafka managing anything. This also means a Kafka broker restart doesn't cold-start its cache: the page cache survives in the OS.
  • Zero-copy (sendfile). When sending a record to a consumer, Kafka asks the OS to copy bytes directly from page cache to the network socket, bypassing the application's memory entirely. No serialize/deserialize, no user-space copy. Data flows disk→NIC with the CPU barely involved.
Worked example — retention: A topic page-views is configured retention.ms=604800000 (7 days). At 09:00 today a record with offset 0 was written a day ago. Seven days from that write, the segment containing it becomes eligible for deletion. Kafka's log-cleaner thread notices the whole segment is past retention and deletes the segment files. Note: retention is about age of data, not whether it was consumed. If a consumer is 8 days behind, it will find the oldest records already gone and get an OffsetOutOfRange — a real operational hazard called falling off the log.

2.5 Retention vs compaction — two ways to bound a log

Kafka offers two cleanup policies. The default is time/size retention (delete): keep records for N days or until the partition exceeds N bytes, then drop old segments — appropriate for event streams where old events lose value. The alternative is log compaction: instead of deleting by age, Kafka guarantees to retain at least the latest value for each key, deleting older records that have the same key. This turns a topic into a "changelog" — a compacted account-balances topic keyed by account will forever hold the most recent balance per account, however old. Deleting a key entirely requires a special record: a tombstone, a message with the key and a null value. Compaction sees the tombstone, removes all prior records for that key, keeps the tombstone briefly (so consumers learn of the deletion), then removes the tombstone too. Compaction is how Kafka can be the source of truth for "current state" (used internally for consumer-offset storage and by Kafka Streams state stores).

Misconception: "More partitions is always better." Partitions cost real resources: each is open file handles, memory, and a unit of replication and rebalancing. Thousands of partitions per broker slow leader elections and recovery, and you can never reduce a topic's partition count (only increase), and increasing it breaks key→partition stability (Chapter 3). Choose deliberately (Chapter 8).
Key takeaways:
  • A topic is split into partitions; a partition is the append-only log. Parallelism and per-partition ordering both come from this split.
  • Offset = position within a partition, assigned at append, never a global ID; (topic, partition, offset) is a consumer's entire position.
  • Partitions are stored as segments with offset/time indexes, making replay seeks and old-data deletion cheap.
  • Kafka is fast via sequential-only I/O, the OS page cache, and zero-copy sends — not by holding data in application RAM.
  • Retention deletes by age/size; compaction keeps the latest value per key (tombstones delete a key) turning a topic into a changelog.

3. Producers Deeply — Partitioning, Batching, Acks, and Idempotence

A producer is any client that writes records to Kafka. It looks trivial — "send a message" — but the producer is where you make the decisions that determine ordering, throughput, and durability. Understanding the producer deeply is understanding half of Kafka's correctness guarantees.

3.1 The partitioner — where does a record go?

Every record can carry a key (optional) and a value. When you send, the producer must pick which partition to append to. The rule:

  • Key present: partition = hash(key) mod numPartitions (Kafka uses murmur2 hashing). This is deterministic: the same key always maps to the same partition. Since order is guaranteed within a partition, all records for a given key are ordered. Keying by account_id guarantees that one account's deposit-then-withdraw are processed in order, while different accounts spread across partitions for parallelism. This is the single most important producer decision.
  • No key: the record is distributed across partitions for load-balancing. Modern Kafka uses "sticky" partitioning — it fills a batch for one partition, then moves to another — to improve batching efficiency. There is no per-key ordering because there is no key.
Pitfall: The mapping is mod numPartitions. If you increase partition count later, existing keys re-hash to different partitions — a key's history is now split across old and new partitions and per-key ordering is broken going forward. This is why you pick partition count carefully up front for keyed topics.

3.2 Batching and linger — throughput vs latency

Sending one network request per record would be catastrophically slow. The producer batches: it accumulates records destined for the same partition into a buffer (up to batch.size bytes) and sends them as one request. linger.ms tells the producer to wait up to that many milliseconds for more records to join the batch before sending, even if the batch isn't full. linger.ms=0 (default) means "send as soon as possible" — lowest latency, smallest batches. Setting linger.ms=10 trades 10ms of latency for far larger batches and much higher throughput (fewer requests, better compression). Compression (lz4, zstd, snappy) is applied per batch, so bigger batches compress better. This is the classic latency/throughput dial.

3.3 Acks — the durability dial (0 / 1 / all)

When the producer sends a batch, how long does it wait for confirmation? The acks setting decides, and it directly controls how much data you can lose:

acksProducer waits forDurabilityFailure that loses data
0Nothing (fire-and-forget)LowestAny network drop or broker crash silently loses records.
1Leader wrote to its logMediumLeader acks, then crashes before a follower copied the record → record lost.
all (-1)Leader + all in-sync replicas wrote itHighestOnly if all in-sync replicas fail simultaneously.
Worked durability timeline (acks=1 data loss): 
t0  Producer sends record R to leader (broker A).
t1  Broker A appends R to its log, replies "ack" to producer.
t2  Producer considers R durable, moves on, forgets R.
t3  Broker A crashes BEFORE follower (broker B) fetched R.
t4  B is elected new leader. B never had R. R is gone forever,
    and the producer has no idea. --> silent data loss.
With acks=all, the ack at t1 would not have been sent until B (and any other in-sync replica) also had R, so B-as-new-leader would still contain R.

3.4 The idempotent producer — mechanics of "exactly one copy"

Even with acks=all, a subtle bug lurks: duplicates from retries. Suppose the leader writes R and then the ack packet is lost on the way back. The producer times out and retries, sending R again — now the log has R twice. Retries are essential for durability but naively cause duplicates. Kafka solves this with the idempotent producer (enable.idempotence=true, default in modern Kafka). The mechanics:

  • On startup the producer is assigned a Producer ID (PID) by the broker.
  • For each partition, the producer attaches a monotonically increasing sequence number to every record (0, 1, 2, ...).
  • The broker remembers the last sequence number it accepted per (PID, partition). When a record arrives, if its sequence number is exactly "last + 1" it is appended; if it is a duplicate of one already written (same PID + sequence), the broker silently discards it and returns success; if there's a gap (out-of-order), it errors.

So the retried R carries the same sequence number as the first R; the broker recognizes it, ignores the duplicate, and the log holds exactly one R. This gives exactly-once semantics for the producer→broker hop, per partition, per producer session — for free, at no throughput cost.

3.5 Retries, ordering, and max.in.flight

Retries interact dangerously with ordering. If the producer has multiple requests in flight to the same partition (max.in.flight.requests.per.connection > 1) and request 1 fails and is retried while request 2 already succeeded, records can land out of order. Historically the safe rule was max.in.flight=1 when order matters — but that kills throughput. The idempotent producer fixes this too: because the broker tracks sequence numbers, it will reject any batch that would create a gap, forcing the producer to re-send in order. With idempotence enabled, Kafka safely allows max.in.flight up to 5 while preserving order. This is why enabling idempotence is nearly always correct: order-safe, duplicate-free, and still fast.

Key takeaways:
  • Keyed records go to hash(key) mod partitions, giving per-key ordering; increasing partition count breaks that mapping.
  • Batching + linger.ms trades latency for throughput and better compression; keyless records "stick" to a partition to batch well.
  • acks=0/1/all is the durability dial; acks=1 can silently lose data if the leader dies pre-replication; acks=all waits for in-sync replicas.
  • The idempotent producer (PID + per-partition sequence numbers) makes producer→broker exactly-once by letting the broker discard retried duplicates.
  • Idempotence also preserves order with max.in.flight up to 5 — enable it; it's correctness for free.

4. Brokers & Replication — Leaders, ISR, and Durability Math

A broker is a single Kafka server. A cluster is a set of brokers. Data survives broker failure because Kafka replicates each partition across multiple brokers. This chapter is the machinery of "how does Kafka not lose your data when a machine dies," which is the heart of its reliability story.

4.1 Leaders and followers

Each partition has a configurable replication factor (commonly 3). Of those replicas, exactly one is the leader and the rest are followers. All reads and writes for a partition go to the leader — followers do not serve clients; their only job is to replicate, continuously fetching new records from the leader to stay identical. If the leader broker dies, a follower is promoted to leader, and clients transparently redirect. Because leadership is per-partition, leaders for different partitions are spread across brokers so that no single broker carries all the write load.

4.2 The In-Sync Replica set (ISR) — the crucial concept

A follower is in-sync if it has fully caught up to the leader within a time bound (replica.lag.time.max.ms). The set of the leader plus all currently-caught-up followers is the ISR (In-Sync Replicas). The ISR is dynamic: a follower that falls behind (slow disk, network hiccup) is removed from the ISR; when it catches up it rejoins. The ISR matters because two guarantees hinge on it: which followers are eligible to become leader without data loss, and (with acks=all) which replicas must acknowledge a write before it is considered committed.

4.3 The high-watermark — what consumers may see

The high-watermark (HW) is the highest offset that has been replicated to all members of the ISR. It is the boundary of "committed" data. Crucially, consumers can only read up to the high-watermark — never the leader's very latest un-replicated records. Why? So that a consumer never reads a record that could vanish. If a consumer could read offset 100 that only the leader had, and the leader then crashed, a new leader (which lacked offset 100) would present a shorter log — the consumer would have "seen the future" that then got un-happened. By hiding un-replicated records, Kafka ensures every record a consumer sees is durable and will survive leader failover.

Worked durability scenario (acks=all + min.insync.replicas): A topic has replication factor 3 and min.insync.replicas=2, producers use acks=all. This pairing is the standard "no data loss" config, and here's the precise contract: 
Replicas: Leader(A) + Followers(B, C).  ISR = {A, B, C}.

Normal:   Producer sends R with acks=all. Broker waits until R is in
          ALL in-sync replicas' logs (min 2 required). Once A and B have R,
          ISR size (3) >= min.insync (2) -> commit, ack producer, HW advances.

Failure:  C falls behind, drops out. ISR = {A, B}. Still >= 2 -> writes OK.
          Now B also fails. ISR = {A}. Size 1 < min.insync 2.
          -> Broker REFUSES the write (NotEnoughReplicas error).
             Producer gets an error and can retry/alert. NO record is
             acked that isn't safely on >=2 machines. Availability is
             sacrificed to guarantee zero data loss.
The lesson: acks=all alone is not enough — without min.insync.replicas>=2, a topic could shrink to ISR={leader} and "acks=all" would ack after just the leader wrote, reintroducing the acks=1 data-loss window. The two settings together give the guarantee.

4.4 Unclean leader election — the availability/durability trade-off

Suppose every in-sync replica for a partition dies, and only an out-of-sync follower (missing recent records) remains alive. Kafka faces a dilemma. Unclean leader election disabled (default, safe): the partition goes offline — no reads, no writes — until an in-sync replica returns. You keep every committed record but lose availability. Unclean leader election enabled: the stale follower becomes leader, restoring availability immediately — but committed records it never received are permanently lost, and its log now diverges from history. This is a fundamental CAP-style choice: consistency (no lost data) vs availability (stay up). Financial systems keep it off; some high-availability telemetry systems accept the loss to stay online.

4.5 KRaft vs ZooKeeper — cluster coordination

A cluster needs a "control plane" to track which brokers exist, who leads each partition, and the ISR. Historically Kafka delegated this to ZooKeeper, a separate distributed coordination service — meaning you ran and operated two systems, and metadata operations bottlenecked on ZooKeeper, capping how many partitions a cluster could hold. Modern Kafka replaced this with KRaft (Kafka Raft): the metadata is itself stored in an internal Kafka log managed by a Raft consensus quorum of "controller" brokers. No ZooKeeper. This simplifies operations (one system), speeds failover and metadata changes, and scales to millions of partitions. As of recent Kafka versions, KRaft is the default and ZooKeeper is being removed. Conceptually: the metadata about the cluster is now managed with the exact same log abstraction the cluster is built on — Kafka eats its own dog food.

Key takeaways:
  • Each partition has a leader (serves all I/O) and followers (replicate only); leadership spreads across brokers to balance load.
  • The ISR is the set of caught-up replicas; it's dynamic and determines both failover safety and acks=all commit requirements.
  • The high-watermark = highest offset in all ISR members; consumers read only up to it so they never see records that could disappear.
  • acks=all + min.insync.replicas>=2 together guarantee no data loss by refusing writes when too few replicas are in sync.
  • Unclean leader election trades durability for availability; KRaft replaces ZooKeeper, unifying metadata into Kafka's own log for scale and simplicity.

5. Consumers Deeply — Groups, Rebalancing, Offsets, and Lag

A consumer reads records from partitions. But Kafka's real power on the read side comes from consumer groups, which turn a set of consumers into an automatically load-balanced, fault-tolerant reading cluster. This chapter explains the pull model, group assignment, the notorious rebalance, and offset semantics that decide whether you lose or duplicate data.

5.1 The pull model — and why Kafka chose it

Kafka consumers pull (they repeatedly ask "give me records after offset X") rather than the broker pushing records to them. This is deliberate. Pull lets each consumer read at its own pace — a slow consumer simply asks less often; the broker never overwhelms it (no need for complex flow-control/backpressure protocols). Pull lets a consumer batch efficiently (fetch many records per request) and rewind (ask for an old offset) trivially. The cost is a little polling overhead, mitigated by long-polling (the fetch waits up to fetch.max.wait.ms for data rather than returning empty). Pull is why one slow consumer can't cause the memory blow-ups that push systems suffer.

5.2 Consumer groups and partition assignment

A consumer group is a set of consumers sharing a group.id that cooperate to consume a topic. The rule: each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. So a topic with 6 partitions and a group of 3 consumers gives each consumer 2 partitions; scale to 6 consumers and each gets 1; a 7th consumer sits idle because there's no partition to give it. This yields horizontal scaling of consumption up to the partition count — partition count is the ceiling on parallelism for one group. Meanwhile, different groups are independent: an analytics group and a fraud group both read every record, each with their own offsets. Same data, many independent readers — exactly the log's promise from Chapter 1.

Group assignment (topic with 4 partitions): 
Group "billing" (2 consumers)        Group "analytics" (1 consumer)
  C1 <- P0, P1                          A1 <- P0, P1, P2, P3
  C2 <- P2, P3
       (each partition -> one consumer within a group)
Both groups read ALL data independently, tracking separate offsets.

5.3 Rebalancing — and why "storms" happen

When group membership changes — a consumer joins, leaves, or crashes (detected by missed heartbeats) — Kafka must reassign partitions. This is a rebalance. The classic protocol is eager rebalancing ("stop-the-world"): every consumer gives up all its partitions, then the group re-divides them from scratch. During this pause, no one consumes — a latency spike for the whole group. If consumers are flapping (e.g. processing takes longer than max.poll.interval.ms, so a consumer is wrongly declared dead, triggering a rebalance, which slows everyone, causing more timeouts), you get a rebalance storm: continuous rebalancing where the group makes little progress. The fix is cooperative (incremental) rebalancing: only the partitions that actually need to move are revoked; consumers keep processing their unaffected partitions throughout. Cooperative rebalancing is now the default and dramatically reduces storm severity.

Pitfall: A common storm cause is slow processing. If your per-record work occasionally takes longer than max.poll.interval.ms (default 5 min), the group coordinator thinks the consumer died and rebalances — but the consumer was merely busy. Fix by making processing faster/async, increasing the interval, or reducing max.poll.records so each poll's batch is finishable in time.

5.4 Offset commits — the source of at-least-once vs at-most-once

To survive restarts, a consumer must remember where it was — it commits offsets (stored in an internal compacted topic __consumer_offsets). When it recovers, it resumes from the last committed offset. When you commit relative to when you process is the entire game:

  • At-least-once — process the record, then commit. If the consumer crashes after processing but before committing, on restart it re-reads and re-processes those records. No data lost, but possible duplicates. This is the common, safe default.
  • At-most-once — commit first, then process. If it crashes after committing but before processing, those records are skipped on restart. No duplicates, but possible data loss. Rarely acceptable.
  • Auto-commit (enable.auto.commit=true) periodically commits in the background on a timer — convenient but dangerous: it may commit offsets for records you fetched but haven't finished processing, silently losing them on a crash. Serious pipelines use manual commit after successful processing.
Worked example — at-least-once duplicate: Consumer reads offsets 10–14, writes them to a database, then crashes just before committing offset 15. On restart, last committed offset was 10, so it re-reads 10–14 and writes them to the database again. Rows 10–14 are now duplicated unless the database write is idempotent (e.g. an upsert keyed by a unique id). This is why "at-least-once + idempotent sink" is the workhorse pattern for correctness without the cost of full transactions (Chapter 6).

5.5 Consumer lag — the single most important health metric

Lag = (latest offset produced to a partition) − (consumer's committed offset). It is the number of records the consumer is behind. Lag near zero means the consumer keeps up with real time. Lag that grows means the consumer is producing-bound — it cannot process as fast as records arrive — and will fall further behind until it risks falling off the log's retention edge (Chapter 2). Lag is the number one operational signal for stream health: steady lag is fine, monotonically rising lag is an incident. You reduce lag by adding consumers (up to partition count), speeding processing, or increasing partitions to allow more parallelism.

Key takeaways:
  • Consumers pull (not pushed) so each reads at its own pace, batches, and rewinds; one slow consumer can't overwhelm the broker.
  • In a group, each partition goes to exactly one consumer, so partition count caps a group's parallelism; separate groups read all data independently.
  • Rebalances reassign partitions on membership change; eager "stop-the-world" causes storms — cooperative (incremental) rebalancing revokes only what must move.
  • Commit-after-process = at-least-once (duplicates possible); commit-before-process = at-most-once (loss possible); auto-commit is risky — prefer manual commit.
  • Lag = produced offset − committed offset; rising lag is the key incident signal for a struggling consumer.

6. Delivery Semantics & Transactions — Exactly-Once, Precisely

"Exactly-once" is the most misunderstood phrase in streaming. This chapter states the problem precisely, then explains the three mechanisms — idempotent producer, transactions, and idempotent sinks — that together deliver end-to-end exactly-once, and the outbox pattern that solves the boundary between your database and Kafka.

6.1 The three delivery semantics, stated precisely

At-most-once: every record is delivered zero or one times (never duplicated, but may be lost). At-least-once: every record is delivered one or more times (never lost, but may be duplicated). Exactly-once: every record's effect is applied exactly one time — no loss, no duplicates. The subtlety: in a distributed system with failures and retries you cannot avoid sending a message twice sometimes; the network won't let you know if the first send succeeded. So "exactly-once" never means "sent exactly once" — it means the observable effect happens once, achieved by making the pipeline recognize and discard the duplicates it inevitably produces.

6.2 Why at-least-once alone isn't exactly-once, and where duplicates come from

Two duplicate sources exist: on the write side (producer retries — solved by the idempotent producer, Chapter 3) and on the process side (a consumer re-reads after crashing before commit — Chapter 5). A typical stream-processing job does both: it reads from a topic, computes, and writes to another topic (and commits its input offset). A crash mid-way can cause the output to be written but the input offset not committed → on restart, re-read, re-compute, re-write → duplicate output and the offset advances inconsistently. To be exactly-once, the output write and the input-offset commit must be atomic — all or nothing. That requires transactions.

6.3 Transactional producer + read_committed

Kafka's transactions let a producer write to multiple partitions and commit consumer offsets as one atomic unit. Mechanics: the producer is given a stable transactional.id; it calls beginTransaction(), produces records, calls sendOffsetsToTransaction() to include the input offsets, then commitTransaction() (or abortTransaction()). Under the hood a transaction coordinator writes markers into the logs; the records physically exist in the partitions but are flagged as part of an open transaction. Consumers set isolation.level=read_committed, which means they only see records from committed transactions — aborted or in-flight records are invisible. So if the job crashes mid-transaction, the transaction is aborted, its half-written outputs are never revealed to downstream consumers, and the input offsets were never committed — the job cleanly retries. This is exactly-once within Kafka (read-process-write to Kafka).

Worked example — read-process-write EOS: A job reads orders, enriches each order, writes to enriched-orders. 
begin txn
  read order at offset 500
  produce enriched-order to output partition
