  • Account keys: two long master keys granting full control of everything in the account. Powerful, un-scoped, un-auditable per-user, and catastrophic if leaked. Avoid; if used, store in Key Vault and rotate.
  • Shared Access Signature (SAS): a signed URL that grants limited access — specific containers/blobs, specific permissions (read/write/list), and an expiry time. Good for handing a third party temporary, scoped access without sharing a key. A "user delegation SAS" is signed with an Entra identity (better) rather than the account key.
  • Entra ID + RBAC (data-plane roles): the recommended default. Identities (users, managed identities) get data roles like "Storage Blob Data Reader"; access is centrally auditable, revocable, and secretless. This ties storage back to the identity model of Chapter 2.
Misconception: "A SAS token is safe because it expires." A SAS is a bearer token — anyone who obtains the URL has exactly its permissions until it expires, and you cannot revoke a single SAS without rotating the signing key (which invalidates all SAS signed by it). Keep SAS lifetimes short and prefer Entra RBAC, which you can revoke instantly per-identity.
Key takeaways:
  • Hierarchy: storage account → container → blob; classic Blob is a flat object store (slashes are just names).
  • ADLS Gen2 = Blob + Hierarchical Namespace → real directories, atomic renames (critical for Spark commits), directory ACLs.
  • Access tiers hot/cool/cold/archive trade storage cost against read cost + latency; lifecycle policies automate re-tiering for huge savings.
  • Redundancy LRS→ZRS→GRS→GZRS trades cost for surviving disk/zone/region failures; geo copies are asynchronous.
  • Access model: prefer Entra RBAC data roles → SAS (scoped, expiring, bearer) → account keys (avoid).

4. Azure Data Factory architecture

Azure Data Factory (ADF) is Azure's managed, serverless data-integration service — the orchestrator that moves and transforms data across your estate. To use it well you must first separate two ideas that beginners conflate: the control plane that decides what happens, and the data plane that actually touches your bytes.

4.1 Control plane vs data plane

ADF's control plane is the brain: it stores your pipeline definitions, schedules triggers, evaluates expressions, tracks run state, and issues commands. It never sees your data. The data plane is the muscle: the actual compute that reads from a source and writes to a sink. That compute is provided by an Integration Runtime (IR). This separation is why ADF can be "serverless" — you author logic in the control plane, and the IR spins up transient compute on demand to do the physical work, then disappears. It also has a security consequence: your data does not necessarily flow through Microsoft's control plane; with the right IR it can move directly source-to-sink over a private network path.

4.2 The object model — linked services, datasets, pipelines, activities

ADF is defined by four nested concepts. Learn them as a stack:

  • Linked service: a connection — the "how do I connect to system X" credentials and endpoint (a storage account, a SQL server, a REST API). Analogy: a database connection string. It answers where and how to authenticate.
  • Dataset: a named view of data reachable through a linked service — e.g. "the customers table on that SQL server" or "CSV files in that container". It answers what shape/location of data, layered on a linked service.
  • Activity: a single unit of work — copy this dataset to that one, run this stored procedure, execute this notebook, loop over items. The verb.
  • Pipeline: an ordered/branching group of activities that together accomplish a job, with dependencies between them (run B only if A succeeds). The workflow.
Pipeline
  ├─ Activity 1 (Copy)   reads Dataset A  ──uses──> Linked Service (SQL)
  │                      writes Dataset B ──uses──> Linked Service (ADLS)
  └─ Activity 2 (Notebook) depends on Activity 1 success
Linked Service = connection | Dataset = data shape | Activity = a task | Pipeline = the workflow

4.3 Integration Runtimes — where compute actually happens

The Integration Runtime (IR) is the most misunderstood and most important concept in ADF, because it determines where your data physically flows and what networks it can reach. There are three types:

  • Azure IR: fully managed, serverless compute inside Azure's network. Use it when both source and sink are cloud-reachable (public endpoints or Azure private networking). It auto-scales, you pick only a region, and Microsoft runs the machines. This is the default for cloud-to-cloud movement and for running mapping data flows.
  • Self-hosted IR (SHIR): an agent you install on a machine inside your own network (on-prem or in a private VNet). The SHIR makes an outbound connection to ADF's control plane and does the actual data movement locally. It is mandatory whenever a source or sink lives behind a firewall or in a private network that Azure's managed IR cannot reach — e.g. an on-premise SQL Server or a database on a locked-down VNet. Data flows through the SHIR, never exposing the private system to the public internet.
  • Azure-SSIS IR: a managed cluster that lifts-and-shifts legacy SQL Server Integration Services (SSIS) packages into Azure so existing on-prem ETL can run unchanged. A migration bridge, not a place to build new pipelines.

4.4 Network paths and when SHIR is required

The mental test for choosing an IR: "Can Azure's managed network reach both endpoints?" If yes → Azure IR. If either endpoint is private/on-prem and unreachable from the public managed IR → Self-hosted IR, installed where it can reach that endpoint. For high-security cloud setups, an Azure IR can be paired with a managed virtual network and private endpoints so that even cloud-to-cloud traffic never traverses the public internet (covered in Chapter 9).

Worked example: You must copy a table from an on-premise Oracle database into ADLS Gen2. The Azure IR lives in Microsoft's network and cannot see your on-prem Oracle (it is behind your corporate firewall with no public IP). So you install a Self-hosted IR on a Windows server inside your data center that can reach Oracle. The copy activity uses that SHIR: it reads Oracle locally and streams to ADLS over an outbound HTTPS connection. No inbound firewall port is opened, and Oracle is never exposed publicly. Had the source been an Azure SQL Database instead, a plain Azure IR would have sufficed.
Key takeaways:
  • ADF splits into a control plane (orchestration brain, never sees data) and a data plane (the IR, which moves the bytes).
  • Object model stack: Linked Service (connection) → Dataset (data shape) → Activity (a task) → Pipeline (the workflow).
  • Azure IR = managed cloud compute for cloud-reachable endpoints; also runs data flows.
  • Self-hosted IR is mandatory when a source/sink is on-prem or in a private network the managed IR cannot reach; it connects outbound.
  • SSIS IR exists only to lift-and-shift legacy SSIS packages.

5. ADF pipeline theory — activities, copy internals, parameterization

With the architecture in place, this chapter is about how pipelines actually do work: the taxonomy of activities, the internals of the workhorse Copy activity, the expression/parameter system that makes pipelines reusable, and the metadata-driven pattern that is the single most important design idea in ADF.

5.1 Activity taxonomy

Activities fall into three families:

  • Data movement: the Copy activity — moves data from a source to a sink, optionally with light transformation (column mapping, format conversion).
  • Data transformation: activities that hand heavy compute to an engine — Mapping Data Flow (Spark), Databricks Notebook, Stored Procedure, Synapse/HDInsight/Azure Function, dbt. ADF orchestrates; the engine transforms.
  • Control flow: activities that shape the workflow itself — ForEach (loop), If Condition, Switch, Until, Wait, Set/Append Variable, Lookup (read a small result to drive logic), Get Metadata, and Execute Pipeline (call another pipeline).

5.2 Copy activity internals — DIUs, parallelism, staging

The Copy activity looks trivial but has real internals that govern its speed and cost:

  • Data Integration Units (DIUs): a DIU is an abstract bundle of CPU, memory, and network allocated to a copy. More DIUs → more throughput, up to the point where the source or sink becomes the bottleneck. You pay per DIU-hour, so throughput has a direct price. ADF can auto-scale DIUs (typically 2–256).
  • Parallel copy: within one activity, ADF can split the work into concurrent streams — e.g. copying many files at once, or reading a table in partitioned ranges. Parallelism multiplies throughput but can overwhelm a fragile source, so it is tunable.
  • Staged copy: for some source→sink pairs (e.g. loading into Synapse, or hopping between regions), ADF first writes to an interim blob "staging" area, then bulk-loads from there. This uses the destination's fastest bulk-load path (e.g. Synapse PolyBase/COPY) and can dramatically speed large loads at the cost of a temporary storage hop.

5.3 Expressions, parameters, and system variables

Hard-coding values (a file path, a date, a table name) makes a pipeline single-use. ADF's expression language (functions like @concat(), @formatDateTime(), @pipeline().parameters.x) lets you compute values at runtime. Parameters exist at three layers, each passed in from the layer above:

  • Linked-service parameters: make a connection reusable — e.g. one SQL linked service parameterized by server/database name.
  • Dataset parameters: make a data reference reusable — e.g. one dataset whose folder path and file name are parameters.
  • Pipeline parameters: inputs to a whole pipeline run, set by the trigger or the caller.
  • System variables: values ADF supplies automatically — @pipeline().RunId, @pipeline().TriggerTime, @trigger().outputs.windowStartTime — invaluable for logging and incremental logic.

5.4 Metadata-driven design — the config-table + ForEach pattern

This is the pattern that separates hobby pipelines from enterprise ones. Instead of building one pipeline per table (100 tables → 100 pipelines, a maintenance nightmare), you build one generic, fully-parameterized pipeline and drive it from a control/config table that lists what to process. The pipeline: (1) Lookup reads the config table, (2) ForEach iterates over the returned rows, (3) inside the loop, a parameterized Copy (and/or transform) runs using each row's values (source table, target path, load type, watermark column). Adding a new table means inserting a row, not editing pipelines.

Worked example: A config table etl_control has columns source_table, target_container, target_path, load_type, watermark_col with one row per source table. Pipeline steps:
1. Lookup → SELECT * FROM etl_control WHERE enabled = 1 returns, say, 80 rows.
2. ForEach over those 80 rows (with a batch/concurrency setting).
3. Inside: a parameterized Copy where source dataset table = @item().source_table and sink path = @concat(item().target_container, '/', item().target_path).
Result: 80 tables ingested by ONE pipeline definition. Onboarding table 81 is a single INSERT. This is the core of scalable ADF.
Misconception: "ForEach always runs items in parallel, so order/consistency is fine." ForEach defaults to parallel execution (batch count up to 50). If your items have dependencies or share a resource, uncontrolled parallelism causes race conditions and throttled sources. Set isSequential or a sensible batch count deliberately.
Key takeaways:
  • Activities are movement (Copy), transformation (hand off to Spark/Databricks/SQL), or control flow (ForEach/If/Lookup/etc.).
  • Copy speed/cost is governed by DIUs (paid per DIU-hour), parallel copy, and optional staging for fast bulk loads.
  • The expression language + parameters at linked-service/dataset/pipeline layers + system variables make pipelines reusable and dynamic.
  • Metadata-driven design (config table → Lookup → ForEach → parameterized Copy) replaces N pipelines with one; new sources are just rows.

6. Triggers and incremental load patterns

Pipelines must run on some cadence and, for large tables, must process only new data rather than reloading everything. This chapter covers ADF's trigger types (with special attention to the stateful tumbling-window trigger) and the watermark pattern that is the backbone of incremental ingestion.

6.1 Trigger types

  • Schedule trigger: fires on a wall-clock schedule (every hour, daily at 02:00). Simple and stateless — it does not know or care whether the previous run succeeded, and firings do not "queue up" if a run is slow.
  • Tumbling-window trigger: a stateful, non-overlapping series of fixed time windows (e.g. hourly windows: 00:00–01:00, 01:00–02:00, ...). Each window is a first-class run with its own windowStartTime/windowEndTime. This is the powerhouse for incremental loads because it adds three things a schedule trigger lacks — see §6.2.
  • Event-based trigger: fires in response to a storage event — e.g. a blob being created or deleted in a container (via Azure Event Grid). Ideal for "process the file the moment it lands" ingestion.
  • Manual/on-demand: triggered by a person or an external system via API — used for ad-hoc runs and integration with other orchestrators.

6.2 Why tumbling window is special — state, retries, dependencies

The tumbling-window trigger encodes three concepts critical to reliable pipelines:

  • State: each window "knows" its exact time slice via windowStartTime/windowEndTime. You use these to filter the source ("load rows where modified between window start and end") — deterministic, replayable slices.
  • Retries: a failed window can auto-retry with a configured count and interval, and — because windows are stateful — you can rerun a specific past window (backfill a single bad hour) without re-running everything.
  • Dependencies: a tumbling-window pipeline can depend on its own previous window (enforcing in-order processing) or on another pipeline's window (fan-in). This lets you build ordered, gap-free processing chains. It also supports controlled concurrency so you can safely backfill many windows at once.

6.3 The watermark pattern, step by step

A watermark is a stored marker of "how far we have already loaded" — typically the maximum value of a monotonically increasing column (a LastModified timestamp or an ever-increasing ID). Incremental load = "give me only rows past the watermark, then advance the watermark." Mechanically:

  1. Read old watermark: Lookup the last saved high-water value from a control table (e.g. SELECT last_wm FROM watermarks WHERE table='orders').
  2. Read new high-water: Lookup the current max from the source (SELECT MAX(modified_at) AS new_wm FROM orders).
  3. Copy the delta: Copy activity with a query SELECT * FROM orders WHERE modified_at > @{old_wm} AND modified_at <= @{new_wm} — only the new/changed rows.
  4. Advance the watermark: on success, Stored-Procedure activity updates watermarks.last_wm = new_wm. Crucially this happens after the copy succeeds, so a failure leaves the old watermark intact and the next run retries the same delta (idempotent).
Worked example: Old watermark = 2026-07-04 00:00:00. Step 2 finds new max = 2026-07-05 00:00:00. Step 3 copies exactly the ~one day of rows whose modified_at falls in that half-open interval. Step 4 advances the watermark to 2026-07-05. If step 3 had failed, the watermark would still read 2026-07-04 and tomorrow's run would safely re-pull the same window — no data lost, no duplicates from a partial advance. Note the half-open bound (> old, ≤ new) prevents both gaps and double-counting the boundary row.

6.4 CDC options in Azure and handling late/failed windows

Watermarking on a timestamp captures inserts and updates but not deletes, and it needs a reliable increasing column. For true Change Data Capture (CDC) — every insert/update/delete in order — Azure offers: database-native CDC (SQL Server / Azure SQL CDC tables read by ADF), ADF's built-in native CDC resource for supported sources, the Change Feed on ADLS/Cosmos DB, and log-based tools like Debezium/Kafka (covered in the streaming section). Late-arriving data (a row whose timestamp is older than the last watermark because it was inserted late) is the classic watermark failure mode; mitigations are re-processing a trailing window (re-scan the last N hours each run) or switching to log-based CDC. Failed windows in a tumbling-window trigger are handled by retries and targeted reruns as in §6.2.

Misconception: "A watermark on updated_at gives me a perfect copy of the source." It misses hard deletes (a row removed from the source never shows up as a change) and misses late inserts stamped with an old time. If you need delete-accurate, order-accurate replication, use CDC, not watermarking.
Key takeaways:
  • Triggers: schedule (stateless clock), tumbling window (stateful time slices), event-based (on blob create/delete), manual/API.
  • Tumbling window adds state, retries, and window dependencies — enabling reliable, replayable, backfillable incremental loads.
  • Watermark pattern: read old WM → read new max → copy the half-open delta → advance WM only after success (idempotent).
  • Watermarking misses deletes and late arrivals; use CDC (DB-native, native CDC, change feed, log-based) when those matter.

7. Data flows and transformation choices

Once data is landed, you must transform it. Azure gives several engines to do this, and choosing correctly is a matter of team skill, complexity, and cost. This chapter covers ADF Mapping Data Flows and the decision theory of Data Flow vs Databricks vs SQL, framed by the ELT pattern.

7.1 Mapping Data Flows — Spark under the hood

A Mapping Data Flow is a visual, drag-and-drop transformation designer inside ADF (joins, aggregates, derived columns, pivots, etc.). The essential theory: it is not doing the work itself — it compiles your visual graph into Apache Spark code and runs it on a managed Spark cluster (an Azure IR). That is why data flows scale to big data and why they have a cluster spin-up cost. Two practical consequences: (1) there is a cluster startup latency (a few minutes) each time the flow runs unless you use a warm/reserved cluster; (2) while authoring, you enable a debug cluster — a live Spark cluster kept running so you can preview data at each step, which is billed for its uptime and should be turned off when idle.

7.2 The transformation decision — Data Flow vs Databricks vs SQL

ChooseWhenBecause
Mapping Data FlowTeam prefers no-code/low-code; moderate complexity; ADF-native orchestrationVisual, Spark-powered, no Spark coding needed; but less flexible and can cost more per job than raw Spark
Databricks / Spark notebooksComplex logic, heavy scale, ML, reusable code, engineers who write Python/Scala/SQLFull programmatic power, best price/performance at scale, version-controlled code, Delta Lake features
SQL (in-database / Synapse)Data is already in a warehouse; set-based transforms; team is SQL-strongPush compute to the engine that holds the data (no movement); simplest for relational, set-based work

The governing principle is push the transformation to where the data already lives and to the tool the team can maintain. Moving data to transform it, when you could transform it in place, wastes time and money. And the "best" engine that no one on the team can debug is a liability.

7.3 ELT in Azure

Classic ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) transforms data before loading it into the destination, using a separate transform engine. Modern cloud favours ELT (Extract, Load, Transform): land the raw data cheaply into the lake/warehouse first, then transform it using the destination's own scalable compute. This works in the cloud precisely because storage is cheap (land everything) and compute is elastic and co-located (transform at scale on demand). In Azure the canonical ELT flow is: ADF/Copy lands raw files in ADLS (bronze) → Databricks or Synapse or Data Flows refines them into cleaned (silver) and business-ready (gold) layers — the medallion architecture covered in the PySpark section.

Worked example: Requirement: join 500M order rows with a customer dimension and aggregate to daily revenue by region. Option A (ETL) — pull both tables out to a transform engine, join, load result: moves 500M rows over the network twice. Option B (ELT) — Copy lands raw orders as Parquet in ADLS; a Databricks job reads them with Spark, joins against the already-present customer Delta table, writes the daily aggregate to a gold table. No round-trip export; Spark parallelises the join; the raw layer is preserved for reprocessing. Option B is the modern default.
Key takeaways:
  • Mapping Data Flows are a visual front-end that compiles to Spark and runs on a managed cluster — hence startup latency and a debug-cluster billing gotcha.
  • Data Flow (low-code) vs Databricks (full Spark power) vs SQL (transform in the warehouse) is chosen by complexity, scale, and team skill.
  • Push transformation to where the data lives and to a tool the team can maintain.
  • ELT (land raw, then transform in place with elastic compute) beats ETL in the cloud because storage is cheap and compute is elastic and co-located → bronze/silver/gold medallion.

8. The wider Azure data estate

ADF and ADLS are the movement and storage core, but a real platform includes a warehouse, a streaming ingress, a secrets vault, and governance. This chapter surveys the surrounding services with enough internal theory to reason about each.

8.1 Azure Synapse Analytics — two engines in one

Synapse bundles two very different SQL engines, plus Spark, under one workspace:

  • Dedicated SQL pool (MPP warehouse): a Massively Parallel Processing data warehouse. It splits your data into 60 distributions (buckets) spread across compute nodes; a query runs in parallel across all distributions and results are combined. You buy a fixed amount of compute measured in DWUs (Data Warehouse Units) — more DWUs = more nodes/parallelism = faster and pricier, billed while the pool is running (you can pause it to stop compute charges). Because data is pre-distributed, how you distribute a table (hash on a join key, round-robin, or replicate small dimensions) determines whether a join needs expensive cross-node data shuffling — the same distribution-style theory as Redshift (Chapter 5 of the AWS section).
  • Serverless SQL pool: no cluster to provision; you run T-SQL directly over files in ADLS and pay per TB of data scanned. Perfect for ad-hoc exploration and lightweight transforms over the lake without loading anything. Same economics as AWS Athena.

8.2 Microsoft Fabric direction — OneLake and the lakehouse

Microsoft Fabric is Microsoft's unification of the analytics stack (Data Factory, Synapse, Power BI) into one SaaS product with a single storage foundation, OneLake — "one data lake for the whole organization", built on ADLS Gen2 and standardised on the open Delta/Parquet format. The strategic idea is one copy of the data, many engines: SQL, Spark, and Power BI all read the same Delta tables in OneLake (via a "lakehouse"), eliminating copies and the "which system has the truth" problem. Direction of travel: new greenfield Azure analytics increasingly starts in Fabric; Synapse/ADF remain widely deployed and fully supported.

8.3 Azure SQL and Event Hubs

Azure SQL Database is a fully-managed PaaS relational database (the SQL Server engine, with the OS/patching/backups handled by Azure) — the go-to for transactional (OLTP) application data and small operational stores, and a frequent source for pipelines.

Azure Event Hubs is Azure's big-data streaming ingress — a managed, high-throughput event ingestion service. Critically, it is protocol-compatible with Apache Kafka: applications that speak the Kafka protocol can produce/consume to Event Hubs unchanged. Its internals mirror Kafka (covered fully in the streaming section): a stream is split into partitions for parallel throughput and ordering within a partition, and capacity is bought in throughput units (TUs) — each TU allows a fixed ingress/egress rate (roughly 1 MB/s in, 2 MB/s out per TU). You scale by adding partitions (parallelism) and TUs (bandwidth).

8.4 Key Vault integration patterns

Azure Key Vault is the managed store for secrets, keys, and certificates. The integration pattern that ties Chapters 2–3 together: instead of putting a connection string or key in an ADF linked service, you store it in Key Vault and reference it. ADF (using its managed identity, granted "Key Vault Secrets User") fetches the secret at runtime. The secret never appears in the pipeline definition, the ARM template, or source control — it lives in one audited, access-controlled, rotatable place. This is the concrete mechanism behind "secrets in Key Vault as a last resort" from Chapter 2.

8.5 Microsoft Purview — governance concept

Microsoft Purview is the unified data-governance service. Conceptually it does three things: catalog (scan sources across the estate to build a searchable inventory of what data exists and its schema), classification (auto-detect sensitive data like credit-card or national-ID numbers and tag it), and lineage (map how data flows source→pipeline→destination so you can answer "where did this column come from and what breaks if I change it"). Governance is the answer to scale: once you have thousands of datasets, you cannot manage trust, compliance, and impact analysis by hand.

Worked example: A compliance auditor asks "which reports contain customer email addresses, and where do those emails originate?" Without governance this is a week of manual archaeology. With Purview: search the catalog for the "Email" classification → it lists every dataset containing emails; open the lineage graph → it traces each email column back through the ADF pipeline that produced it to the source Azure SQL table. The audit becomes minutes.
Key takeaways:
  • Synapse dedicated pool = MPP warehouse (60 distributions, DWUs, distribution-style-driven joins, pausable); serverless pool = pay-per-TB-scanned SQL over the lake.
  • Fabric unifies the stack on OneLake (Delta/Parquet, one copy, many engines) and is the strategic greenfield direction.
  • Azure SQL = managed OLTP relational DB; Event Hubs = Kafka-protocol-compatible streaming ingress (partitions for parallelism, throughput units for bandwidth).
  • Key Vault stores secrets/keys/certs; ADF's managed identity fetches them at runtime so nothing sensitive lives in pipelines or Git.
  • Purview provides catalog + classification + lineage — the governance layer that makes a large estate trustworthy and auditable.

9. Operations, cost, and security architecture

A pipeline that runs is not a pipeline that is production-ready. Operations (monitoring, rerun), cost control, and security architecture are what separate a demo from a system. This chapter closes the Azure section with the operational discipline a data engineer owns.

9.1 Monitoring — runs, alerts, Log Analytics

ADF exposes execution telemetry at two grains: pipeline runs (did the whole workflow succeed/fail, how long, with what parameters) and activity runs (per-step status, rows read/written, duration, error message). The built-in monitor shows the recent history, but retention there is short (about 45 days) and it is not queryable at scale. For real observability you route these logs (via diagnostic settings) into Azure Monitor / Log Analytics, where they are stored long-term and queryable with KQL (Kusto Query Language). On top of that you configure alerts — e.g. "notify on any pipeline failure" or "alert if a run exceeds its normal duration" — so failures page a human instead of being discovered by an angry report consumer.

9.2 Rerun semantics

When a pipeline fails partway, ADF lets you rerun either the whole pipeline or from the failed activity onward (re-using the successful earlier steps' state). This is why idempotent design matters: a rerun must not double-load data. Techniques: overwrite/merge instead of append, keyed upserts, and the watermark-after-success pattern from Chapter 6. Tumbling-window pipelines additionally support rerunning a specific historical window for surgical backfills.

9.3 The ADF cost model — a worked example

ADF billing has a few components, and the intuition to internalise is you pay per activity run and per unit of data-movement/transform compute, not for an always-on server. The main meters: (1) activity runs — a small per-run charge (orchestration), priced per 1,000 runs; (2) data movement — DIU-hours consumed by Copy activities; (3) data-flow execution — vCore-hours of the Spark cluster (plus its startup time); (4) small charges for pipeline "activity" duration and self-hosted IR hours.

Worked cost example: A daily metadata-driven pipeline ingests 80 tables. Per run it executes ~5 orchestration activities + 80 copies = ~85 activity runs. Over 30 days: 85 × 30 = 2,550 activity runs ≈ 2.55 × ($1 per 1,000) ≈ $2.55/month for orchestration — negligible. The real cost is data movement: suppose the copies together consume 4 DIUs for 20 minutes each run. That is 4 DIU × (1/3 hr) = 1.33 DIU-hours/run × 30 ≈ 40 DIU-hours/month × ~$0.25 ≈ $10/month. Add a nightly data flow on an 8-vCore cluster running 15 min/day: 8 vCore × 0.25 hr × 30 = 60 vCore-hours × ~$0.27 ≈ $16/month, plus ~5 min cluster startup billed each run. Total ≈ ~$30/month — and the biggest lever is not the tiny activity-run fee but the DIU-hours and data-flow vCore-hours, i.e. how much compute your movement/transform actually burns. Turning off idle debug clusters is the other big saver.

9.4 Common failure modes

  • Throttling: a source (a REST API, a SQL DB) rejects requests when parallelism is too high → tune concurrency/DIUs and add retries with backoff.
  • Schema drift: a source adds/renames a column and a strictly-mapped copy or data flow breaks → use schema-drift handling or late-binding mappings.
  • Timeouts & transient network errors: handled by activity retry policies; make the work idempotent so retries are safe.
  • Credential/permission errors: the classic management-plane-vs-data-plane RBAC mistake from Chapter 2, or an expired secret (mitigated by managed identity).

9.5 Security architecture — private endpoints and network isolation

By default, PaaS resources (storage, SQL, ADF) have public endpoints — reachable over the internet, protected by auth. High-security enterprises go further and remove the public path entirely using private endpoints: a private endpoint gives the resource a private IP address inside your virtual network (VNet), so traffic to it never leaves Azure's private backbone. Combined with an ADF managed virtual network IR (so ADF's own compute runs inside a managed VNet and talks to your data over private endpoints), you achieve network isolation: the data plane is fully private, public access is disabled, and firewall rules restrict who can reach what. This layers on top of identity (Entra/RBAC) — network isolation and identity are complementary defences, not substitutes.

Misconception: "Private endpoints replace RBAC." No — they are different layers. A private endpoint controls network reachability (can packets get to the resource at all); RBAC controls authorization (may this identity perform this action). Defence in depth uses both: even inside the private network, an identity still needs the right data role.
Key takeaways:
  • Monitor at pipeline-run and activity-run grain; route logs to Log Analytics (KQL, long retention) and configure failure/duration alerts.
  • Rerun-from-failed-activity and window reruns require idempotent design (upsert/overwrite, advance-watermark-after-success).
  • ADF cost = per-activity-run (tiny) + DIU-hours (Copy) + vCore-hours (data flows); the real spend is compute, not orchestration — kill idle debug clusters.
  • Common failures: throttling, schema drift, transient errors, and management-vs-data-plane permission mistakes.
  • Private endpoints give resources private VNet IPs for network isolation; they complement, not replace, Entra/RBAC identity control.

AWS for Data Engineering — Complete Theory

The AWS analytics stack from first principles — IAM, S3, Glue, Athena, Redshift, Kinesis, and the compute/CDC/ops layers — with a full Azure↔AWS map at the end.

1. The AWS resource model and IAM

AWS was the first major cloud and its model differs from Azure in organization and, most importantly, in identity. Where Azure centralises identity in one tenant, AWS grounds everything in the account and a powerful, policy-driven system called IAM. Master IAM and the rest of AWS falls into place.

1.1 Accounts and Organizations

The AWS account is the fundamental boundary — it is simultaneously the billing boundary, the primary security/isolation boundary, and the default limit of what resources can see each other. (Contrast Azure, where the subscription is billing and the tenant is identity; in AWS the account fuses more of these.) Enterprises rarely use one account; they use AWS Organizations to manage many accounts as a tree, with a management account at the root and Organizational Units (OUs) grouping member accounts (dev, prod, security). Service Control Policies (SCPs) applied to OUs set the maximum permissions any account under them can have — guardrails that even an account's own admins cannot exceed. The best-practice pattern is account-per-environment/team for strong blast-radius isolation.

1.2 IAM — the four building blocks

IAM (Identity and Access Management) decides who can do what. Four primitives:

  • Users: a permanent identity for a human or legacy app, with long-lived credentials (password, access keys). Modern practice minimises IAM users in favour of federated/temporary access.
  • Groups: a collection of users; attach a policy to the group and all members inherit it. Groups are for humans only (a role cannot be in a group).
  • Roles: the most important concept — an identity with no permanent credentials that can be assumed temporarily by a trusted principal (a service, a user, another account). Assuming a role yields short-lived temporary credentials. This is how services get permissions and how you avoid long-lived keys.
  • Policies: JSON documents listing permissions — Effect (Allow/Deny), Action (e.g. s3:GetObject), Resource (which ARNs), and optional Condition. Policies attach to users, groups, or roles.

1.3 Policy evaluation logic

When a request is made, IAM evaluates all applicable policies (identity policies, resource policies, SCPs, permission boundaries) and decides by these rules, in order of precedence: (1) an explicit Deny anywhere always wins — nothing can override it; (2) otherwise, access requires an explicit Allow; (3) with no matching Allow, the default is Deny (implicit deny). So the mental model is: "deny by default; you need an Allow and no Deny." This is why over-broad grants are dangerous and why a targeted Deny is a strong safety mechanism.

1.4 Assume-role and instance profiles

AssumeRole is the mechanism behind almost all secure AWS access. A principal calls STS (Security Token Service), which returns temporary credentials scoped to the role's policies and a short expiry (minutes to hours). Two vital applications: (a) cross-account access — account A's role trusts a principal in account B, letting B's users/services act in A without any shared long-lived key; (b) instance profiles — an EC2 instance (or a Glue job, Lambda, ECS task) is associated with a role, and the AWS SDK on it automatically obtains temporary credentials for that role. This is the AWS equivalent of Azure's managed identity: the compute proves its identity to AWS and gets short-lived creds — no access keys stored in code.

Worked example: A Glue ETL job must read from S3 and write to a database. Wrong way: embed an IAM user's access key + secret in the job script (a permanent leakable credential). Right way: create an IAM role with a policy allowing s3:GetObject on the source bucket and the DB actions, and attach the role to the Glue job. At runtime Glue assumes the role and receives temporary credentials automatically; the script contains no secrets. If the job is compromised, the attacker gets only that role's narrow, expiring permissions.
Misconception: "IAM roles are just users without a login." The defining difference is credential lifetime: users carry long-lived credentials; roles issue short-lived temporary ones on assumption. That expiry is the whole security value — a leaked temporary credential is useless in an hour. Prefer roles everywhere.

1.5 Regions and pricing variation

Like Azure, AWS is organized into regions (e.g. us-east-1, eu-west-1) each containing multiple Availability Zones. The same region theory applies: latency, data residency, service availability, and price vary by region — us-east-1 (N. Virginia) is typically the cheapest and gets new services first, while distant regions cost more. Most services are regional (their data stays in-region); a few (IAM, Route 53, CloudFront) are global. Choosing a region trades user latency, compliance, feature availability, and cost.

Key takeaways:
  • The account is AWS's fused billing + security boundary; Organizations/OUs/SCPs manage many accounts with top-down guardrails.
  • IAM primitives: users (long-lived), groups (bundle policies for humans), roles (assumable, temporary creds), policies (Allow/Deny JSON on Action+Resource+Condition).
  • Evaluation: explicit Deny wins → else need an explicit Allow → else implicit Deny. "Deny by default."
  • AssumeRole/STS gives short-lived creds; instance profiles = roles on compute = AWS's managed-identity equivalent (no stored keys).
  • Regions/AZs mirror Azure; price and feature availability vary by region (us-east-1 cheapest/earliest).

2. S3 theory — the data lake substrate

Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) is the foundation of essentially every AWS data platform — the object store where the data lake lives. Its semantics differ from a filesystem in ways that shape all downstream design, so we build it up carefully.

2.1 Object storage semantics — no filesystem

S3 stores objects (files + metadata) in buckets (globally-unique-named containers). The critical truth: S3 is a flat key-value store, not a filesystem. An object has a key — its full name, e.g. sales/2026/01/data.parquet. The slashes are part of the key string; there are no real directories. What tools display as "folders" are just key prefixes (common leading substrings). Consequences: there is no atomic "rename a folder" (renaming = copy every object to new keys + delete old ones), and listing a "folder" is really "list all keys starting with this prefix". This flatness is the same issue that motivated Azure's Hierarchical Namespace (§Azure 3.2).

2.2 Consistency model — strong now, eventual historically

A consistency model answers: after you write, will the next read see the new value? For years S3 offered only eventual consistency for overwrites/lists — a read right after a write might return stale data, which caused subtle data-pipeline bugs (a job listing files could miss just-written ones). Since December 2020, S3 provides strong read-after-write consistency for all operations: once a write (PUT/DELETE) succeeds, every subsequent read and list immediately reflects it. This history matters because older tutorials, tools, and "eventual-consistency workarounds" (like Netflix's S3mper or EMRFS consistent view) were built around the old behaviour and are now obsolete.

2.3 Storage classes and lifecycle economics

Like Azure tiers, S3 has storage classes trading storage price vs access price/latency/availability:

ClassStorage costRetrievalUse for
S3 StandardHighestInstantHot, frequently accessed
S3 Intelligent-TieringAuto-optimisedInstantUnknown/changing access patterns (auto-moves objects)
S3 Standard-IA / One Zone-IALowerInstant + per-GB feeInfrequent access (IA = Infrequent Access)
Glacier Instant / Flexible / Deep ArchiveLowestms → minutes → hoursArchive, compliance, rarely read

Lifecycle policies automate transitions and expiration by object age/prefix — identical concept to Azure lifecycle management, and just as high-leverage for cost. Intelligent-Tiering is unique: for a small monitoring fee it automatically moves each object between tiers based on its actual access, removing the need to guess.

2.4 Versioning, encryption, multipart upload

  • Versioning: when enabled on a bucket, every overwrite/delete keeps the prior version (delete just adds a "delete marker"). Protects against accidental deletion and overwrites; note that every version stores and bills separately, so pair with a lifecycle rule to expire old versions.
  • Encryption: S3 encrypts at rest by default. Modes: SSE-S3 (AWS manages the keys), SSE-KMS (keys in AWS Key Management Service — auditable, per-key access control, the common enterprise choice), and SSE-C/client-side (you manage keys). In transit, use HTTPS.
  • Multipart upload: large objects are split into parts uploaded in parallel and reassembled server-side. This gives higher throughput and resilience (a failed part re-uploads without restarting the whole file); it is required above 5 GB and recommended above ~100 MB. Abandoned incomplete multipart uploads silently cost money, so add a lifecycle rule to abort them.

2.5 Request costs, throughput, and prefix parallelism

S3 charges not only for storage but for requests (per 1,000 GET/PUT/LIST). This is why "many tiny files" is an anti-pattern: a billion 1 KB files cost far more in requests (and in per-file overhead for query engines) than a few large files holding the same bytes. On throughput: S3 scales request rate per prefix — roughly 5,500 GET and 3,500 PUT requests/second per prefix. Spreading keys across many prefixes multiplies achievable throughput, because S3 partitions its index by key prefix. Well-chosen prefixes (often the partition columns of your data) therefore serve both query pruning and raw throughput.

2.6 S3 as data-lake substrate — zone layout

A data lake on S3 is organised into zones by refinement stage — the same medallion idea seen in Azure, expressed as bucket/prefix conventions:

s3://company-lake/
  raw/      (bronze)  landing, immutable, source format, partitioned by date
  cleaned/  (silver)  validated, deduped, typed, Parquet, partitioned
  curated/  (gold)    business aggregates, star schemas, ready for BI/ML
Ingest --> raw/ --Glue/Spark--> cleaned/ --transform--> curated/ --> Athena/Redshift/BI
Worked example: Raw JSON events land at raw/events/dt=2026-07-05/…json. A Glue job parses, validates, and writes columnar Parquet to cleaned/events/dt=2026-07-05/…parquet. A second job aggregates to daily metrics in curated/daily_events/dt=2026-07-05/…. The date dt= prefix does triple duty: it is a Hive-style partition (query pruning), a lifecycle boundary (archive raw after 90 days), and a throughput spread (many date prefixes). Note the same physical bytes are re-shaped, not moved out of S3 — ELT in action.
Misconception: "Folders in S3 organise storage physically." There are no folders — only key strings. Deleting a "folder" means deleting each object under a prefix; "moving" means copy+delete every object. Design keys deliberately (partitions as prefixes) because the key is the physical layout for indexing and throughput.
Key takeaways:
  • S3 is a flat object store: buckets + object keys; "folders" are just prefixes; no atomic rename.
  • Consistency is now strong read-after-write (was eventual pre-2020) — old consistency workarounds are obsolete.
  • Storage classes (Standard→IA→Glacier→Deep Archive, plus auto Intelligent-Tiering) + lifecycle policies drive cost; versioning, SSE-KMS encryption, and multipart upload are the core object features.
  • You pay per request (avoid tiny-file storms); throughput scales per prefix (~5,500 GET/s per prefix) — prefixes serve both pruning and bandwidth.
  • Lay out the lake in raw/cleaned/curated (bronze/silver/gold) zones via prefixes; the same bytes are reshaped in place (ELT).

3. Glue and the Data Catalog

Raw files in S3 have no inherent schema an engine can query. AWS Glue supplies the missing metadata layer (the Data Catalog) and a serverless Spark engine for ETL. Understanding the catalog is essential because Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR all read from it.

3.1 Hive metastore lineage

The idea traces to Apache Hive: to run SQL over files in a data lake you need a metastore — a catalog mapping "table names + schemas + partitions" to "these files in this format at this location". Hive's metastore was the original; the Glue Data Catalog is AWS's managed, serverless drop-in for the Hive metastore. This lineage is why Glue tables use Hive concepts (databases, tables, Hive-style partitions like dt=2026-07-05) and why engines that "speak Hive metastore" plug into Glue directly.

3.2 The Glue Data Catalog as central schema store

The Data Catalog is a single, account-wide (region-scoped) registry of table definitions. A catalog "table" is metadata only — it does not hold data; it records the schema, the S3 location, the file format (Parquet/CSV/JSON), and the partitions. Multiple engines read the same table definition: Athena queries it, Redshift Spectrum reads external tables from it, EMR/Glue Spark load it. This is the linchpin of the AWS lakehouse — one schema definition, many query engines, over data that stays in S3.

3.3 Crawlers — inference mechanics and when they mislead

A Glue crawler scans a set of S3 paths, samples the files, infers the schema and partition structure, and writes/updates table definitions in the catalog automatically. Mechanically it: samples files, deduces column names/types, detects the format, recognises Hive-style partition folders (col=value), and can group similar files into one table. This is convenient but inference is a guess and can mislead:

  • Sampling can mis-type a column (a mostly-integer column with one non-numeric value inferred as string, or vice versa).
  • Schema drift between files can make the crawler either merge incompatible schemas or split one logical table into many.
  • Deeply nested or inconsistent JSON is inferred inconsistently across runs.
Misconception: "Run a crawler and trust the schema." For controlled pipelines, prefer explicitly defined tables (schema-as-code) or run the crawler once and then manage the table definition deliberately. Crawlers shine for discovery/exploration of unknown data; they are risky as the source of truth for production schemas, and re-crawling can silently change types under a running query.

3.4 Glue ETL — serverless Spark, DPUs, job bookmarks

Glue ETL is a serverless Apache Spark service: you write PySpark/Scala (or use Glue's DynamicFrame API) and Glue provisions and runs a transient Spark cluster, then tears it down. Capacity is measured in DPUs (Data Processing Units) — each DPU is a bundle of vCPU + memory; more DPUs = more parallelism = faster and pricier, billed per DPU-hour (with a minimum). The key incremental feature is job bookmarks: Glue persists state about what data a job has already processed (the highest partition/key/timestamp seen), so the next run automatically processes only new data. This is Glue's built-in equivalent of the watermark pattern — enable bookmarks and the job becomes incremental without you hand-managing a watermark table.

Worked example: A Glue job reads raw/events/ daily. With bookmarks disabled, every run reprocesses all history (slow, expensive, and re-emits duplicates). With bookmarks enabled, Glue records that it has processed up to dt=2026-07-04; today's run reads only dt=2026-07-05 — the new partition — and advances the bookmark on success. If the run fails, the bookmark is not advanced, so tomorrow safely retries the same new data. Same idempotent principle as Azure's watermark-after-success.
Key takeaways:
  • Glue Data Catalog = managed serverless Hive metastore: databases/tables/Hive-style partitions as metadata pointing at S3 files.
  • The catalog is the central, region-wide schema store read by Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR — one schema, many engines.
  • Crawlers infer schema/partitions by sampling — great for discovery, risky as production truth (mis-typing, drift, silent re-typing); prefer explicit schemas for pipelines.
  • Glue ETL = serverless Spark billed in DPU-hours; job bookmarks persist processed-state for automatic incremental runs (watermark equivalent).

4. Athena — serverless SQL over S3

Amazon Athena lets you run standard SQL directly over files in S3 with no servers to manage, paying per query. Its performance and cost are dominated by two ideas — schema-on-read and scan-based pricing — that reward good file design enormously.

4.1 Presto/Trino architecture

Athena is built on Presto/Trino, a distributed SQL query engine. Its architecture is a coordinator + workers: the coordinator parses the SQL, builds a plan, and splits the work; workers read chunks of data from S3 in parallel and process them; results stream back through the coordinator. Because it is serverless, AWS manages this cluster invisibly — you just submit SQL. The parallelism across workers is why Athena can scan terabytes quickly, and the cluster reads straight from S3 (no data loaded into a warehouse first).

4.2 Schema-on-read

Athena uses schema-on-read: the data sits in S3 in whatever format, and the schema (from the Glue Catalog) is applied at query time. Contrast schema-on-write (a traditional warehouse where data must conform to the schema before it is loaded). Schema-on-read means zero load step and instant queryability of raw files, at the cost of no enforced integrity and per-query interpretation overhead. This is the defining property of a query-in-place lake engine.

4.3 Partition pruning and projection

Partitioning physically splits a table's files into prefixes by column value (e.g. dt=2026-07-05/). When a query filters on the partition column, Athena reads only the matching partitions — partition pruning — instead of scanning the whole table. Since you pay per byte scanned, pruning is directly money saved. Partition projection is an optimisation where you tell Athena the partition scheme (a range/format) so it computes partitions mathematically rather than listing them from the catalog — vastly faster when there are millions of partitions.

4.4 CTAS mechanics

CTAS (Create Table As Select) runs a SELECT and writes its result to new S3 files as a new table, in a chosen format (Parquet), compression, and partitioning. It is the standard Athena tool for transforming/optimising data: read messy CSV, write partitioned Parquet, and every subsequent query is faster and cheaper. It is also how Athena does ELT within the lake.

4.5 Cost model — $/TB scanned

Athena's price is brutally simple and instructive: you pay per terabyte of data scanned (about $5/TB), with no charge for compute time or idle. Therefore the entire optimisation game is scan less. Two levers cut scans by an order of magnitude: (1) columnar formats (Parquet/ORC) — a query touching 3 of 50 columns reads only those 3 columns' bytes, not the whole row; (2) partitioning + pruning — read only relevant date/region partitions.

Worked cost example: A table is 1 TB of CSV; a query needs 2 of 40 columns for one day out of 365. As-is (CSV, no partitions): Athena scans the full 1 TB → 1 × $5 = $5 per query. Convert to partitioned Parquet: the one-day partition is ~1/365 of the data, and columnar reads touch ~2/40 of the width. Scan ≈ 1 TB × (1/365) × (2/40) ≈ 0.14 GB → about $0.0007 per query — a >99% cost cut, and far faster. This single transformation (CSV → partitioned Parquet) is the highest-ROI action in the entire AWS analytics stack.
Misconception: "SELECT * is fine, I'll just filter in the WHERE clause." With columnar formats, SELECT * forces reading every column's bytes; the WHERE filter runs after the scan, so you already paid to read them. Scan cost is set by which columns and partitions are read, not by how many rows survive the filter. Select only needed columns and filter on partition keys.

4.6 Limits and tuning

Athena is not a low-latency serving engine — queries have startup overhead and it is tuned for analytical scans, not thousands of concurrent point lookups (use a database/Redshift for that). Tuning is mostly file design: prefer Parquet/ORC, partition on common filter columns (but avoid over-partitioning into millions of tiny files), compact small files, and use partition projection at high partition counts. Query concurrency and per-account scan limits apply and are managed via workgroups (which also let you cap and monitor spend).

Key takeaways:
  • Athena = serverless Presto/Trino (coordinator+workers) running SQL over S3, no servers, pay per query.
  • Schema-on-read: schema (from Glue Catalog) applied at query time; no load step, no enforced integrity.
  • Partition pruning reads only matching prefixes; partition projection scales to millions of partitions; CTAS writes optimised tables.
  • Cost = ~$5 per TB scanned → Parquet (columnar) + partitioning cut scans 90–99%+; SELECT * defeats columnar savings.
  • Athena is for analytical scans, not low-latency/high-concurrency point lookups; tune via file format, partitioning, and compaction.

5. Redshift — the MPP data warehouse

Amazon Redshift is AWS's petabyte-scale data warehouse. Unlike Athena (query-in-place over S3), Redshift traditionally loads data into its own columnar storage tuned for fast, repeated analytical queries. Its performance rests on MPP architecture and physical data-layout choices you must understand.

5.1 MPP architecture — leader, compute nodes, slices

Redshift is Massively Parallel Processing. A cluster has one leader node (receives SQL, builds the query plan, coordinates, and aggregates final results — it holds no user data) and multiple compute nodes that store data and execute the plan in parallel. Each compute node is subdivided into slices — a slice is a portion of the node's memory and disk with its own share of the data and its own query threads. Data and work are spread across all slices, so a query runs in parallel across the entire cluster. The number of slices (parallelism) scales with cluster size, which is the core of Redshift's speed.

5.2 Columnar storage and zone maps

Redshift stores data column by column, not row by row. Analytical queries touch few columns over many rows; columnar storage reads only those columns' blocks (like Parquet), and stores each column compressed (values in a column are similar, so they compress well). Redshift also keeps zone maps — the min and max value of each 1 MB block per column. When a query filters a column, Redshift skips any block whose min/max range cannot contain matching rows, reading far less from disk. This block-skipping is why sort keys (next) matter so much.

5.3 Distribution styles — where data physically sits

Because data is spread across slices, how rows are distributed determines whether a join can happen locally on each slice or requires shuffling rows across the network (slow). The distribution style per table:

StyleHow rows are placedBest for
KEYBy hash of a chosen column; equal values land on the same sliceLarge tables joined on that key — join is local, no shuffle
ALLA full copy on every nodeSmall dimension tables — every join finds them locally
EVENRound-robin, evenly spreadTables not joined, or no clear join key — balanced load
AUTORedshift chooses/adapts (ALL when small, KEY/EVEN as it grows)Default; good when unsure

The theory: if two big tables are joined on column X and both use DISTKEY(X), matching rows already sit on the same slice, so the join is collocated — no data movement. Mis-distribute and Redshift must redistribute (shuffle) rows across the network for every join, which dominates query time at scale.

5.4 Sort keys and their link to zone maps

A sort key defines the physical order of rows on disk. Its power comes from zone maps: if rows are sorted by event_date, then each block holds a tight date range, so a query filtering on event_date skips almost all blocks via the zone map. Choose sort keys to match your most common range/filter predicates (often a date). Sort + zone maps together are Redshift's version of partition pruning.

5.5 WLM, concurrency scaling, Spectrum

  • WLM (Workload Management): defines query queues with memory/priority so a heavy ETL query does not starve interactive dashboards. Concurrency scaling automatically adds transient cluster capacity to handle bursts of concurrent queries, then removes it.
  • Redshift Spectrum: lets Redshift query data still sitting in S3 as external tables (defined in the Glue Catalog) without loading it — you join warehouse tables against lake data in one query. Spectrum is billed per TB scanned (like Athena) for the S3 portion. This is the bridge between the warehouse and the lake.

5.6 Vacuum, analyze, and the RA3/serverless evolution

Classic Redshift housekeeping: VACUUM reclaims space from deleted rows and re-sorts data into sort-key order (deletes/updates leave rows out of order and marked); ANALYZE refreshes the table statistics the planner uses to choose good plans. Modern Redshift automates much of this. Architecture has evolved: older node types coupled storage and compute; RA3 nodes separate them (compute scales independently, storage backed by "managed storage" on S3); Redshift Serverless removes cluster management entirely, auto-scaling capacity (measured in RPUs) and billing per use — the serverless economics finally reaching the warehouse.

5.7 When Redshift vs Athena vs EMR vs Snowflake

Worked decision:
Athena — occasional/ad-hoc SQL over lake data, unpredictable usage, no cluster to manage, pay-per-query. Choose when query volume is spiky and low-to-moderate.
Redshift — a warehouse hit constantly by many concurrent BI users/dashboards needing consistent low latency on modelled data; loading pays off because the same data is queried repeatedly. Choose for steady, high-concurrency serving.
EMR (Spark/Hadoop) — heavy custom big-data processing, ML, or non-SQL transformations at massive scale where you want full engine control. Choose for complex programmatic pipelines.
Snowflake — third-party warehouse chosen for its clean separation of storage/compute, multi-cluster concurrency, cross-cloud portability, and ease of use; competes with Redshift when teams want a cloud-agnostic, low-ops warehouse.
Rule of thumb: spiky ad-hoc → Athena; steady high-concurrency serving → Redshift/Snowflake; complex custom compute → EMR.
Misconception: "Redshift is just Athena with a cluster." They are architecturally different: Athena queries files in place with an ephemeral serverless engine (pay per scan, no state); Redshift loads data into its own optimised, distributed, sorted, statistics-driven storage for repeated fast queries. Redshift's distribution/sort/vacuum machinery has no analogue in Athena. Pick by access pattern, not by superficial "SQL over data".
Key takeaways:
  • Redshift MPP: leader (plans/aggregates, no data) + compute nodes split into slices that store and process data in parallel.
  • Columnar storage + zone maps (per-block min/max) skip irrelevant blocks; sort keys order data so filters skip most blocks.
  • Distribution style (KEY/ALL/EVEN/AUTO) decides if joins are collocated (local) or need a costly network shuffle — the #1 performance lever.
  • WLM/concurrency scaling manage load; Spectrum queries S3 external tables (Glue Catalog, pay-per-TB) to join lake + warehouse.
  • VACUUM re-sorts/reclaims, ANALYZE refreshes stats (now largely automatic); RA3 separates storage/compute, Serverless removes cluster ops.
  • Choose Athena (spiky ad-hoc), Redshift/Snowflake (steady high-concurrency serving), EMR (complex custom compute).

6. Streaming and messaging on AWS

Real-time data needs a service that ingests continuous event streams and delivers them to consumers. AWS offers several with overlapping-but-distinct semantics; choosing correctly requires understanding queues vs streams vs pub/sub vs event bus.

6.1 Kinesis Data Streams — shards vs Kafka partitions

Kinesis Data Streams is AWS's managed streaming service, conceptually close to Apache Kafka (covered in depth in the streaming section). A stream is divided into shards, the exact analogue of Kafka partitions: each shard is an ordered sequence of records, provides a fixed capacity (~1 MB/s or 1,000 records/s in, ~2 MB/s out), and preserves order within the shard. Records are routed to shards by a partition key (hash), so all events with the same key (e.g. one user id) land in the same shard and stay ordered. Total throughput = number of shards × per-shard capacity, so you scale by adding shards. Consumers track their position with a sequence number and data is retained for a configurable window (default 24 h, up to 365 days).

Resharding is how you change capacity: splitting a shard into two increases throughput; merging two shards reduces it. Because records are keyed to shards, resharding redistributes the key space and is a deliberate scaling operation (the newer "on-demand" mode auto-scales shards for you).

6.2 Kinesis Data Firehose — delivery semantics

Firehose is the "load a stream into a destination" service. It has no shards to manage; you point it at a source and a destination (S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, etc.) and it buffers incoming records by size or time (e.g. "flush every 5 MB or 60 seconds") and delivers them, optionally converting format (JSON → Parquet) and compressing en route. Its semantics are at-least-once delivery (a record may be delivered more than once on retry, so downstream must dedupe if needed) and near-real-time (bounded by the buffer interval, not truly instant). Use Firehose when the goal is simply "reliably land the stream in S3/warehouse"; use Data Streams when you need custom, ordered, low-latency consumer processing.

6.3 MSK — managed Kafka

Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) runs actual open-source Kafka as a managed service. Choose MSK over Kinesis when you specifically want Kafka — existing Kafka apps/connectors, the Kafka ecosystem (Kafka Connect, Streams), longer retention as a log, or portability across clouds. Kinesis is simpler and more deeply AWS-integrated; MSK is Kafka-native and portable. Same underlying partition/consumer-group theory (see the Kafka section) applies.

6.4 SQS, SNS, EventBridge — queue vs pub/sub vs bus

ServicePatternSemanticsUse for
SQSQueue (point-to-point)One message → consumed by one worker; decouples producers from workers; buffers backlogWork distribution, task queues, load-levelling
SNSPub/Sub (fan-out)One message → pushed to many subscribers at once (topics)Broadcast notifications, fan-out to many consumers
EventBridgeEvent bus + routingEvents routed by content rules to many targets; schema registry; SaaS/AWS event integrationEvent-driven architectures, routing by rule
Kinesis/MSKStream (ordered log)Ordered, replayable records; multiple independent readers; retained windowHigh-volume ordered streaming, replay, analytics

The distinctions: a queue (SQS) delivers each message to a single consumer and it disappears when processed — good for distributing work. Pub/sub (SNS) pushes each message to all subscribers — good for broadcasting. An event bus (EventBridge) adds content-based routing and integrations, sending events to different targets by rule. A stream (Kinesis/MSK) is an ordered, replayable log where many consumers read independently at their own pace and can re-read history — the only option when order, replay, and high-volume analytics matter.

Worked example: An order-placed event must (a) trigger fulfilment, (b) update analytics, (c) email the customer. SNS fans the single event out to three SQS queues (fan-out + buffering) so each service processes independently and a slow email service does not block fulfilment — SNS for broadcast, SQS per consumer for decoupled, retryable work. If instead you needed an ordered, replayable feed of every order for a real-time dashboard and later reprocessing, you would put orders on Kinesis/MSK, not SQS/SNS (which do not preserve a replayable ordered history).
Misconception: "SQS and Kinesis are interchangeable message services." SQS is an unordered (standard) work queue where a consumed message is deleted — you cannot replay it, and there is no shared ordered history. Kinesis is a retained, ordered log multiple consumers replay. If you need order, replay, or multiple independent readers, SQS is the wrong tool.
Key takeaways:
  • Kinesis Data Streams = shards (≙ Kafka partitions): ordered, keyed, fixed per-shard capacity; scale by adding/splitting shards (resharding).
  • Firehose buffers and delivers a stream to S3/Redshift/etc., at-least-once and near-real-time, with format conversion — "just land the stream".
  • MSK = managed real Kafka; pick it for Kafka ecosystem/portability, Kinesis for simplicity + deep AWS integration.
  • SQS = queue (one consumer, no replay), SNS = pub/sub fan-out, EventBridge = routing event bus, Kinesis/MSK = ordered replayable stream. Choose by ordering/replay/fan-out needs.

7. Compute for pipelines

Orchestration and transformation need compute. AWS offers a range from tiny event-driven functions to full clusters, and picking the right one is a recurring design decision. This chapter covers each execution model and ends with a decision matrix.

7.1 Lambda — the serverless function

AWS Lambda runs your code in response to an event, with no server to manage and scale-to-zero (you pay only per invocation and per GB-second of execution). The execution model: an event arrives → AWS provisions a short-lived execution environment → your function runs → the environment may be kept warm for reuse. Key constraints define what Lambda is for: a hard 15-minute maximum per invocation (so no long batch jobs), limited memory/CPU, and cold starts — the first invocation (or after idle) pays a startup delay to initialise the environment, which matters for latency-sensitive paths. The classic data pattern is the S3 event trigger: an object lands in a bucket → S3 emits an event → Lambda runs to process/validate/route that file. Lambda is ideal for small, quick, event-driven glue; wrong for long or heavy jobs.

Worked example: A partner drops a CSV into s3://landing/. An S3 ObjectCreated event triggers a Lambda that validates the header, records the file in a metadata table, and starts a Step Functions workflow (or a Glue job) to process it. The Lambda runs for 2 seconds and costs a fraction of a cent; nothing runs (or is billed) when no files arrive. But if that file needed a 40-minute Spark transform, Lambda's 15-minute limit rules it out — hand off to Glue/EMR instead.

7.2 Step Functions — state machine orchestration

Step Functions orchestrates multi-step workflows as a state machine: you declare states (task, choice, parallel, map, wait, fail) and transitions in JSON, and the service executes them with built-in retries, error catching, and state passing between steps. It is the AWS-native orchestrator for wiring together Lambdas, Glue jobs, and other services with reliable control flow — conceptually like ADF's control-flow activities, expressed as an explicit state machine.

7.3 Step Functions vs MWAA

MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) is managed Airflow — DAGs written in Python, a rich scheduler, huge operator ecosystem, and a UI DEs often already know. Step Functions is serverless, JSON-defined, pay-per-transition, deeply AWS-integrated, and scale-to-zero. Rule of thumb: choose MWAA for complex, code-heavy, cross-system DAGs where you want Airflow's ecosystem and Python; choose Step Functions for AWS-native, serverless, event-driven orchestration without running an Airflow cluster. (Airflow itself is covered in the orchestration section.)

7.4 EMR — cluster vs serverless

EMR (Elastic MapReduce) runs big-data frameworks (Spark, Hive, Presto, Flink) on managed clusters. EMR on EC2 gives you a real, tunable cluster (choose instance types, use cheap spot instances, full control) — best for large, steady, or highly-customised workloads. EMR Serverless removes cluster sizing: you submit a Spark/Hive job and AWS auto-provisions capacity for its duration — best for spiky or occasional jobs where you do not want to manage a cluster. Both are for heavy, code-driven big-data compute that exceeds Glue's or Lambda's scope.

7.5 ECS/Fargate for containerized ETL

When your ETL is packaged as a container (a custom Python/Java app, a tool not offered as a managed service), ECS (Elastic Container Service) runs it, and Fargate is the serverless mode where AWS runs the containers without you managing any EC2 hosts. Use ECS/Fargate for custom, long-running or containerised jobs that do not fit Lambda's limits and are not Spark (which would go to Glue/EMR).

7.6 Decision matrix

NeedUse
Short (<15 min), event-driven glue, scale-to-zeroLambda
Orchestrate steps, AWS-native, serverlessStep Functions
Complex Python DAGs, Airflow ecosystemMWAA (Airflow)
Heavy Spark/Hadoop, big scale, custom clusterEMR (EC2 or Serverless)
Serverless Spark ETL with catalog + bookmarksGlue ETL
Custom containerised app, no server managementECS/Fargate
Misconception: "Lambda can run any pipeline if I just add more memory." Lambda's 15-minute ceiling and limited resources are hard limits by design; it is glue and short tasks, not a batch engine. Long or heavy processing belongs on Glue, EMR, or Fargate. Forcing everything into Lambda leads to brittle chained functions and timeouts.
Key takeaways:
  • Lambda = event-driven, scale-to-zero functions with a 15-min cap and cold starts; the S3-event trigger is the canonical data pattern.
  • Step Functions = serverless state-machine orchestration (retries, error handling); MWAA = managed Airflow for code-heavy Python DAGs.
  • EMR runs heavy Spark/Hadoop as tunable clusters (EC2) or auto-provisioned (Serverless); Glue ETL is the lighter serverless-Spark option.
  • ECS/Fargate run custom containerised ETL without managing hosts.
  • Match compute to the job: short glue → Lambda; orchestration → Step Functions/MWAA; big Spark → EMR/Glue; custom containers → Fargate.

8. Data movement and CDC

Getting data into AWS from databases and other systems — especially keeping it continuously in sync — is its own discipline. AWS's primary tool is DMS, complemented by transfer services and cross-account patterns.

8.1 DMS architecture — full load + CDC

AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) moves data from a source database to a target (another database, S3, Redshift, etc.), either as a one-time migration or as ongoing replication. Its architecture: you provision a replication instance (a managed compute VM that runs the migration), define endpoints for source and target, and create a task that runs in up to two phases:

  • Full load: the initial bulk copy of existing data — reads the current contents of the source tables and writes them to the target.
  • CDC (Change Data Capture): after (and during) the full load, DMS reads the source's transaction log (redo/binlog/WAL) to capture every ongoing insert/update/delete and applies it to the target, keeping the two in sync. Because it reads the log, CDC captures deletes and preserves order — the properties plain timestamp-watermarking lacks (see Azure Ch. 6).
Source DB --(reads log)--> DMS Replication Instance --> Target (S3 / Redshift / DB)
   [1] Full load: bulk copy existing rows
   [2] CDC: stream inserts/updates/deletes from the transaction log, in order
Worked example: Migrate an on-prem PostgreSQL to S3 for a lake with zero downtime. DMS full-load copies all current rows to s3://lake/raw/pg/. Simultaneously CDC reads Postgres's WAL and streams every subsequent change to S3 (as change records). When the target is caught up you cut over reads to the lake; the source stayed online throughout. Because CDC reads the log, a row deleted in Postgres appears as a delete record — something a MAX(updated_at) watermark would have silently missed.

8.2 DataSync and the Transfer Family

  • AWS DataSync: a service for fast, managed transfer of files between on-prem storage (NFS/SMB) or other clouds and AWS (S3/EFS/FSx) — handles large-scale file movement with parallelism, integrity checks, and scheduling. Use it for bulk file/data-lake migration, not database change capture.
  • AWS Transfer Family: managed SFTP/FTPS/FTP endpoints backed by S3 — lets external partners upload/download via standard file-transfer protocols while the data lands directly in your S3 lake. Use it for partner file exchange over legacy protocols.

8.3 Cross-account patterns

Enterprises split data across accounts (Ch. 1), so pipelines must move data between accounts securely. The core mechanisms: (1) cross-account IAM roles — account A grants a role that account B's principals assume (from Ch. 1) to read/write; (2) S3 bucket policies — a resource policy on the bucket that explicitly allows a principal in another account; (3) Lake Formation cross-account sharing (Ch. 9) for governed table-level sharing; and (4) resource sharing (RAM) for sharing catalog/other resources. The recurring principle is the same as all AWS security: grant a narrowly-scoped, assumable role rather than copying credentials across accounts.

Misconception: "CDC gives me the source data in real time with no lag." CDC is near-real-time but not instant — there is latency from reading and applying the log, and heavy source write volume can make DMS lag. Also, if the source's transaction log is not retained long enough, or logging is misconfigured, CDC can miss changes. CDC needs source-side log configuration and monitoring of replication lag.
Key takeaways:
  • DMS = replication instance + source/target endpoints + task; runs full load (bulk copy) then CDC (log-based, captures inserts/updates/deletes in order).
  • Log-based CDC captures deletes and ordering that timestamp watermarking misses; it enables zero-downtime migration and continuous sync.
  • DataSync moves files at scale (on-prem/other-cloud → AWS); Transfer Family gives managed SFTP/FTPS backed by S3 for partner exchange.
  • Cross-account movement uses assumable IAM roles, bucket policies, Lake Formation sharing, and RAM — narrow assumable roles over shared keys.
  • CDC is near-real-time (has lag) and depends on correctly configured, sufficiently-retained source transaction logs.

9. Operations, security, cost — and the Azure↔AWS map

The AWS section closes with the operational trio — observability, cost governance, and lake security — plus a full translation table between the two clouds so knowledge transfers both ways.

9.1 CloudWatch — metrics, logs, alarms

Amazon CloudWatch is the observability backbone. Three pillars: metrics (numeric time-series like invocation count, DPU usage, queue depth, error rate — emitted by every service), logs (CloudWatch Logs collects application and service log streams for searching), and alarms (thresholds on a metric that fire an action — notify via SNS, trigger auto-scaling — when breached, e.g. "alarm if Lambda errors > 0 in 5 min"). This is the AWS analogue of Azure Monitor / Log Analytics: you route pipeline telemetry here, query it, and alarm on failures so humans are paged.

9.2 CloudTrail — the audit log

CloudTrail records who did what — every API call in the account (which principal, from where, what action, on what resource, when). Where CloudWatch answers "is the system healthy/how is it performing", CloudTrail answers "who deleted that table" — it is the audit/forensics/compliance log. Both are essential and distinct: metrics/logs for operations, CloudTrail for security auditing.

9.3 Cost management — tags, budgets

Cloud cost governance rests on tags (key-value labels on resources, e.g. team=analytics, env=prod, project=X) that let you slice the bill by team/project in Cost Explorer, and Budgets that track spend against a limit and alert (or act) when a threshold is crossed. Without a tagging discipline a large account's bill is an unattributable blob; with it, every dollar maps to an owner. This is the practical arm of the pay-per-use economics from Chapter 1.

9.4 Lake Formation — governed lake permissions

AWS Lake Formation adds a governance/permission layer over the S3 data lake and Glue Catalog. Instead of managing dozens of raw S3 bucket policies and IAM statements, Lake Formation lets you grant permissions at the database / table / column / row level ("this group may SELECT these columns of this table"), and enforces them across Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and Glue. It is the AWS answer to fine-grained, centrally-managed lake access — conceptually parallel to how Purview/RBAC govern access in Azure.

9.5 Well-Architected — the data lens highlights

AWS's Well-Architected Framework codifies best practice across pillars — Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. For data workloads the highlights are: automate operations and monitoring; enforce least-privilege IAM and encryption everywhere; design for failure and idempotent reprocessing; optimise for cost (columnar formats, partitioning, right storage class, kill idle compute); and choose the right tool for each access pattern. These are not new rules — they are the disciplined summary of everything in these two sections.

9.6 The full Azure ↔ AWS mapping

ConceptAzureAWS
Identity providerEntra ID (tenant)IAM (per account)
Billing/isolation boundarySubscriptionAccount
Resource groupingResource Group(tags / stacks / OU per account)
Multi-account/subscription governanceManagement Groups + PolicyOrganizations + OUs + SCPs
Secretless compute identityManaged IdentityIAM Role / instance profile
Object storage / data lakeBlob / ADLS Gen2S3
Hierarchical namespaceADLS Gen2 HNS(S3 is flat; no native HNS)
Secrets vaultKey VaultSecrets Manager / KMS
Orchestration / ETL serviceData Factory (ADF)Glue + Step Functions
Serverless Spark ETLMapping Data Flows / Synapse SparkGlue ETL / EMR Serverless
Metadata catalogPurview / Unity (Databricks)Glue Data Catalog
Serverless SQL over lakeSynapse serverless SQLAthena
MPP data warehouseSynapse dedicated poolRedshift
Managed OLTP databaseAzure SQL DatabaseRDS / Aurora
Streaming ingestEvent Hubs (Kafka-compatible)Kinesis Data Streams
Managed KafkaHDInsight Kafka / Event HubsMSK
Stream-to-store deliveryStream Analytics / Event Hubs CaptureKinesis Firehose
Message queueService Bus / Storage QueuesSQS
Pub/sub & event routingEvent GridSNS / EventBridge
Serverless functionsAzure FunctionsLambda
Managed AirflowData Factory Managed AirflowMWAA
Container ETL (serverless)Container Apps / ACIECS / Fargate
DB migration + CDCAzure Database Migration Service / ADF CDCDMS
Monitoring / logsAzure Monitor + Log AnalyticsCloudWatch
Audit logAzure Activity LogCloudTrail
Lake governance / permissionsPurview + RBAC/ACLsLake Formation
Unified analytics SaaSMicrosoft Fabric (OneLake)(no single equivalent; Glue+Athena+Redshift+LakeFormation)
Worked example — translating a design: An Azure pipeline is "ADF copies from Azure SQL to ADLS Gen2, a Synapse serverless query models it, results serve Power BI, secrets in Key Vault, ADF uses a managed identity." The AWS equivalent: "Glue (orchestrated by Step Functions) copies from RDS to S3, Athena queries and CTAS-models it, results serve QuickSight, secrets in Secrets Manager, Glue uses an IAM role."
