  • Fail-fast economics: a bug caught in seconds is nearly free; the same bug in production is enormously expensive. Keep pipelines fast so people integrate often.

2. Continuous Delivery / Deployment Theory

CI gets a verified change into the mainline. Continuous Delivery/Deployment (CD) is about getting that change safely into production. The two D's are often conflated but differ in one human step.

2.1 Deployment vs release

A subtle but powerful distinction: deployment is the technical act of putting new code onto the production servers; release is the business act of making a feature available to users. They can be decoupled — you can deploy code that's hidden behind a feature flag (a runtime switch), then "release" later by flipping the flag, with no new deployment. This lets teams deploy continuously and safely while controlling when users actually see changes. Continuous Delivery means every change that passes CI is automatically prepared and provably ready to deploy, but a human approves the final push to production. Continuous Deployment removes even that button — every passing change goes to production automatically. Delivery keeps a human gate; deployment is fully automatic.

2.2 Environments & promotion

Code advances through a series of environments that increasingly resemble production: typically dev (rapid, unstable, developers' playground), staging (a production-like mirror for final validation with realistic data/scale), and prod (the real thing). Promotion is moving the same tested artifact up this chain — build once, then promote that identical build through dev → staging → prod, changing only injected configuration (recall Docker's "build once, configure per environment"). Building separately per environment would mean prod runs something never tested; promoting one artifact guarantees what you validated is what ships.

2.3 Approvals

Between environments — especially into prod — teams often insert approval gates: a required human sign-off (a lead, or a change-advisory step) before promotion proceeds. This balances automation with control for high-stakes deployments and satisfies audit/compliance requirements ("who approved this prod change and when"). Continuous delivery keeps such a gate; continuous deployment automates it away where risk tolerance allows.

2.4 Rollback strategies

When a deployment goes wrong you need to get back to a good state fast. Two approaches: redeploy the previous version — because you build immutable, versioned artifacts (Docker images/tags), rolling back is just deploying the last known-good tag, which is fast and reliable; or roll forward with a revert — commit a fix (or a Git revert, from the Git section) and deploy that. Redeploy-previous is the emergency lever (fastest); roll-forward is cleaner once you understand the failure. Immutable versioned artifacts are what make either rollback trustworthy — you know exactly what "the previous version" is.

2.5 Blue-green and canary — applied to data

  • Blue-green — run two identical production environments, "blue" (current) and "green" (new). Deploy to green, validate it, then switch all traffic from blue to green at once. If green misbehaves, switch back instantly. Zero-downtime and instant rollback. In data, this maps to building a new version of a table/dataset alongside the live one and atomically swapping which one consumers read (revisited as "blue-green tables" in chapter 6).
  • Canary — release the new version to a small fraction first (say 5% of traffic), watch metrics, and only expand to 100% if it's healthy. It limits blast radius. In data, a canary looks like processing a subset of partitions or a shadow run comparing new vs old outputs before full cutover.

Both strategies exist to make deployment low-risk: blue-green trades double infrastructure for instant switch/rollback; canary trades a slower rollout for early detection on a small blast radius.

Worked example: A team practices continuous delivery. A change passes CI, is auto-deployed to staging where it runs against a production-like dataset, and waits at an approval gate. A lead approves; the same image tag etl:4.2.0 promotes to prod (build once, promote). It uses a blue-green table swap: the new logic writes orders_v2, a validation job checks row counts and key metrics against orders, and only then does a view atomically repoint consumers to orders_v2. A metric looks wrong an hour later — they repoint the view back to the old table instantly (blue-green rollback) and redeploy tag 4.1.0 while investigating.
Common misconception: "Deploying and releasing are the same thing." Decoupling them (via feature flags and traffic control) is what makes continuous deployment safe: you can ship code to production continuously while controlling — and instantly reversing — its exposure to users/consumers. Conflating them forces risky big-bang releases.
Key takeaways:
  • Deployment (code onto servers) ≠ release (feature visible to users); decouple with feature flags.
  • Continuous Delivery keeps a human approval before prod; Continuous Deployment automates even that.
  • Promote one immutable, versioned artifact through dev → staging → prod, changing only config — so prod runs exactly what was tested.
  • Roll back by redeploying the previous version (fast) or reverting forward (clean); immutable artifacts make this reliable.
  • Blue-green (two envs, instant switch/rollback) and canary (small-fraction first) reduce deployment risk — and map onto data via table swaps and partition/shadow runs.

3. The GitHub Actions Model

The abstract CI/CD ideas need a concrete engine. GitHub Actions is a widely-used one, and its vocabulary (shared broadly with GitLab CI, Azure Pipelines, etc.) is worth knowing precisely because it maps cleanly onto the theory.

3.1 Events → Workflows → Jobs → Steps → Runners

  • Event — the trigger. A push, a pull request opened, a schedule (cron), a tag, or a manual dispatch. Events are how "on every change" (CI) and "on merge to main" (CD) get wired up.
  • Workflow — a YAML file (in .github/workflows/) describing what to do when an event fires. A repo can have many workflows (e.g. one for tests, one for deploy).
  • Job — a unit of the workflow that runs on one machine. Jobs run in parallel by default and can declare dependencies (needs:) to force ordering — e.g. a deploy job that only runs after a test job succeeds.
  • Step — an individual command or reusable action within a job, run sequentially. Steps do the actual work: check out code, set up Python, install deps, run tests.
  • Runner — the machine (VM or container) that executes a job. GitHub provides hosted runners; you can also self-host runners inside your own network (needed to reach private resources like an internal warehouse).

So a CI workflow reads: "on pull_request (event), run a test job on an Ubuntu runner, whose steps check out code, set up the environment, and run the suite." The quality-gate theory of chapter 1 becomes this concrete YAML.

3.2 Matrix builds

A matrix runs the same job across multiple parameter combinations in parallel — e.g. test against Python 3.10 and 3.11, or against two Spark versions. Instead of writing the job several times, you declare the axes and GitHub fans out a job per combination. This catches version-specific breakage cheaply and in parallel.

3.3 Secrets & OIDC — the passwordless breakthrough

Pipelines often need credentials (to push images, deploy to cloud, reach a warehouse). Storing them requires care. Secrets are encrypted values GitHub injects into a workflow as environment variables at run time, never printed in logs — the basic mechanism for handing a workflow a token without committing it to the repo (recall the Git section: secrets must never live in history).

The bigger idea is OIDC (OpenID Connect), which enables passwordless cloud authentication. Instead of storing a long-lived cloud key as a secret (which can leak and lasts forever), the workflow presents a short-lived, cryptographically-signed identity token proving "I am this workflow, in this repo, on this branch." The cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP), configured to trust GitHub's identity provider, exchanges that token for temporary credentials scoped to exactly what's needed. Nothing long-lived is stored anywhere. This is a major security upgrade: no static keys to leak, credentials expire in minutes, and access is tied to a verifiable workflow identity. Modern data-platform CD strongly prefers OIDC over stored cloud keys.

3.4 Caching & artifacts

  • Caching — runners start fresh each time, so re-downloading dependencies every run is slow. A cache (keyed on, say, the hash of requirements.txt) restores the dependency directory between runs, cutting minutes off each build — the same order-and-reuse logic as Docker layer caching.
  • Artifacts — files a job produces that you want to keep or pass to a later job: build outputs, test reports, coverage, a packaged wheel. Artifacts persist beyond the ephemeral runner and can be downloaded or consumed by a downstream job (e.g. build produces an image reference that deploy uses).
Worked example: A workflow triggers on: pull_request. A test job runs on an Ubuntu runner across a matrix of Python 3.10/3.11; its steps check out code, restore a pip cache keyed on requirements.txt, install deps, and run pytest — uploading the coverage report as an artifact. A separate deploy job declares needs: test and on: push to main; it authenticates to AWS via OIDC (no stored keys), builds and pushes the pipeline image, and promotes it. The matrix guards version compatibility, the cache keeps it fast, OIDC keeps it secure, and needs: enforces test-before-deploy — the whole CI/CD theory realized in one file.
Common misconception: "Store the AWS access key as a GitHub secret — that's the secure way." Long-lived stored keys are a standing liability: if leaked they work forever and everywhere their scope allows. OIDC removes them entirely by issuing short-lived, workflow-scoped credentials on demand. Prefer OIDC; reserve stored secrets for systems that can't do federated identity.
Key takeaways:
  • Event triggers a Workflow of Jobs (parallel, orderable via needs) of Steps, executed on Runners.
  • Matrix runs a job across parameter combinations (e.g. Python versions) in parallel.
  • Secrets inject credentials safely; OIDC goes further — passwordless, short-lived, workflow-scoped cloud auth with no stored keys.
  • Caching reuses dependencies across runs for speed; artifacts persist and pass outputs between jobs.

4. Testing Data Code

CI is only as good as the tests it runs. Testing data code has a special structure, because there are really two things that can be wrong — the code and the data — and traditional software testing only addresses the first.

4.1 The test pyramid, adapted to data

The classic test pyramid says: have many fast, cheap unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and few slow end-to-end tests at the top. Adapted to data engineering, the three layers become:

  • Unit tests — transformations with fixture frames. Test a single transformation function in isolation by feeding it a small, hand-crafted input DataFrame (a "fixture") with known values and asserting the exact output. No warehouse, no cluster — just the logic. These are fast and numerous, and they pin down business rules ("nulls in amount become 0," "duplicate order IDs collapse to the latest"). This is where most of your data-logic testing should live.
  • Integration tests — against a test warehouse. Run the pipeline (or a chunk) against a real (but small/disposable) database or warehouse to verify the pieces work together: connections, SQL dialect, schema DDL, that the write actually lands. Slower and fewer; they catch wiring problems units can't.
  • Data tests — expectations on real data. Assertions that run against actual production data as it flows: "no nulls in the primary key," "revenue is non-negative," "row count is within 10% of yesterday." These don't test code correctness — they test that the data meets its contract (deep-dived in the Data Quality section). They live in the pipeline, not just in CI.

4.2 Where each kind lives — dbt tests and Great Expectations placement

Unit and integration tests run in CI (on every PR, before merge) — they gate code. Data tests run in the pipeline at runtime — they gate data, on every batch, because the data changes even when the code doesn't. Tools: dbt tests attach simple assertions (not-null, unique, accepted-values, referential integrity, plus custom SQL) directly to models and run as part of the dbt build; Great Expectations defines richer "expectation suites" (distributions, ranges, cross-column rules) that run as a validation step in the pipeline. Placement is the key idea: code tests belong in CI as a merge gate; data tests belong in the running pipeline as a data gate. (Chapter 3 of the Data Quality section formalizes the ingest-gate vs post-transform-audit placement.)

4.3 Why data testing fundamentally differs

Here is the crux an interviewer probes: in software, the code is the variable and the data is fixed; in data engineering, the code can be perfectly correct and the data can still be wrong. A transformation can pass every unit test forever, and yet one morning the upstream source sends nulls it never sent before, or duplicates a batch, or shifts a currency — and your correct code faithfully produces wrong results. Software tests can't catch this because the defect is in the input, which arrives fresh and unpredictable every run. That's why data engineering needs a whole second category of testing — data quality checks on live data — layered on top of ordinary code tests. Correct code is necessary but not sufficient; the data must also be validated continuously.

Worked example: A clean_orders function has a unit test: given a fixture frame with a duplicate order ID and a null amount, assert the output has one row per ID and zero nulls. This passes in CI on every PR — the code is proven correct. Separately, a dbt test on the orders model asserts order_id is unique and amount ≥ 0, running on every production batch. One day the source doubles a file; the code still dedups within a batch correctly, but cross-batch duplicates slip through — the data test (unique order_id across the table) is what catches it. Two categories, two failures, two safety nets.
Common misconception: "If my transformations are well unit-tested, my data is reliable." Unit tests prove the code behaves on the inputs you imagined. They say nothing about the messy, changing real inputs production actually sends. Data reliability needs runtime data tests on real data in addition to code tests — the two catch different failure classes.
Key takeaways:
  • Data test pyramid: many unit tests (transformations on fixture frames) → fewer integration tests (against a test warehouse) → data tests (expectations on real data).
  • Code tests (unit/integration) gate in CI before merge; data tests gate in the running pipeline on every batch.
  • dbt tests attach assertions to models; Great Expectations runs expectation suites as a pipeline step.
  • The defining difference: correct code + wrong data still yields wrong results — so DE needs runtime data validation on top of code tests.

5. Deploying Data Platforms

Deploying a data platform means promoting not just code but pipeline definitions, warehouse objects, and infrastructure across environments. Each major tool has a mechanism; the common thread is "define it as code, then promote it."

5.1 Databricks Asset Bundles

A Databricks Asset Bundle (DAB) packages a Databricks project — notebooks/code, job definitions, cluster configs, pipelines — as declarative YAML plus source, versioned in Git. You then deploy the bundle to a target workspace (dev, staging, prod), with per-target overrides (smaller clusters in dev, real schedules in prod). This makes Databricks deployments reproducible and promotable through CI/CD instead of clicking around the UI — the same bundle promotes across environments, differing only by target config (the "build once, configure per environment" principle again).

5.2 ADF promotion via ARM / az-cli

Azure Data Factory (ADF) pipelines are defined as JSON. To promote them, ADF exports the factory as an ARM template (Azure's infrastructure-as-code format), which a release pipeline deploys into the next environment, substituting environment-specific parameters (connection strings, storage accounts) at deploy time. The Azure CLI (az-cli) scripts these steps in CI/CD. The pattern mirrors DABs: the pipeline definition is an artifact, parameterized per environment and promoted, so staging and prod run the exact structure validated in dev.

5.3 dbt in CI — slim CI and state:modified

Running all dbt models and tests on every PR is slow and wasteful when a PR only touches a few models. Slim CI solves this: dbt compares the PR against the last production run's stored state (the manifest) and, using state:modified, builds and tests only the changed models and everything downstream of them. This dramatically shrinks CI time while still catching anything the change could break. It's a data-specific application of the fail-fast/keep-it-fast principle from chapter 1 — test exactly what's affected, no more.

5.4 Schema migrations discipline

Changing a table's schema in production is dangerous — a dropped or renamed column can break every consumer. Schema migration discipline means treating schema changes as versioned, reviewed, forward-only steps (migration scripts in Git, applied in order), and favoring backward-compatible changes: add columns rather than remove, deprecate before deleting, expand-then-contract (add the new column, migrate readers, only later drop the old one). This lets consumers keep working across the change — the discipline that prevents a "quick schema tweak" from cascading into an outage. (Schema evolution is explored further in the System Design section.)

5.5 Infrastructure as Code — the Terraform mental model

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) means defining your cloud resources (storage, warehouses, clusters, networks, permissions) in declarative files under version control instead of clicking in a console. Terraform is the leading tool, and its mental model is three words:

  • State — Terraform records the current real infrastructure it manages in a state file, its source of truth for what exists.
  • Plan — you write the desired state in config; terraform plan diffs desired vs current state and shows exactly what it would create, change, or destroy — a preview you review before touching anything.
  • Apply — terraform apply executes that plan, reconciling reality to your declared desired state, and updates the state file.

This is the same declarative-desired-state-plus-reconciliation idea as Kubernetes (Docker section) — you describe the target, the tool computes and applies the delta. IaC makes infrastructure reproducible, reviewable (the plan is a diff in a PR), and promotable across environments, and it prevents the "someone changed something in the console and now prod differs from staging" drift.

Worked example: A PR changes two dbt models. Slim CI, using state:modified against the prod manifest, rebuilds only those two plus their three downstream models and runs their tests — 5 models instead of 300, done in a minute. Separately, the platform's warehouses and storage are defined in Terraform; the PR that resizes a warehouse shows a terraform plan diff ("~ modify warehouse size S → M") that a reviewer approves before apply. A schema change to add a column follows expand-then-contract: the migration adds customer_segment, readers adopt it, and only a later PR removes the deprecated old column — no consumer ever breaks.
Common misconception: "Just make the schema/infra change directly in prod to move fast." Manual production changes create drift (prod no longer matches the tested config), leave no review trail, and can break consumers instantly. The discipline — define as code, plan/review, promote — is slower per change but vastly safer and reproducible, and it's what makes rollbacks and audits possible.
Key takeaways:
  • Databricks Asset Bundles and ADF ARM/az-cli both package pipeline definitions as promotable, parameterized artifacts.
  • dbt Slim CI uses state:modified to build/test only changed models + downstream — fast, targeted CI.
  • Schema migration discipline: versioned, backward-compatible, expand-then-contract changes so consumers never break.
  • IaC (Terraform) = declarative desired state; plan previews the diff, apply reconciles reality — reproducible, reviewable, drift-free infrastructure.

6. Versioning & Environments for Data

Software CI/CD versions code. Data adds a second dimension — the data itself changes independently of code — and this makes "environments for data" genuinely harder than for software. This chapter addresses that extra dimension.

6.1 Versioning code vs versioning data

You version code so you can reproduce and roll back logic. But a data pipeline's output depends on both the code version and the input data at that time — so reproducing a past result needs both. Versioning data means being able to reference the exact state of a dataset at a point in time. Table formats like Delta/Iceberg provide this via time travel (query the table as of a version/timestamp), and tools like lakeFS bring Git-like branching/commits to data lakes. The mental shift: in DE, "what produced this number?" requires pinning code version and data version together.

6.2 Reprocessing after logic changes

When you fix or change transformation logic, the data already produced by the old logic is now stale — it was computed the old way. Reprocessing (or backfilling, deep-dived in the Data Quality section) means re-running the new logic over historical inputs to bring past outputs in line with the new code. The design questions this raises — how far back to reprocess, how to do it without disrupting live consumers, how to make it idempotent — are central to data platform operations and are why data deployments are more involved than "just ship the code": shipping new logic often implies a data-recomputation plan.

6.3 Blue-green tables pattern

Applying blue-green deployment (chapter 2) to data: build the new version of a table alongside the current one (e.g. write orders_v2 while orders stays live), validate it fully (row counts, key metrics, data tests), then atomically swap consumers to the new version — typically by repointing a view or renaming — so readers cut over instantly and can be rolled back just as fast by repointing to the old table. This gives data deployments zero-downtime, validated, instantly-reversible cutovers, the same benefits blue-green gives code. It's the safe way to ship a big transformation change.

6.4 Feature-branch data isolation — zero-copy clone

Developers need to test pipeline changes against realistic data without corrupting production and without copying terabytes. Zero-copy cloning (Snowflake clones, Databricks shallow clones) creates an isolated, writable copy of a table or database that shares the underlying storage until modified (copy-on-write) — so it's instant and free until you change something. A developer branches a clone for their feature, experiments freely, and throws it away when done, with production untouched. Combined with per-developer workspaces/schemas, this gives data the equivalent of a feature branch: isolated environments to validate data changes cheaply, the missing piece that makes true CI/CD for data practical.

Worked example: An engineer changes the revenue calculation. They zero-copy clone the prod warehouse (instant, no storage cost) into their dev schema, run the new logic there, and compare results to prod — catching that the change double-counts refunds. After fixing, they deploy via blue-green: the pipeline writes revenue_v2, a validation job confirms totals tie out to within tolerance against the old table, and a view atomically repoints BI to revenue_v2. Because the old table remains, a discovered issue is a one-line rollback. Finally, because the logic changed, they schedule a controlled reprocessing of the last 90 days so history reflects the corrected calculation.
Common misconception: "CI/CD for data is the same as for software — just version the code." Data has a second axis: the data itself. You must think about data versioning, reprocessing old outputs after logic changes, safe table cutovers, and isolated data environments for testing. Ignoring the data axis is why "we just deployed the new logic" so often silently produces inconsistent historical data.
Key takeaways:
  • Reproducing a data result needs the code version and the data version; time travel / lakeFS provide data versioning.
  • Changing logic implies reprocessing historical outputs so past data matches the new code — plan it.
  • Blue-green tables: build alongside, validate, atomically swap a view/name — zero-downtime, instantly reversible data cutovers.
  • Zero-copy clones give instant, cheap, isolated data environments — the feature-branch equivalent that makes real CI/CD for data workable.

Data Quality & Reliability — Complete Theory

Why data breaks, how to measure and enforce quality, how to reconcile across systems, how to build idempotent reliable pipelines, and how to run incident response like a production professional.

1. Why Data Breaks — A Taxonomy of Failure

To build reliable data systems you must first know the enemy: the specific ways data goes wrong. Unlike a software crash, bad data often fails silently — the pipeline runs green, dashboards render, and yet the numbers are subtly (or catastrophically) wrong. Cataloguing the failure sources gives you a checklist to defend against.

1.1 The failure taxonomy

  • Schema drift — the shape of incoming data changes: a column is renamed, dropped, retyped, or added upstream without warning. Your pipeline either errors or, worse, silently maps the wrong field.
  • Upstream changes — the meaning or behavior of a source changes: a source system deploys new logic, changes an enum's values, or alters how it calculates a field. Your code is unchanged but its inputs now mean something different.
  • Late-arriving data — records that belong to an earlier period arrive after you've already processed and reported that period, so your "final" numbers were incomplete. (Handled in chapter 5.)
  • Duplicates — the same record arrives more than once (a retried delivery, a re-sent file, a double-run job), inflating counts and sums if not deduplicated.
  • Semantic drift — the data is structurally fine but its meaning erodes over time: a "status" field accumulates new undocumented values, a unit silently changes from dollars to cents, a category is repurposed. The schema passes; the interpretation is wrong.
  • Backfill accidents — a reprocessing/backfill run double-writes, overwrites good data, or reprocesses with wrong parameters, corrupting history. (This is why backfills need the safe protocol in chapter 5.)

1.2 The cost of bad data

Bad data is expensive in ways that compound. There's the direct cost — a wrong revenue figure drives a bad decision, a miscalculated balance triggers a compliance breach, a broken feature degrades an ML model. There's the detection lag cost — silent data errors can persist for weeks before someone notices, by which point many downstream decisions and datasets are contaminated (a "garbage in, garbage everywhere" cascade). And there's the trust cost, often the most damaging: once stakeholders catch the data lying to them a few times, they stop trusting the entire platform and revert to spreadsheets, undermining the whole data function. This is why detecting bad data fast (chapter 6's observability) is as important as preventing it — the longer it hides, the more it costs.

Worked example: An upstream team changes an order-status enum from SHIPPED to DISPATCHED without notice (upstream change + semantic drift). Your pipeline's schema is unaffected — it's still a string column — so nothing errors. But your "shipped orders" metric, which filters status = 'SHIPPED', silently drops to near zero. No alert fires because the pipeline ran fine. A stakeholder notices the dashboard a week later; by then, downstream reports and an ML feature built on that metric are all contaminated. A distribution monitor on the status field's values (chapter 6) would have caught the new enum value the day it appeared.
Common misconception: "If the pipeline ran without errors, the data is fine." Most serious data-quality failures are silent — the code executes perfectly on inputs that are structurally valid but wrong in content or meaning. Green pipelines and correct data are different guarantees; you need explicit data checks to assert the second.
Key takeaways:
  • Failure sources: schema drift, upstream (behavioral) changes, late data, duplicates, semantic drift, backfill accidents.
  • Bad data fails silently — green pipelines can emit wrong numbers.
  • Costs compound: direct wrong decisions + contamination cascade during detection lag + eroded stakeholder trust.
  • Fast detection is as vital as prevention; the longer bad data hides, the more it poisons downstream.

2. Data Quality Dimensions Formalized

"Good data" is vague until you decompose it into measurable dimensions. Each dimension is a distinct question about the data, and — crucially — each maps to a concrete SQL check you can run. Making quality measurable is what lets you monitor and enforce it.

2.1 The six core dimensions with measurable definitions

  • Completeness — is all the expected data present? Measured as the fraction of non-null (or non-missing) values in required fields, or expected-vs-actual row counts. "Are any required fields empty? Did we get all the rows we should have?"
  • Accuracy — does the data correctly describe the real world? The hardest to measure automatically because it needs a source of truth; often checked by reconciliation against an authoritative system (chapter 4).
  • Consistency — do related pieces of data agree, within and across systems? E.g. order totals equal the sum of line items; the same customer has the same ID everywhere. "Do the numbers that should match, match?"
  • Timeliness — is the data fresh enough for its purpose? Measured as the lag between an event happening and it being available. "Is the latest data recent enough?"
  • Uniqueness — is each real-world entity represented exactly once? Measured by counting duplicate keys. "Are there dupes on the primary key?"
  • Validity — does each value conform to its rules — type, format, range, allowed set? "Are emails well-formed? Are amounts non-negative? Are statuses in the allowed enum?"

2.2 SQL check patterns for each

The power of formalizing dimensions is that each becomes a query returning a number you can threshold and alert on:

-- COMPLETENESS: % of rows with a null required field
SELECT 100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN customer_id IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
       / COUNT(*) AS pct_missing_customer
FROM orders;

-- UNIQUENESS: how many primary keys are duplicated?
SELECT order_id, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM orders GROUP BY order_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

-- VALIDITY: rows violating a range / allowed-set rule
SELECT COUNT(*) AS invalid_rows
FROM orders
WHERE amount < 0
   OR status NOT IN ('NEW','SHIPPED','DELIVERED','CANCELLED');

-- CONSISTENCY: order total must equal sum of its line items
SELECT o.order_id
FROM orders o
JOIN (SELECT order_id, SUM(line_amount) AS s
      FROM order_lines GROUP BY order_id) L
  ON o.order_id = L.order_id
WHERE o.total <> L.s;

-- TIMELINESS: how stale is the freshest record?
SELECT MAX(event_ts) AS latest,
       CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - MAX(event_ts) AS lag
FROM events;

Each query yields a metric (a percentage, a count, a lag) that a check compares against a threshold. This is the bridge from the abstract idea "quality" to an automated, enforceable gate — and it plays directly to a strength you already have as a SQL user: most DQ checks are just well-chosen aggregate queries.

Worked example: A daily orders load is guarded by six checks, one per dimension: completeness (≤ 0.1% null customer_id), uniqueness (zero duplicate order_id), validity (zero negative amounts / bad statuses), consistency (order totals tie to line items), timeliness (latest event < 3 hours old), and a row-count sanity band (within 20% of the trailing average, a completeness proxy). Each is a SQL query returning a number; the pipeline fails or alerts if any breaches its threshold. Formalizing quality into six queryable dimensions turned a vague "is the data ok?" into six objective, automated yes/no gates.
Common misconception: "Data quality means one big 'is it good?' check." Quality is multi-dimensional; a dataset can be complete but invalid, or valid but stale, or unique but inconsistent with another system. You must check the dimensions separately, because they fail independently and each needs its own measurement and threshold.
Key takeaways:
  • Six measurable dimensions: completeness, accuracy, consistency, timeliness, uniqueness, validity.
  • Each maps to a concrete SQL check returning a number to threshold — the bridge from "quality" to enforceable gate.
  • Dimensions fail independently; check them separately with their own thresholds.
  • Accuracy usually needs an external source of truth (reconciliation); the rest are largely self-contained queries.

3. The Testing Stack & Where Checks Live

Knowing what to check (chapter 2) leaves two questions: how to express checks maintainably, and where in the pipeline to place them. Both have real design consequences.

3.1 Checks-as-code

Checks-as-code means expressing data-quality rules as versioned, reviewed definitions in your repository — not ad-hoc queries someone runs by hand. The benefits mirror why we version everything else: the checks live next to the pipeline, evolve with it through PR review, run automatically, and form documentation of the data's expected shape. When someone asks "what does 'valid' mean for this table?", the answer is the committed checks. This turns tribal knowledge into an enforceable, auditable spec.

3.2 The tool landscape compared

ToolStyleBest fit
dbt testsAssertions attached to models (not-null, unique, accepted-values, relationships, custom SQL)Teams already using dbt; checks that run as part of the transform build
Great ExpectationsRich "expectation suites" (distributions, ranges, cross-column), with data docsDetailed, statistical validation as a standalone pipeline step
SodaConcise checks in a simple YAML-like language (SodaCL)Lightweight, readable monitoring across many datasets

All three implement checks-as-code; they differ in expressiveness and where they naturally sit. dbt tests are frictionless if you already transform with dbt; Great Expectations is the most powerful for statistical/distribution checks; Soda favors concise, broad coverage. The choice matters less than the discipline of having checks versioned and running automatically.

3.3 Where checks live — ingest gate vs post-transform audit

Placement is a genuine design decision:

  • Ingest gate — validate data as it enters, before it flows downstream. Catches bad data at the door so it never contaminates anything. The cost: it can block ingestion, so you need a plan for what to do with rejected data (quarantine, chapter 3.4).
  • Post-transform audit — validate the output after transformation, before publishing to consumers. Catches errors your logic introduced and confirms the final product meets its contract. This is where "is what I'm about to serve correct?" gets answered.

Mature pipelines do both: gate on the way in and audit on the way out, because inputs and transformations fail differently. The design tension is strictness vs availability — an aggressive ingest gate protects downstream but can halt the pipeline; a lenient one keeps data flowing but risks propagating bad records.

3.4 Severity, quarantine, and Write-Audit-Publish

Not every check failure should stop the world. Severity levels distinguish errors (block the pipeline — e.g. a null primary key) from warnings (log/alert but continue — e.g. a slightly-off distribution). Quarantine is the pattern of diverting failing records to a separate holding area (a quarantine/dead-letter table) so good records proceed while bad ones are isolated for inspection and reprocessing — you don't lose the bad data, and you don't let it poison the good.

The gold-standard placement pattern is Write-Audit-Publish (WAP): (1) Write the new data to a staging location that consumers can't see; (2) Audit it there by running all your quality checks; (3) Publish — only if the audit passes — by atomically making the staged data the live version (a swap/rename, like blue-green tables from the CI/CD section). If the audit fails, you never publish, and consumers keep seeing the last good data. WAP is powerful because it guarantees consumers never see unvalidated data — bad data is stopped before the publish step, not discovered after it's already live. This is the reliability pattern to name in interviews.

Write-Audit-Publish (WAP): 
   new batch
      |
      v
  [WRITE] --> staging table (hidden from consumers)
      |
      v
  [AUDIT] --> run DQ checks (completeness, unique, valid, ...)
      |            |
   pass?           fail?
      |               \--> DO NOT publish; alert + quarantine
      v                    (consumers still see last good data)
  [PUBLISH] --> atomic swap: staging becomes live table
      |
      v
   consumers only ever read validated data
Worked example: A nightly load uses WAP. The pipeline writes to sales_staging, then runs checks: unique sale_id, non-negative amount, row count within 20% of trailing average. Tonight an upstream glitch duplicated a file, so the uniqueness check fails. Because the checks run in the audit phase on the staging table, the pipeline stops before publish — sales (the live table consumers read) is untouched and still shows yesterday's good data. The duplicate rows are quarantined for inspection and a warning-severity distribution anomaly is logged for review. Consumers never saw the bad batch; the team fixes and reruns. Contrast a naive pipeline that writes straight to sales and checks after — the bad data would already be live.
Common misconception: "Run the quality checks right after writing to the production table." If you write to the live table first and check after, consumers can read the bad data in the window before your check fails — and rolling it back is messy. WAP inverts this: write to a hidden staging area, check there, and only publish on success, so bad data is never exposed. Check before publish, not after.
Key takeaways:
  • Checks-as-code: versioned, reviewed, auto-running rules that double as documentation.
  • dbt tests (in-transform), Great Expectations (rich/statistical), Soda (concise/broad) all do checks-as-code; pick by fit.
  • Place checks at the ingest gate (stop bad data at the door) and as a post-transform audit (verify output); mature pipelines do both.
  • Use severity (error vs warning) and quarantine (isolate bad records). WAP — Write to staging, Audit there, Publish only on pass — guarantees consumers never see unvalidated data.

4. Reconciliation Theory

Reconciliation is the discipline of proving that two datasets that should agree actually do — typically a source and its copy/derivation in a target system. This is the core of your production-support work at Jio, and formalizing it turns hard-won experience into interview-ready theory. Reconciliation is how you assert the "accuracy" and "consistency" dimensions from chapter 2 across system boundaries.

4.1 The three tie-out levels: count, sum, hash

Reconciliation works in increasing strength, and you escalate only as far as needed:

  • Count tie-out — do source and target have the same number of rows? The cheapest check; catches missing or extra records. But equal counts don't prove the values match (you could have the right number of wrong rows).
  • Sum (aggregate) tie-out — do key numeric columns sum to the same total on both sides (total revenue, sum of balances)? Catches value-level corruption that counts miss. Cheap and powerful for financial data because a single wrong amount changes the sum. Still not perfect — offsetting errors could cancel out.
  • Hash tie-out — compute a hash/checksum of each row (or of the whole dataset) and compare. Row-level hashing pinpoints which rows differ; it's the strongest check because any single-byte difference changes the hash. More expensive, so reserved for when count+sum aren't enough or when you must locate exact discrepancies.

The practical protocol: start with count (fast, catches gross errors), add sum on key measures (catches value corruption), and drop to row hashing when you need certainty or must find the specific mismatched rows. Each level catches what the cheaper one can miss.

4.2 Tolerance windows

Exact equality isn't always the right bar. Because of late-arriving data and processing timing, a source and target compared at the same instant may legitimately differ slightly (the target is a few minutes behind). A tolerance window defines acceptable difference — either a time allowance ("compare source-as-of-T against target, allowing T minus 15 minutes") or a magnitude allowance ("counts within 0.01%"). Tolerances prevent false alarms from normal timing skew while still catching real breaks. Setting them well — tight enough to catch problems, loose enough to ignore benign lag — is a core reconciliation skill.

4.3 Source-vs-target strategies across systems

Reconciling across heterogeneous systems (e.g. an Oracle source vs a Snowflake target, or a Kafka stream vs a Delta table) adds friction: different SQL dialects, type representations, null handling, and timezones can make "the same" data look different. Strategies: agree on a canonical form (normalize types, timezones, rounding on both sides before comparing); reconcile on a business key plus a few measures rather than every column; and reconcile per partition/period (day by day) so a break localizes to a specific slice rather than "somewhere in a billion rows." Cross-system reconciliation is really about defining a fair, apples-to-apples comparison despite representational differences.

4.4 Sampling for large tables

Full row-hash reconciliation of billions of rows can be prohibitively expensive. Sampling trades certainty for cost: reconcile a representative subset (random sample, or a targeted slice like the most recent or highest-value partitions) to gain statistical confidence without scanning everything. A common layered approach: cheap full count+sum tie-outs on the whole table (always), plus row-hash on sampled or recent partitions (for depth). Sampling won't catch a needle-in-a-haystack single-row error, so it's paired with the full aggregate checks that would move if such errors accumulated.

Worked example: Replicating a MySQL transactions table to Snowflake. Daily reconciliation runs three tiers per day-partition: (1) count — COUNT(*) on both sides for yesterday, expecting equality within a tolerance for records still trickling in (a 15-minute late-arrival window); (2) sum — SUM(amount) on both, which catches a corrupted value the count would miss; (3) hash — on a 1% random sample plus the full most-recent partition, computing a row checksum to locate exact mismatches if the sums diverge. When one day's sum was off by one transaction's value, the sample hash didn't catch it but the sum tie-out flagged the day, and a targeted full-partition hash then pinpointed the single mismatched row — a decimal-precision difference in how the two systems stored the amount, fixed by canonicalizing rounding on both sides.
Common misconception: "Matching row counts means the data reconciled." Equal counts only prove the same number of rows, not the same content — you can have the right count of wrong rows, or offsetting errors. Real reconciliation layers count and sum (value-level) and, when needed, hash (exact) checks, because each strength catches errors the weaker ones cannot.
Key takeaways:
  • Reconcile in increasing strength: count (rows present) → sum (values correct) → hash (exact, locates the diff).
  • Tolerance windows (time or magnitude) absorb benign late-data/timing skew without hiding real breaks.
  • Cross-system reconciliation means defining an apples-to-apples comparison (canonical types/timezones/rounding, business keys, per-partition).
  • Sampling gives cheap depth on huge tables, paired with full aggregate tie-outs that move if errors accumulate.

5. Pipeline Reliability — Idempotency & Correct Reprocessing

Reliability is the property that a pipeline produces correct results despite retries, partial failures, duplicates, and reruns — all of which will happen in production. The foundational concept is idempotency, and everything else (exactly-once, safe backfills) builds on it.

5.1 Idempotency, formally

An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times has the same effect as applying it once. This is the single most important reliability property, because distributed systems retry: a job times out and reruns, a message is redelivered, an operator manually re-triggers a failed task. If your write is idempotent, a retry is harmless — it converges to the same correct state. If it's not, a retry double-counts, duplicates rows, or corrupts totals. Concretely, an idempotent write means "processing the same input twice leaves the target identical to processing it once."

How to achieve it: prefer overwrite/upsert by key over blind append. Writing a partition by replacing it (overwrite partition 2026-07-05 with the full recomputed day) is idempotent — rerun and you get the same partition. Blind INSERT ... APPEND is not — rerun and you double the rows. Using a stable business key with MERGE/upsert (insert if new, update if exists) is idempotent — the same record maps to the same row every time. Idempotency is designed in by choosing key-based, replace-or-merge semantics rather than accumulate semantics.

5.2 Exactly-once, decomposed

"Exactly-once processing" sounds like a delivery guarantee but is really a construction. Networks can't guarantee a message is delivered exactly once — you get at-least-once (retried until acknowledged, so possibly duplicated) or at-most-once (never retried, so possibly lost). Exactly-once effect is achieved by combining two things: at-least-once delivery (never lose data) + an idempotent sink or deduplication (never double-count). The system delivers possibly-duplicate messages, and the idempotent write (or an explicit dedup on a unique event ID) collapses duplicates so the result is as if each was processed once. So the mantra: exactly-once = at-least-once + dedup/idempotency. There's no magic; it's a reliable-delivery guarantee plus an idempotent consumer.

5.3 Checkpoints and state

For a pipeline to resume correctly after a crash, it must remember how far it got. A checkpoint periodically persists the processing position (e.g. the Kafka offsets consumed, the last processed watermark) and any in-flight aggregation state. On restart, the job reads the last checkpoint and resumes from exactly there — not re-processing everything (wasteful) nor skipping ahead (data loss). Combined with idempotent writes, checkpointing gives crash-safe, resumable processing: replay from the checkpoint may reprocess a few records, but idempotency makes that harmless. (This connects to the streaming discussion in the Kafka/Spark sections.)

5.4 The safe backfill protocol

A backfill re-runs a pipeline over historical data — to fix a bug, apply new logic, or fill a gap. Done carelessly it corrupts history (recall "backfill accidents," chapter 1). Done with a protocol it's safe:

Safe backfill protocol:
  1. Scope & freeze: define the exact date/partition range and the precise logic version to apply; snapshot or note the current state so you can roll back.
  2. Isolate: backfill into a separate location (staging table/partitions), never directly over live data — so consumers are unaffected while you work.
  3. Idempotent, partition-at-a-time: process one partition at a time with overwrite/upsert semantics, so any partition can be safely re-run and a failure localizes to one partition.
  4. Validate: run the quality checks and reconcile the backfilled staging data against expectations (count/sum tie-outs vs the old data where appropriate).
  5. Publish atomically: swap the validated backfilled partitions into place (blue-green / WAP style), so the cutover is instant and reversible.
  6. Throttle & monitor: pace the backfill to avoid overwhelming shared resources, watching for impact on live pipelines.

The through-line: isolate, make each unit idempotent and small, validate before publishing, and cut over atomically — the same reliability primitives (idempotency, WAP, blue-green) applied to reprocessing.

5.5 Late and out-of-order data strategies

Events often arrive late or out of order (a mobile device was offline; network paths differ). Strategies: watermarks — declare "I'll wait up to N minutes/hours for late data, then finalize the window," bounding lateness against completeness; reprocessing windows — keep recent periods "open" and recompute them as late data trickles in, only sealing after a grace period; and idempotent updates — because your writes are idempotent (5.1), incorporating a late record is just a safe re-run of its partition. The design tension is latency vs completeness: wait longer and you're more complete but less timely; finalize sooner and you're timely but may miss stragglers. Watermarks make that trade-off explicit and controllable.

Worked example: A daily aggregation job is triggered twice by mistake (an operator re-ran it). Because it overwrites the day's partition rather than appending, the second run simply reproduces the identical partition — no double-counting. Its upstream stream uses at-least-once delivery with a dedup on event_id, giving exactly-once effect. When a batch of yesterday's events arrives late this morning, the job — running with a 6-hour watermark — recomputes yesterday's still-open partition idempotently to include them, then seals it. Later a logic bug is found; the team backfills 30 days using the safe protocol: into staging, one partition at a time with overwrite, validated by count/sum tie-outs against expectations, then atomically swapped in. Every step leaned on idempotency to stay safe under reruns.
Common misconception: "Exactly-once is a delivery guarantee the system gives me." Delivery is at-most-once or at-least-once; exactly-once is an effect you engineer by combining at-least-once delivery with an idempotent sink or explicit dedup. If your writes aren't idempotent, retries — which are inevitable — will double-count no matter what "exactly-once" label a tool advertises.
Key takeaways:
  • Idempotency (apply-many = apply-once) is the core reliability property; achieve it with overwrite-by-partition or key-based MERGE, not blind append.
  • Exactly-once = at-least-once delivery + dedup/idempotent sink — an engineered effect, not free delivery magic.
  • Checkpoints persist position + state for crash-safe resume; with idempotency, replay-on-restart is harmless.
  • Safe backfill: scope/freeze → isolate to staging → idempotent partition-at-a-time → validate → atomic publish → throttle.
  • Late/out-of-order data: watermarks bound lateness (latency vs completeness), reprocessing windows + idempotent updates fold in stragglers safely.

6. Observability & Incident Response

Prevention (checks, WAP, idempotency) reduces failures but never eliminates them. Data observability is how you detect problems fast, and incident response is how you handle them well when they occur. This chapter is where your Jio production-support experience becomes explicit interview gold.

6.1 The four monitor types

Data observability watches four signals, each catching a different failure class from chapter 1:

  • Freshness — is data arriving on time? Monitors the lag since the last update; catches stalled pipelines and stuck sources (the timeliness dimension). "The table hasn't updated in 6 hours" is often the first sign of an outage.
  • Volume — is the amount of data normal? Monitors row counts per load against expected ranges; catches missing data (a partial load) or floods (a duplicate load). A sudden drop or spike in row count is a strong early signal.
  • Schema — has the structure changed? Monitors columns, types, and nullability; catches schema drift (a renamed/dropped/retyped column) before it silently breaks logic.
  • Distribution — have the values shifted? Monitors statistical properties (null rate, mean, min/max, cardinality, category frequencies); catches semantic drift and upstream behavioral changes — like the new DISPATCHED enum from chapter 1 — that structural checks miss.

Together these approximate "is this data behaving like it usually does?" and surface the silent failures that green pipelines hide.

6.2 Alert design

Detection is worthless if alerts are ignored. Good alert design has several elements. Severity — distinguish critical (wake someone up: prod data wrong/missing) from warning (review during business hours) so response matches impact. Ownership — every alert routes to a clear owner who can act; an alert nobody owns is noise. Runbooks — each alert links to documented steps ("if freshness alert on table X: check source Y, then rerun job Z"), so responders act fast without re-deriving the fix each time. And the central hazard: alert fatigue — if alerts fire too often, are too noisy, or are mostly false positives, responders learn to ignore them, and the one real alert gets missed. Fighting fatigue means tuning thresholds, suppressing duplicates, alerting only on actionable conditions, and ruthlessly deleting alerts that never lead to action. Fewer, higher-signal alerts beat a flood of noise.

6.3 The RCA discipline

When an incident happens, Root Cause Analysis (RCA) is the structured practice of finding why it happened so it doesn't recur — the heart of production support. Three techniques:

  • Timeline reconstruction — build an ordered account of what happened when: when the bad data was introduced, when it was detected, what changed just before (a deploy? an upstream change?), what actions were taken. The timeline turns chaos into a causal narrative and often reveals the trigger (e.g. "the break started exactly at the 02:00 upstream deploy").
  • The 5 Whys — repeatedly ask "why?" to drill from symptom to root cause. "Revenue was wrong. Why? Duplicate rows. Why? The source file was sent twice. Why? A retry fired after a timeout. Why? The ingest wasn't idempotent. Why? It appended instead of merging on key." The root cause (non-idempotent ingest) is actionable; the symptom (wrong revenue) is not.
  • Blameless postmortem — document the incident focusing on systems and processes, not individual fault. The premise: people act reasonably given the information and tools they had, so the fix is better systems (a missing check, a fragile design), not blame. Blamelessness is practical, not just kind — if people fear punishment they hide information, and you lose the data needed to actually prevent recurrence.
Blameless postmortem template:
  1. Summary — what happened, in one paragraph.
  2. Impact — who/what was affected, for how long, how bad (rows, dashboards, decisions, dollars).
  3. Timeline — key events with timestamps (introduced → detected → mitigated → resolved).
  4. Root cause — the underlying why (via 5 Whys), not just the symptom.
  5. Detection — how it was found, and how long detection took (could it have been faster?).
  6. Resolution — what fixed it (immediate mitigation vs permanent fix).
  7. Action items — concrete, owned, dated preventive changes (add a check, make ingest idempotent, tune an alert).
Worked example (your interview story): A freshness+volume alert fires — a table is stale and its row count dropped. The on-call owner opens the runbook, reconstructs the timeline (break began at 02:00, coinciding with an upstream schema deploy), and runs 5 Whys to reach the root cause: the upstream renamed a column, the ingest silently mapped it to null, and the downstream filter then dropped most rows. Immediate mitigation: pin the ingest to the old column mapping and reprocess the affected partition idempotently. The blameless postmortem's action items: add a schema monitor to catch upstream renames instantly, add a data contract (chapter 7) with the upstream team, and add a volume check as a WAP audit gate so the bad batch would never have published. This is exactly the production-support RCA narrative interviewers want to hear — detection, disciplined root-causing, and systemic prevention.
Common misconception: "The root cause was human error — someone made a mistake." "Human error" is where analysis stops being useful, not a root cause. Blameless RCA asks why the system let a reasonable person's action cause harm — no validation, a fragile mapping, a missing alert. Blaming individuals hides information and fixes nothing; fixing systems prevents recurrence.
Key takeaways:
  • Four observability monitors: freshness (on time?), volume (right amount?), schema (structure changed?), distribution (values shifted?) — they catch the silent failures green pipelines hide.
  • Alert design: severity, clear ownership, runbooks — and relentless fighting of alert fatigue (fewer, actionable, high-signal alerts).
  • RCA discipline: reconstruct the timeline, drill with 5 Whys to the real root cause, and write a blameless postmortem.
  • Blameless = practical: fix systems, not people, so information flows and recurrence is actually prevented. This is your production-support edge.

7. Data Contracts & Lineage

The final layer of reliability is preventing upstream surprises and understanding impact when they occur. Data contracts stop breakage at the source; lineage lets you trace it. Together with SLAs they turn reliability from reactive firefighting into a managed guarantee.

7.1 Data contracts — schema + SLA + semantics as an API

A data contract is a formal, agreed specification of what a data producer promises to a consumer — treating a dataset like an API with a stable interface. It typically covers three things: the schema (columns, types, nullability — the structure), the SLA (freshness, availability, volume expectations — the operational guarantees), and the semantics (what fields mean, allowed value sets, business rules — the meaning). The power of a contract is that it makes the producer accountable: the upstream team can't silently rename a column or change an enum, because doing so violates a contract that's enforced (checks fail, the change is caught in the producer's CI before it ships). Contracts attack the root cause of most incidents from chapter 1 — uncoordinated upstream changes — by making the interface explicit and versioned, exactly like a software API can't change its signature without breaking (and thus alerting) its callers.

7.2 Lineage — how tools infer it, column-level

Data lineage is the map of how data flows and transforms: which sources feed which tables, which columns derive from which upstream columns, and which dashboards/models consume each dataset. Table-level lineage tracks dataset-to-dataset dependencies; column-level lineage goes finer, tracing that gold.revenue derives from silver.orders.amount minus silver.refunds.amount. Tools infer lineage primarily by parsing SQL/transformation code — reading the queries and following which inputs produce which outputs — supplemented by runtime metadata from the orchestrator and warehouse query logs. Lineage answers two vital operational questions: impact analysis ("if this source breaks or changes, what downstream breaks?" — trace forward) and root-cause tracing ("this metric is wrong — what upstream could have caused it?" — trace backward). It makes the RCA timeline of chapter 6 far faster because you can see the dependency path instead of guessing it.

7.3 SLAs, SLOs, and error budgets for data

Borrowed from site reliability engineering and applied to data:

  • SLA (Service Level Agreement) — the external promise to consumers ("the sales table will be fresh by 6 AM and 99.9% available"). It's the commitment, often with consequences if breached.
  • SLO (Service Level Objective) — the internal target you engineer toward, usually stricter than the SLA to give headroom ("aim for 6 AM freshness 99.95% of the time"). SLOs are measured against SLIs (indicators — the actual freshness/availability numbers).
  • Error budget — the flip side of an SLO: if the target is 99.9%, you have a 0.1% "budget" of allowed failure. This reframes reliability economically — you don't need perfection, you need to stay within budget. If you're burning the budget fast, slow down risky changes and invest in reliability; if you have budget to spare, you can move faster. The error budget turns the endless "how reliable is reliable enough?" debate into a measurable, negotiated number that balances velocity against stability.
Worked example: The analytics team's orders table has a data contract with the source: schema (fixed columns/types), SLA (fresh by 6 AM, 99.9% of days), and semantics (status ∈ a defined enum, amount ≥ 0). When the source team wants to rename a column, their CI runs the contract check and blocks the change until they coordinate — the incident from chapter 1 is prevented at the source. Column-level lineage, inferred by parsing the dbt SQL, shows a finance dashboard depends on orders.amount; when a freshness SLO burns error budget one week, the team sees exactly which dashboards are at risk and pauses risky deploys until the budget recovers. Contract + lineage + SLO/budget turn reliability into a managed, measurable guarantee rather than hope.
Common misconception: "Lineage is just documentation / a nice diagram." Lineage is an operational tool: it powers instant impact analysis before a change (what will this break?) and fast root-cause tracing during an incident (where did this bad number come from?). Without it, both are slow manual detective work. And a data contract isn't a wiki page — it's an enforced spec that fails the producer's build when violated; an unenforced "contract" prevents nothing.
Key takeaways:
  • A data contract treats a dataset as an API: schema + SLA + semantics, enforced so producers can't silently break consumers — attacking the top incident cause.
  • Lineage (table- and column-level), inferred by parsing transformation SQL, powers forward impact analysis and backward root-cause tracing.
  • SLA = external promise, SLO = stricter internal target (measured by SLIs), error budget = allowed failure that balances velocity vs reliability.
  • Contracts + lineage + SLOs convert reliability from reactive firefighting into a measurable, managed guarantee.

Data Engineering System Design — Complete Theory

A universal framework for designing data platforms: requirements, back-of-envelope math, the design spaces for ingestion/storage/processing/serving, cross-cutting concerns, and three fully worked designs.

1. The Design Framework & Back-of-Envelope Math

A data-engineering system design question — "design a platform for X" — assesses whether you can turn vague requirements into a justified architecture, reason about scale with numbers, choose components with real trade-offs, and think about operating the thing. It's the same skill whether in an interview or designing a real platform: structured thinking under ambiguity. The antidote to a rambling answer is a framework you apply every time.

1.1 The universal framework

Walk every design through these stages, in order:

  1. Requirements — clarify what's actually needed before designing. Functional: what data, what outputs, what questions must it answer? Non-functional: latency (how fresh?), scale (how much data?), reliability, budget. Ask questions; a good design starts by narrowing the problem, not by naming technologies.
  2. Volume math — quantify the scale with back-of-envelope estimates (below). Numbers drive every downstream choice; "big data" is meaningless, "50 GB/day growing to 18 TB/year" is designable.
  3. Architecture — sketch the high-level flow (sources → ingest → store → process → serve) before details. Get the shape right first.
  4. Components — choose concrete technologies for each box, each justified by the requirements and math (not by fashion).
  5. Trade-offs — explicitly state what each choice costs (latency vs throughput, cost vs speed, complexity vs flexibility). Showing you see the trade-offs is what distinguishes a senior answer.
  6. Operations — how it's monitored, how it fails and recovers, how it's deployed and scaled, what it costs to run. Designs that ignore operability are incomplete.

The discipline of moving through these stages — rather than jumping to "I'd use Kafka and Spark" — is itself most of what's being evaluated.

1.2 Back-of-envelope math — the training

You must be able to go from a business number to infrastructure sizing in your head. The chain:

  • Events/day → GB/day. Multiply event count by average event size. Example: 100 million events/day × 1 KB/event = 100 GB/day of raw data. (Know the units: 1 KB × 1 million = 1 GB; × 1 billion = 1 TB.)
  • GB/day → storage/year. Multiply by 365, then account for replication (×3 is common), retention, and derived copies (bronze/silver/gold each store a version). 100 GB/day × 365 ≈ 36.5 TB/year raw; with 3× replication and multiple layers, plan for a multiple of that.
  • Throughput → partitions/executors. Peak events/second sizes your streaming and parallelism. 100 M/day averages ~1,160/sec, but peaks might be 5–10×, so design for ~10 K/sec. If one Kafka partition handles ~10 MB/s and one Spark core processes a known rows/sec, divide the peak load by per-unit capacity to get partition/executor counts.
  • Peak vs average. Always design for peak, not average — traffic is bursty (business hours, sales events). A rule of thumb of 2–10× average depending on domain keeps you from under-provisioning.

You don't need precision — you need the right order of magnitude and the reasoning shown. Stating "~100 GB/day, ~36 TB/year raw, peak ~10 K events/sec, so ~16 Kafka partitions and a handful of executors" demonstrates exactly the quantitative judgment interviewers probe.

Worked example: "Design analytics for an app with 50 M daily active users, each generating ~40 events/day at ~500 bytes." Math: 50 M × 40 = 2 billion events/day; × 500 B = 1 TB/day raw. Yearly: ~365 TB/year raw, and with 3× replication plus bronze/silver/gold copies, budget for >1 PB/year of storage. Throughput: 2 B/day ≈ 23 K events/sec average; design for ~5× peak ≈ 115 K/sec. At ~10 MB/s per Kafka partition and ~500 B events, that's ~58 MB/s peak → a dozen-plus partitions with headroom, say 32. These numbers immediately tell you: this needs distributed streaming ingestion, a lake (not a single database), and partitioned parallel processing — the math chose the architecture class before you named a single tool.
Common misconception: "Jump straight to naming technologies (Kafka! Spark! Snowflake!)." Leading with tools signals you're pattern-matching, not designing. The framework — requirements → math → architecture → components → trade-offs → operations — is what's assessed. The math in particular is non-negotiable: it's how you justify why a technology is needed rather than asserting it.
Key takeaways:
  • Apply the framework every time: requirements → volume math → architecture → components → trade-offs → operations.
  • Start by clarifying requirements (functional + non-functional latency/scale/reliability/budget), not by naming tools.
  • Master the math chain: events/day → GB/day → storage/year (×replication×layers); peak throughput → partitions/executors. Design for peak, not average.
  • Numbers justify architecture — the right order of magnitude with shown reasoning beats false precision.

2. Ingestion Design Space

Ingestion is how data enters your platform from sources. Choosing the right ingestion pattern is the first architectural fork, and it's driven by the source type and the latency requirement.

2.1 The four ingestion patterns

  • Batch pull — you periodically query the source (a database, an API) and pull a chunk of data (e.g. every night, "give me all rows updated today"). Simple, robust, easy to reason about. Best when data is naturally periodic and some latency (hours) is acceptable. Weakness: latency equals the batch interval, and repeatedly scanning large sources is heavy.
  • Push — the source sends data to you (webhooks, events pushed to an endpoint or a queue) as things happen. Lower latency and no polling load, but you don't control the timing and must handle bursts and retries/duplicates.
  • CDC (Change Data Capture) — capture changes to a source database by reading its transaction log (the write-ahead log the DB already keeps), streaming inserts/updates/deletes as they commit. This gives near-real-time replication without heavy queries on the source and without missing changes — the log is the source of truth. The premier pattern for keeping a warehouse in sync with an operational database (covered in the worked design 7c).
  • Streaming — continuous ingestion of an unbounded event flow (through Kafka or similar) for real-time use cases. Lowest latency, highest complexity; needed when seconds matter (fraud, live dashboards).

2.2 A decision tree

Choosing an ingestion pattern: 
Is the source a database you must keep in sync?
   |-- yes --> Need low latency / no source load? --> CDC (read the log)
   |           else (nightly is fine)             --> Batch pull
   |
Is the source emitting a continuous event stream?
   |-- yes --> Need real-time (seconds)?  --> Streaming (Kafka)
   |           else                        --> Micro-batch / batch
   |
Does the source PUSH to you (webhooks/events)?
   |-- yes --> Push ingestion (endpoint/queue + dedup)
   |
Default / files dropped periodically --> Batch pull

The two questions that resolve most cases: how fresh must the data be? (drives batch vs streaming/CDC) and what can the source tolerate? (drives pull vs CDC — CDC spares an operational DB the load of repeated big queries).

2.3 The connector landscape

You rarely hand-build ingestion. Connectors are pre-built integrations for common sources. Managed EL tools (Fivetran, Airbyte) offer hundreds of source connectors (databases, SaaS APIs) with CDC and schema handling built in — great for standard sources, trading per-row cost for speed of delivery. For streaming, Kafka Connect provides source/sink connectors (including Debezium for CDC). The design judgment: use managed connectors for commodity sources (why rebuild a Salesforce extractor?), and build custom ingestion only where you have unusual sources or scale/cost reasons. Knowing when to buy vs build is itself a design signal.

Worked example: A platform ingests three sources. (1) The core MySQL operational DB must stay near-real-time in the warehouse without slowing the app → CDC via Debezium reading the binlog into Kafka. (2) Salesforce CRM data, needed daily → a managed batch-pull connector (Fivetran), because rebuilding a CRM extractor is wasted effort. (3) Clickstream from the mobile app, needed in seconds for a live dashboard → streaming into Kafka. Three sources, three patterns, each chosen by latency need and source constraints — not one tool forced everywhere.
Common misconception: "Stream everything for lowest latency." Streaming is the most complex and expensive pattern; using it where a nightly batch suffices adds cost, operational burden, and failure modes for no benefit. Match the pattern to the actual latency requirement — most analytics is fine with batch or CDC; reserve full streaming for genuine real-time needs.
Key takeaways:
  • Four patterns: batch pull (simple, periodic), push (source sends, handle bursts/dupes), CDC (log-based near-real-time DB sync, no source load), streaming (continuous, real-time, complex).
  • Resolve with two questions: how fresh must data be, and what load can the source tolerate?
  • CDC is the go-to for keeping a warehouse in sync with an operational database without heavy queries.
  • Use managed connectors for commodity sources; build custom only for unusual sources/scale. Don't stream what a batch can do.

3. Storage Design Space

Where data lives is chosen by access pattern — how it will be read and written — not by default. Picking the wrong storage class is a foundational, expensive mistake, so this is where a selection matrix earns its keep.

3.1 The storage selection matrix

StoreOptimized forUse when
Data lake (object storage: S3/ADLS/GCS)Cheap, massive, any format; schema-on-readRaw/landing data, huge volumes, ML, flexibility over structure
Lakehouse (Delta/Iceberg on a lake)Lake economics + ACID transactions + fast SQLYou want warehouse reliability on open-format lake storage (the modern default)
Data warehouse (Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift)Fast analytical SQL over structured data (OLAP, columnar)BI, aggregations, dashboards over structured data
OLTP database (Postgres/MySQL)Fast row-level reads/writes, transactionsOperational apps, point lookups/updates — sources, not analytics stores
NoSQL (Cassandra/DynamoDB/Mongo)Massive scale, low-latency key lookups, flexible schemaKnown-key access at huge scale/throughput; serving layers

The driving question is the access pattern: full-table analytical scans and aggregations → columnar warehouse/lakehouse; single-row lookups by key at scale → NoSQL/OLTP; store-everything-cheaply-figure-out-later → lake. The classic error is using a warehouse as an operational key-value store (slow point lookups) or a row-store OLTP DB for big analytical scans (crawls). OLAP (analytics, columnar, scans) and OLTP (operations, row-oriented, point access) are opposite optimizations — match the store to which one you're doing.

3.2 Hot/warm/cold tiering

Not all data is accessed equally, and storage cost scales with access speed. Tiering places data by access frequency: hot (frequently queried, recent) on fast/expensive storage; warm (occasional) on cheaper storage; cold (rarely touched, kept for compliance/history) on cheapest archival storage (e.g. S3 Glacier) where retrieval is slow but storage is pennies. Lifecycle policies move data down the tiers automatically as it ages. This is a major cost lever: keeping years of rarely-read history on hot storage wastes money; tiering can cut storage bills dramatically while keeping recent data fast. Cost engineering (chapter 6) leans heavily on tiering.

3.3 Partition and cluster design method

Within a store, partitioning physically splits a table by a column's value (commonly date) into separate files/directories, so a query filtering on that column reads only the relevant partitions — partition pruning — instead of scanning everything. The method: partition by the column you most often filter on (usually a date), at a granularity that yields reasonably-sized partitions (not so fine you get millions of tiny files — the "small files problem" — nor so coarse that pruning doesn't help). Clustering (or Z-ordering/sorting within partitions) co-locates rows with similar values of a secondary column so queries skip more data via file-level min/max statistics. The design rule: partition on the primary filter dimension (date), cluster on the next-most-common filter (e.g. a high-cardinality key like customer_id), and size partitions to avoid both huge scans and tiny-file overhead. Good partition design is often the single biggest query-performance and cost factor.

Worked example: Designing storage for the app-analytics platform (1 TB/day from chapter 1). Raw events land in a lake (cheap, any format) as bronze. A lakehouse (Delta) holds cleaned silver and aggregated gold tables — ACID + fast SQL on open format. BI runs on the gold tables (warehouse-style access). A per-user feature-serving layer that needs millisecond key lookups uses NoSQL (DynamoDB), not the warehouse. Tables are partitioned by event date (the universal filter) and clustered by user_id (the next-most-common filter), so a "last 7 days for these users" query prunes to 7 partitions and skips most files. Recent 90 days stay hot; older data tiers to cold archival storage via lifecycle policy. Each choice traces to an access pattern and the volume math.
Common misconception: "One database can serve everything." Analytical scans and operational key-lookups are opposite optimizations (columnar OLAP vs row-oriented OLTP); NoSQL scale trades away rich querying. Forcing one store to do all jobs means it does most of them badly. Real platforms compose several stores, each matched to an access pattern — and partition/tier within them for cost and speed.
Key takeaways:
  • Choose storage by access pattern: lake (cheap/any format/raw), lakehouse (lake + ACID + SQL, modern default), warehouse (analytical SQL/BI), OLTP (operational row access — sources), NoSQL (key lookups at scale/serving).
  • OLAP (scans/aggregations, columnar) vs OLTP (point access, row-oriented) are opposite optimizations — don't force one store to do both.
  • Tier hot/warm/cold by access frequency to slash storage cost while keeping recent data fast.
  • Partition on the primary filter (date) for pruning, cluster on the next filter; size partitions to avoid huge scans and tiny-file overhead.

4. Processing Design Space

Processing turns raw data into useful data. The core design choice is the processing model, driven by the real latency requirement — the honest answer to "how fresh does the output actually need to be?", which is usually less demanding than stakeholders first claim.

4.1 Batch vs micro-batch vs streaming

  • Batch — process a large bounded chunk on a schedule (hourly, nightly). Highest throughput and efficiency per row, simplest to reason about and reprocess, cheapest. Latency = the schedule interval. The default for analytics; most reporting is perfectly served by batch.
  • Micro-batch — process small batches very frequently (every few seconds/minutes), as Spark Structured Streaming does. A pragmatic middle ground: near-real-time latency with much of batch's simplicity and exactly-once tooling. Good when you need "seconds-to-minutes" freshness without true event-at-a-time complexity.
  • Streaming — process each event as it arrives, continuously. Lowest latency (sub-second possible), but the most complex: you manage state, watermarks, out-of-order data, and continuous operation. Reserved for genuine real-time needs (fraud, alerting, live personalization).

The decision rule: use the simplest model the latency requirement allows. Batch unless you need fresher; micro-batch for near-real-time; full streaming only when seconds genuinely matter. Complexity and cost rise steeply from batch → streaming, so don't buy latency you don't need.

4.2 Incremental processing patterns

Reprocessing the entire history every run is wasteful once data is large; incremental processing handles only what's new or changed:

  • Watermark/high-water-mark — track the maximum timestamp/ID processed so far; next run processes only records beyond it. Simple and effective when the source has a reliable increasing column.
