If a company does batch analytics, the odds it uses dbt are high, and it's the single highest-ROI tool to learn for your target roles.

dbt in one glance: 
-- models/gold/fct_orders.sql
select
  o.order_id,
  o.customer_id,
  o.amount,
  d.region
from {{ ref('silver_orders') }} o
join {{ ref('dim_customer') }} d using (customer_id)
where o.status <> 'cancelled'

# schema.yml
models:
  - name: fct_orders
    columns:
      - name: order_id
        tests: [unique, not_null]
dbt resolves the refs into a DAG, runs silver_orders and dim_customer first, then fct_orders, then runs the tests. That's the whole model of modern SQL transformation.

3.2 Spark / Databricks — big-data transformation

Apache Spark is a distributed compute engine for transforming data too big for one machine, using Python/Scala/SQL. Databricks is the managed, batteries-included Spark platform (plus a lakehouse). Chosen when data is large (tens of GB to PB), or when you need programmatic transforms beyond SQL, or ML workloads. It's more powerful and more complex than dbt-on-a-warehouse; the two increasingly coexist (dbt can even run on Spark/Databricks).

3.3 Warehouse-native SQL

You can transform with plain SQL and stored procedures directly in Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift, no dbt. Common in older or simpler setups. It works but lacks dbt's testing, modularity, and lineage — which is exactly why dbt took over.

3.4 pandas / polars / duckdb — small-data transformation

For genuinely small data (fits in memory), you don't need distributed compute. pandas is the classic Python dataframe library; polars is a faster, modern alternative; duckdb is an in-process analytical SQL engine ("SQLite for analytics") that's superb for local analysis and even lightweight pipelines. Using Spark for a 50 MB dataset is over-engineering — small data deserves small tools.

3.5 Choosing by data size + team skill

Data sizeTeam skillReach for
Fits in warehouse, SQL workloadsSQL-strongdbt on the warehouse
Large / needs programmatic logic / MLPython/ScalaSpark / Databricks
Small (fits in memory)Pythonpandas / polars / duckdb
Simple, legacy, few modelsSQLWarehouse-native SQL
Pitfall: Defaulting to Spark for everything because it sounds impressive. Spark has real operational overhead (clusters, tuning, cost) that only pays off at scale. Most analytics data fits comfortably in a modern warehouse, where dbt is simpler, cheaper, and faster to develop in. Reach for distributed compute when the data actually demands it, not before.
Key takeaways:
  • dbt dominates SQL transformation via models, refs (DAG), tests, and docs — it brought engineering discipline to analytics SQL. Learn it.
  • Spark/Databricks handle big or programmatic transforms; the two increasingly coexist.
  • Warehouse-native SQL works but lacks testing/lineage; pandas/polars/duckdb are right for small data.
  • Choose by data size and team skill; don't reach for Spark until the data actually demands it.

4. Orchestration & workflow tools

Orchestration is what runs your pipelines on schedule, in the right order, with retries and alerting when things fail. Without it you have scripts; with it you have a system. This layer also carries a genuine philosophical split worth understanding.

4.1 The main options

  • Apache Airflow: the incumbent. You define workflows as DAGs in Python; it schedules tasks, manages dependencies, retries, and backfills. Ubiquitous, huge ecosystem, battle-tested. Weaknesses: task-centric (not data-aware by default), can be clunky, operationally heavy. If you learn one orchestrator, learn this — it's the most common in the market.
  • Dagster: a modern, asset-based orchestrator. You declare the data assets (tables) you want to exist and their dependencies; Dagster orchestrates to produce them, with strong typing, testing, and lineage built in. Growing fast, loved for developer experience.
  • Prefect: another modern orchestrator, Pythonic and lightweight, emphasizing dynamic workflows and ease of use.
  • ADF / Databricks Workflows: the native schedulers in Azure and Databricks. Chosen when you're already all-in on that platform and don't want a separate orchestrator.
  • cron: the humble OS scheduler. Reality check: plenty of small setups still run pipelines on cron. It has no dependency management, retries, or visibility — fine for one simple job, a trap as soon as you have several with dependencies.

4.2 Asset-based vs task-based philosophy

This is the key conceptual divide. Task-based (classic Airflow): you orchestrate actions — "run this task, then that task." The scheduler thinks in operations. Asset-based (Dagster, and dbt's model): you declare the data products you want — "this table should exist and depends on these tables" — and the tool figures out what to run to produce them. Asset thinking aligns orchestration with what you actually care about (fresh, correct tables) and gives lineage and freshness for free. The industry is drifting from task-thinking toward asset-thinking; understanding the distinction signals modern awareness.

4.3 What companies actually run

Honest reality: Airflow is still the most common in the market by a wide margin — most job descriptions list it, so it's the safe learn. Dagster/Prefect are growing, especially at modern/greenfield teams. ADF/Databricks Workflows dominate within their ecosystems. And a surprising amount of the world still runs on cron + a pile of scripts, which is often the "before" state you're hired to modernize.

Worked example: A team runs 60 dbt models and 15 ingests with interdependencies, plus a nightly reconciliation that must run only after all ingests finish. On cron this is a fragile web of "hope the 3am job finished before the 4am job." In Airflow you express the true dependency graph — ingests → dbt run → reconciliation — with retries and alerts, and Airflow guarantees order and tells you when anything fails. That's the leap from "scripts on a timer" to "an orchestrated system," and it's a classic modernization project you can drive.
Pitfall: Encoding dependencies as time offsets on cron ("job B at 4am because job A usually finishes by 3:45"). The day job A runs late, job B processes stale or missing data silently. Orchestrators exist precisely to replace time-based hope with real dependency edges. If you see time-coupled cron jobs in the wild, that's tech debt waiting to bite.
Key takeaways:
  • Orchestration schedules pipelines in dependency order with retries and alerts — it turns scripts into a system.
  • Airflow is the market incumbent (learn it); Dagster/Prefect are modern; ADF/Databricks Workflows are ecosystem-native; cron is the fragile baseline.
  • Task-based (Airflow) orchestrates actions; asset-based (Dagster/dbt) declares desired data products — the industry is drifting toward assets.
  • Never encode dependencies as cron time offsets; express real dependency edges in an orchestrator.

5. Storage, warehouse & lakehouse platforms

This layer is where your data lives and gets queried. It's the center of gravity of most stacks — the platform choice shapes everything else. The good news: the concepts (columnar storage, separation of compute and storage, table formats) transfer across all of them, so learning one teaches you most of the rest.

5.1 The main platforms and their positioning

  • Snowflake: cloud-native data warehouse, famed for ease of use, separation of storage and compute (scale each independently), and multi-cloud. The default modern warehouse for many SaaS/product companies. Strong SQL, low ops burden.
  • Databricks: the lakehouse pioneer — combines a data lake's cheap, flexible storage with a warehouse's performance and governance, built on Spark and the Delta table format. Strong for big data, ML, and unified batch+streaming. Snowflake and Databricks are the two heavyweights, converging toward each other.
  • BigQuery: Google Cloud's serverless warehouse — no clusters to manage, scales automatically, pay per query/storage. Excellent for GCP shops and bursty analytics.
  • Redshift: AWS's warehouse. Mature, deeply integrated with AWS; older architecture but still widespread in AWS-native companies.
  • Synapse / Microsoft Fabric: Azure's analytics platforms. Fabric is Microsoft's newer unified analytics offering (lake + warehouse + BI). The default in Azure/Microsoft-heavy enterprises, including many fintechs.

5.2 Table formats and the lakehouse

Under the lakehouse sits an open table format — Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, or Apache Hudi — that adds database features (ACID transactions, schema evolution, time travel, upserts) on top of plain files (Parquet) in object storage. This is what lets a data lake behave like a warehouse. Iceberg in particular is becoming an open standard that even the warehouses are adopting — a sign of the whole industry converging on open formats.

5.3 The consolidation trend

The historical split between "data lake" (cheap, flexible, files) and "data warehouse" (fast, structured, SQL) is collapsing into the lakehouse — one platform that does both. Simultaneously, the platforms are converging: Databricks added warehouse features and SQL; Snowflake added lakehouse/Iceberg and Python. Companies increasingly want one platform, not a lake plus a separate warehouse. Expect this consolidation to continue.

5.4 Picking for a project vs for a career

For a project, you pick the platform the company runs (usually dictated by their cloud) — you don't get to choose. For your career, don't over-index on one product; learn the concepts — columnar storage, compute/storage separation, partitioning/clustering, table formats, cost models — because they transfer directly. Someone who deeply understands Snowflake can be productive on BigQuery in weeks; the SQL and mental model carry over. Tools are the current job; concepts are the career (the recurring theme of this whole section).

Worked example: You know Snowflake well and interview at a Databricks shop. Instead of panicking about the tool gap, you map concepts: Snowflake's virtual warehouses ↔ Databricks clusters (compute separated from storage); Snowflake micro-partitions ↔ Delta files + partitioning; Snowflake Time Travel ↔ Delta time travel; both give ACID and SQL. You honestly say "I've worked in Snowflake; the concepts map cleanly to Databricks and I'd be productive quickly" — and you'd be right. That conceptual fluency is what platform knowledge really is.
Pitfall: Treating your résumé as "Snowflake engineer" or "Databricks engineer" rather than "data engineer who has used Snowflake." Over-specializing on one vendor makes you brittle to market shifts and undersells your transferable understanding. Lead with concepts and let the specific platform be an implementation detail you happen to know.
Key takeaways:
  • Snowflake and Databricks are the two heavyweights (warehouse vs lakehouse, converging); BigQuery, Redshift, and Synapse/Fabric are the cloud-native options.
  • Open table formats (Delta, Iceberg, Hudi) give files ACID, time travel, and upserts — the lakehouse foundation; Iceberg is becoming a shared standard.
  • The lake/warehouse split is consolidating into the lakehouse, and platforms are converging.
  • Pick the company's platform for a project; learn transferable concepts for a career — one platform teaches you most of the rest.

6. Observability, quality, catalog & the rest — and two real stacks

The final layer wraps everything: knowing your data is fresh and correct, knowing where it came from and who can use it, and delivering it to consumers. These tools are what separate a trustworthy platform from a pile of pipelines. We close with two honest end-to-end example stacks so the whole landscape clicks together.

6.1 Data observability & quality

  • Great Expectations / Soda: open frameworks for defining and running data-quality checks (expectations/assertions) as part of pipelines — the "test your data" tools that complement dbt tests.
  • Monte Carlo: a "data observability" platform that automatically monitors freshness, volume, schema, and anomalies across your warehouse and alerts on incidents (data downtime), often using ML to learn normal behavior. Chosen by larger teams to catch problems they didn't explicitly write a test for.

The distinction: quality tools (Great Expectations, Soda, dbt tests) check rules you define; observability tools (Monte Carlo) also detect anomalies you didn't think to check.

6.2 Data catalog & governance

  • DataHub: open-source metadata/catalog platform — discovery, lineage, and documentation of all your data assets.
  • Atlan / Collibra: commercial catalog + governance platforms. Atlan is modern and collaboration-focused; Collibra is enterprise governance-heavy (policies, stewardship, compliance) — common in regulated industries like fintech.

These answer "what data do we have, where did it come from, who owns it, and who's allowed to use it" — increasingly non-optional as data volumes and regulation grow.

6.3 BI, reverse ETL, feature stores, and the MLOps boundary

  • BI tools: Power BI (Microsoft ecosystem, ubiquitous in enterprises/fintech), Tableau (powerful visualization), Looker (code-defined semantic layer, GCP), Metabase (lightweight, open-source, popular at startups). These are how humans consume your marts.
  • Reverse ETL (e.g. Hightouch, Census): pushes warehouse data back out into operational tools (CRM, ad platforms) so teams act on it where they work. Closes the loop from analytics to action.
  • Feature stores (e.g. Feast): manage ML features for consistent training and serving (archetype f) — they sit on the DE/ML boundary.
  • The MLOps boundary: DE typically owns data up to clean features/tables; ML engineering owns model training, deployment, and serving. The line is fuzzy and you'll often work across it — the more of it you understand, the more valuable you are.

6.4 Two honest end-to-end stacks

A realistic fintech company stack: 
Sources:     core banking Postgres, card txn stream, KYC/CRM, ledger
Ingestion:   Debezium CDC (core DB) + ADF/Fivetran (SaaS) + Kafka (txn events)
Storage:     Azure — Databricks lakehouse (Delta) / Synapse/Fabric; strict data residency
Transform:   dbt + Spark; heavy business logic, audited
Orchestrate: Airflow (or Databricks Workflows)
Serve:       Power BI dashboards; regulatory + finance reporting
Observe:     Monte Carlo + Great Expectations; Collibra for governance/lineage
Governance:  PII masking, fine-grained access control, audit logs, retention policies
              — governance is FIRST-CLASS here, not an afterthought
Note how compliance reshapes every layer: CDC for auditable change history, strict residency in storage, audited transforms, heavy governance tooling. This is the environment you're targeting — its defining trait is that trust, lineage, and control matter as much as speed.
A realistic startup stack: 
Sources:     app Postgres, Stripe, product events, a few SaaS tools
Ingestion:   Fivetran/Airbyte (buy everything — few engineers)
Storage:     Snowflake or BigQuery (managed, low ops)
Transform:   dbt (SQL-first, small team, fast iteration)
Orchestrate: dbt Cloud scheduler / Dagster / lightweight Airflow
Serve:       Metabase or Looker; self-serve analytics
Observe:     dbt tests + freshness; maybe Soda later
Governance:  light — a few PII rules, basic access; formalized as they grow
The contrast tells the whole story: the startup buys everything to move fast with few people and keeps governance light; the fintech builds and controls more and makes governance first-class. Same seven layers, opposite pressures.
Pitfall: Assuming there's one "best" stack to memorize. The fintech and startup stacks above are both correct — for their context. In an interview, the impressive answer isn't reciting a tool list; it's reasoning about why a stack fits its constraints (team size, compliance, volume, budget). That reasoning is the durable skill this entire section has been building toward.
Key takeaways:
  • Quality tools (Great Expectations, Soda, dbt tests) check rules you define; observability (Monte Carlo) also catches anomalies you didn't.
  • Catalogs/governance (DataHub, Atlan, Collibra) answer what/where/who-owns/who-can-use — critical and non-optional in fintech.
  • BI tools deliver marts to humans; reverse ETL pushes data back to ops tools; feature stores and the MLOps boundary sit at the DE/ML edge.
  • Fintech stacks make governance first-class and control-heavy; startup stacks buy everything and stay light — same layers, opposite pressures. Reason about fit, don't memorize lists.

A–Z Glossary — Complete Theory

A precise, self-sufficient dictionary of the data engineering vocabulary, cross-referenced to the sections that treat each term in depth.

This glossary is a fast reference for every term used across the Bible. Each entry gives a two-to-four sentence definition that is precise enough to stand on its own, and points to the section where the concept is developed fully. Use it as your first stop when you meet an unfamiliar word: read the definition here to get oriented, then jump to the referenced section for the mechanics. Terms are grouped into five alphabetical blocks so you can scan quickly.

A–D

ACID
The four transactional guarantees: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state to valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't corrupt each other), and Durability (committed data survives crashes). Classic databases provide ACID; lakehouse formats like Delta bring ACID to files on object storage. See the Databases and Delta Lake sections.
AQE (Adaptive Query Execution)
A Spark feature that re-optimizes a query plan at runtime using actual statistics gathered during execution, rather than relying only on compile-time estimates. It can coalesce shuffle partitions, switch join strategies, and split skewed partitions on the fly. Covered in the PySpark section.
At-least-once
A delivery guarantee where every message is processed one or more times, so no data is lost but duplicates are possible. It is cheaper than exactly-once and is made safe by pairing it with idempotent writes. See the Kafka and pipeline-reliability material.
Avro
A row-oriented binary serialization format that stores the schema alongside the data and supports rich schema evolution. Because it is row-based it suits write-heavy and streaming workloads (e.g. Kafka messages) better than analytical scans. Contrast with columnar Parquet/ORC in the File Formats section.
Backfill
Re-running a pipeline over historical time ranges to populate or correct data that is missing or wrong — for example after a bug fix or a newly added column. Safe backfilling depends on idempotency so re-processing a date doesn't double-count. See the Airflow and Real DE Work sections.
Batch processing
Processing data in finite chunks on a schedule (hourly, daily) rather than continuously. It is simpler and cheaper than streaming and fits most analytics, at the cost of latency. Contrast with streaming in the Architectures section.
Broadcast join
A join strategy where one small table is copied ("broadcast") in full to every executor so the join happens locally, avoiding a shuffle of the large table. It is very fast when one side fits in memory but fails or wastes memory if the "small" side is actually large. Detailed in the PySpark section.
Bronze / Silver / Gold
The three layers of the medallion architecture: bronze holds raw ingested data as-is, silver holds cleaned and conformed data, and gold holds business-level aggregates ready for consumption. Data flows bronze → silver → gold, gaining quality and structure at each hop. See the Lakes & Architectures section.
Bucketing
Pre-hashing rows into a fixed number of files ("buckets") by a key so that joins and aggregations on that key can avoid a shuffle. Unlike partitioning, bucketing controls file count and is chosen for join performance. Covered in the PySpark and File Formats material.
CAP theorem
States that a distributed system facing a network partition (P) must choose between Consistency (C) and Availability (A) — it cannot fully guarantee both during the partition. It shapes design choices in distributed databases and message systems. Discussed in the System Design section.
CDC (Change Data Capture)
A technique for capturing inserts, updates, and deletes from a source database as a stream of change events, typically by reading the database's transaction log. It lets downstream systems stay in sync without full reloads. Central to the Kafka and ingestion material.
CDF (Change Data Feed)
A Delta Lake feature that records row-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) a table undergoes, so consumers can read just what changed between versions instead of rescanning the whole table. It powers incremental silver/gold processing. See the Delta Lake section.
Checkpoint
A saved snapshot of a streaming job's progress and state (including offsets processed) written to durable storage, so the job can restart after failure without reprocessing or losing data. In Spark Structured Streaming it also stores aggregation state. See the PySpark and Kafka sections.
Clustering key
In Snowflake, a column (or expression) chosen to co-locate related rows within micro-partitions so queries that filter on it scan far less data. It is a physical-layout hint, not an index. Covered in the Snowflake section.
Coalesce
A Spark operation that reduces the number of partitions without a full shuffle by merging existing partitions, used to cut down small output files. Unlike repartition it cannot increase partition count evenly. See the PySpark section.
Columnar storage
A physical layout that stores each column's values together rather than each row's values together. This makes analytical queries that touch few columns fast and compresses well because similar values sit adjacent. Parquet and ORC are columnar; see the File Formats section.
Compaction
Merging many small data files into fewer larger ones to fix the small-files problem and speed up reads. In Delta this is often done with OPTIMIZE. See the Delta Lake and File Formats sections.
Consumer group
In Kafka, a set of consumers that cooperatively read a topic, with each partition assigned to exactly one consumer in the group so work is shared and scaled. Different groups each get their own full copy of the stream. See the Kafka section.
CTE (Common Table Expression)
A named, temporary result set defined with WITH that you reference later in the same query, used to break complex SQL into readable steps and to enable recursion. It improves clarity but is not automatically materialized. See the SQL section.
DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)
A graph of tasks with directed dependencies and no cycles, defining what must run before what. Airflow models pipelines as DAGs, and Spark builds an internal DAG of transformations. See the Airflow and PySpark sections.
Data contract
An explicit, versioned agreement between a data producer and its consumers specifying schema, semantics, quality guarantees, and change rules. It prevents silent breakage when upstream teams change data. See the Data Quality and Real DE Work sections.
Data lake
A storage repository (usually cheap object storage) that holds raw and processed data in open file formats at any scale, without imposing a schema up front. It trades the guarantees of a warehouse for flexibility and cost. See the Lakes & Architectures section.
Data mart
A subject-focused subset of a data warehouse serving a single team or domain (e.g. a marketing mart). It offers simpler, faster access to a curated slice of the gold layer. See the Modeling and Warehouse material.
Data mesh
An organizational and architectural approach that treats data as a product owned by decentralized domain teams, with self-serve platform infrastructure and federated governance. It is a response to central data teams becoming bottlenecks. Discussed in the Architectures section.
Data warehouse
A system optimized for analytical queries over structured, modeled data, historically with schema-on-write and strong performance for aggregations. Snowflake, Redshift, and Synapse are examples. See the Databases, Modeling, and Cloud sections.
dbt (data build tool)
A framework for building transformation pipelines in the warehouse using version-controlled SQL, with built-in testing, documentation, and dependency management. It brings software-engineering practices to the "T" of ELT. See the Real DE Work and Modeling sections.
DBU (Databricks Unit)
Databricks' unit of processing consumption used for billing; a cluster consumes DBUs per hour at a rate depending on its type and tier. Managing DBUs is core to controlling Databricks cost. See the Databricks section.
Dead-letter queue (DLQ)
A separate destination where messages that cannot be processed successfully are diverted, so a bad record does not block the main stream. It preserves the failed data for inspection and later reprocessing. See the Kafka and Data Quality sections.
Delta Lake
An open storage layer that adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, and upserts on top of Parquet files, using a transaction log (the _delta_log). It turns a data lake into a lakehouse. Covered fully in the Delta Lake section.
Denormalization
Deliberately introducing redundancy (e.g. pre-joining tables) to make reads faster and simpler, at the cost of update complexity and storage. Analytical/dimensional models are typically denormalized. Contrast with normalization in the Modeling section.
Dimension
In dimensional modeling, a table of descriptive attributes (customer, product, date) that provides context for the measurements in fact tables. Dimensions are the "by what" you slice metrics. See the Modeling section.
DIU (Data Integration Unit)
Azure Data Factory's unit of compute power (a mix of CPU, memory, and network) allocated to a copy activity; more DIUs move data faster at higher cost. See the Azure section.
Docker
A tool for packaging an application and its dependencies into a portable image that runs identically as a container anywhere. It solves "works on my machine" and underpins reproducible pipeline environments. See the Docker section.

E–J

ELT
Extract, Load, Transform — a pattern where raw data is loaded into the warehouse/lake first and transformed in place using its compute. It suits cheap, scalable cloud warehouses and pairs with tools like dbt. Contrast with ETL in the Architectures section.
ETL
Extract, Transform, Load — the traditional pattern where data is transformed in a dedicated engine before loading into the target. It was standard when warehouse compute was expensive. See the Architectures section.
Exactly-once
The strongest processing guarantee: each record affects the result once and only once, even across failures and retries. It is achieved by combining offset tracking, transactions, and idempotent/atomic writes. See the Kafka and PySpark streaming material.
Executor
In Spark, a worker process running on a cluster node that executes tasks and holds data partitions in memory or on disk. The number and size of executors is a primary performance and cost lever. See the PySpark section.
Fact table
In dimensional modeling, the central table holding measurable business events (sales, clicks) as numeric measures plus foreign keys to dimensions. Its size and grain drive warehouse performance. See the Modeling section.
Fan-out
A pattern where one input event triggers many downstream messages or tasks — for example one order event producing per-item events. High fan-out amplifies load and must be sized for. Discussed in the Kafka and System Design sections.
Foreign key
A column whose values must match a primary key in another table, expressing a relationship between rows. In analytical stores it is often modeled but not enforced for performance. See the Databases and Modeling sections.
Grain
The precise level of detail one row of a fact table represents (e.g. "one row per order line per day"). Declaring the grain first is the foundational step of dimensional modeling; mixing grains corrupts aggregates. See the Modeling section.
Hash join
A join algorithm that builds an in-memory hash table on the smaller input's key, then probes it with the larger input. It is efficient for equality joins on unsorted data and is Spark's common shuffle-join mechanism. See the Databases and PySpark sections.
HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)
A distributed file system that stores large files as replicated blocks across many machines, designed for high-throughput sequential reads. It was the original big-data storage layer, now largely superseded by cloud object storage. See the Lakes and Big Data material.
Idempotency
The property that performing an operation multiple times yields the same result as performing it once. Idempotent writes make retries and backfills safe by preventing duplicates. It is one of the most important reliability concepts — see the pipeline-reliability and Real DE Work sections.
Incremental load
Loading only data that is new or changed since the last run, rather than reprocessing everything, using a watermark column or CDC. It cuts cost and runtime dramatically at scale. See the Airflow and ingestion material.
Index
An auxiliary data structure (often a B-tree) that lets a database find rows matching a predicate without scanning the whole table, trading write cost and storage for read speed. Analytical stores rely more on partitioning/clustering than indexes. See the Databases section.
ISR (In-Sync Replicas)
In Kafka, the set of partition replicas that are fully caught up with the leader. Requiring acknowledgment from the ISR set governs durability; if it shrinks too far, writes may be refused. See the Kafka section.
Isolation level
The setting that controls how much concurrent transactions can see each other's uncommitted or in-flight changes, ranging from read-uncommitted to serializable. Higher isolation prevents anomalies but reduces concurrency. See the Databases section on ACID.
JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)
A standard API and driver mechanism for connecting to relational databases, used by Spark and many tools to read from and write to databases over the network. Tuning JDBC parallelism matters for extraction performance. See the PySpark and ingestion material.
Join
An operation that combines rows from two tables based on a matching condition, in variants such as inner, left/right/full outer, and cross. Joins are central to both SQL and distributed processing, where their cost is dominated by shuffles. See the SQL and PySpark sections.

K–O

Kafka
A distributed, durable, log-based publish/subscribe streaming platform where producers write records to partitioned topics and consumers read them at their own pace by offset. It is the de-facto backbone for real-time data movement. See the Kafka section.
Kappa architecture
A streaming-only design that treats all data as a stream and reprocesses history by replaying the log, avoiding the separate batch layer of Lambda architecture. It simplifies the stack when stream reprocessing is feasible. Contrast with Lambda in the Architectures section.
Lakehouse
An architecture that adds warehouse features — ACID transactions, schema management, performant SQL — directly on top of a data lake's open files, unifying lake and warehouse. Delta Lake, Iceberg, and Hudi enable it. See the Lakes & Architectures section.
Lambda architecture
A design combining a batch layer for accurate, comprehensive historical views with a speed layer for low-latency recent data, merged at serving time. It is powerful but complex due to maintaining two code paths. See the Architectures section.
Late-arriving data
Records that reach a pipeline after their logical event time has already been processed, common in streaming due to network delays or offline devices. Handling it requires watermarks and update-capable sinks. See the Kafka and streaming material.
Lineage
The traceable record of where data came from and how it was transformed on its way to a given output — which sources, jobs, and columns fed it. It is essential for debugging, impact analysis, and governance. See the Data Quality and Real DE Work sections.
Materialized view
A precomputed, stored result of a query that is refreshed periodically or incrementally, giving fast reads at the cost of staleness and maintenance. Warehouses use them to accelerate expensive aggregations. See the Databases and Snowflake sections.
Medallion architecture
The bronze/silver/gold layering pattern for progressively refining data quality in a lakehouse: raw → cleaned → business-ready. It gives each stage clear responsibilities and reprocessing points. See the Lakes & Architectures section.
MERGE
A SQL/Delta statement that combines insert, update, and delete logic in one atomic operation based on whether source rows match a target — the standard way to perform upserts. It is the workhorse of incremental silver/gold loads. See the SQL and Delta Lake sections.
Micro-partition
In Snowflake, the automatic, immutable storage unit (a few hundred MB of columnar, compressed data) into which tables are divided. Snowflake tracks per-micro-partition metadata to prune scans; users never manage them directly. See the Snowflake section.
MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)
A concurrency technique where writes create new versions of data rather than overwriting, so readers see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers. It underlies isolation in many databases and in Delta's transaction log. See the Databases and Delta Lake sections.
Namespace
A logical grouping that qualifies and isolates names (schemas, catalogs, buckets, topics) to prevent collisions and organize resources. In lakehouse catalogs it forms part of the three-part table identifier. See the Platforms and governance material.
Normalization
Organizing relational data into related tables to eliminate redundancy and update anomalies, following normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF). It suits transactional (OLTP) systems; analytics often denormalizes instead. See the Databases and Modeling sections.
Offset
In Kafka, the monotonically increasing position of a record within a partition; consumers track their committed offset to know where to resume. Managing offsets correctly is the basis of at-least-once and exactly-once semantics. See the Kafka section.
OLAP
Online Analytical Processing — workloads characterized by large scans and aggregations over historical data for analysis and reporting. OLAP systems favor columnar storage and denormalized models. Contrast with OLTP; see the Databases section.
OLTP
Online Transaction Processing — workloads of many small, fast reads and writes on current data (e.g. an order being placed). OLTP systems favor row storage, indexes, and normalization. Contrast with OLAP; see the Databases section.
ORC (Optimized Row Columnar)
A columnar file format from the Hadoop ecosystem with strong compression, built-in indexes, and lightweight statistics for predicate pushdown. It is a peer of Parquet, historically favored in Hive. See the File Formats section.
Orchestration
The coordination of when and in what order pipeline tasks run, including scheduling, dependencies, retries, and alerting. Airflow is the canonical orchestrator. See the Airflow section.
Outbox pattern
A reliability pattern where a service writes a business change and an event record to the same database transaction (the "outbox" table), and a separate process later publishes those events. It guarantees the event and the data change are consistent, avoiding dual-write problems. See the Kafka and System Design sections.

P–S

Parquet
The dominant open columnar file format for analytics, storing data by column with rich statistics and per-column compression and encoding. It enables partition pruning and predicate pushdown, and underlies Delta and Iceberg. See the File Formats section.
Partition (data)
A physical division of a dataset — for example folders by date — so queries can read only relevant slices. Good partitioning speeds queries; over-partitioning creates the small-files problem. See the File Formats and PySpark sections.
Partition (Spark)
The unit of parallelism inside Spark: a chunk of a DataFrame processed by one task on one executor core. Too few partitions underuse the cluster; too many add overhead. See the PySpark section.
Partition pruning
The optimization of skipping entire data partitions that cannot match a query's filter, based on partition metadata, so far less data is read. It depends on filtering by partition columns. See the File Formats and PySpark sections.
Predicate pushdown
Pushing filter conditions down to the storage/scan layer so rows are eliminated as early as possible using file statistics, reducing I/O. Columnar formats like Parquet and ORC enable it. See the File Formats section.
Primary key
A column or set of columns that uniquely identifies each row in a table. In transactional systems it is enforced and indexed; in warehouses it is often logical rather than enforced. See the Databases and Modeling sections.
Producer
In Kafka, a client that publishes records to topics, choosing partitions and durability/acknowledgment settings that trade throughput against safety. See the Kafka section.
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
An authorization model where permissions are granted to roles and users acquire permissions by holding roles, simplifying governance at scale. Snowflake and cloud platforms use RBAC extensively. See the Platforms and governance material.
Rebalancing
In Kafka, the process of reassigning partitions among consumers in a group when membership changes, so work stays evenly distributed. Frequent rebalances cause processing pauses and are something to tune against. See the Kafka section.
Reconciliation
Systematically comparing counts, sums, or keys between source and target to prove a pipeline moved data correctly and completely. It is a core data-quality control, especially after backfills. See the Data Quality and Real DE Work sections.
Repartition
A Spark operation that reshuffles data into a specified number of partitions (or by a key) to balance load or prepare for a join. Unlike coalesce it triggers a full shuffle and can increase partition count. See the PySpark section.
Retention
How long data is kept before it is deleted or expired — for Kafka topics, Delta history, or warehouse time travel. Retention balances recoverability and replay needs against storage cost. See the Kafka and Delta Lake sections.
Salting
A skew-mitigation technique that adds a random prefix to a hot join/group key to spread its rows across multiple partitions, then removes it afterward. It relieves the straggler task caused by one dominant key value. See the PySpark section.
SCD (Slowly Changing Dimension)
Techniques for handling changes to dimension attributes over time: Type 1 overwrites, Type 2 adds a new versioned row with effective dates, Type 3 keeps limited prior values. SCD Type 2 preserves history for point-in-time analysis. See the Modeling section.
Schema evolution
The ability to change a dataset's schema over time — adding, renaming, or retyping columns — without rewriting or breaking existing data. Formats like Avro, Parquet, and Delta support it with rules. See the File Formats and Delta Lake sections.
Schema-on-read
Applying structure to data only when it is read, rather than when written, so raw data can land untyped and be interpreted flexibly later. It is the lake philosophy, contrasted with warehouse schema-on-write. See the Lakes section.
Schema-on-write
Enforcing and validating structure at load time, rejecting non-conforming data, so readers always get consistent types. It is the traditional warehouse approach. Contrast with schema-on-read in the Databases and Lakes sections.
Schema registry
A central service that stores and versions message schemas (e.g. for Avro on Kafka) and enforces compatibility rules so producers and consumers can evolve safely. It prevents a schema change from silently breaking consumers. See the Kafka section.
Shard
A horizontal partition of a database's data spread across multiple servers so no single node holds everything, enabling scale-out. Choosing a good shard key avoids hotspots. See the Databases and System Design sections.
Shuffle
The expensive redistribution of data across the network between Spark stages so that rows sharing a key land together — required by wide operations like joins, groupBy, and repartition. Minimizing shuffle is central to Spark tuning. See the PySpark section.
Skew
An uneven distribution of data across partitions where a few partitions hold far more rows than others, causing straggler tasks that dominate runtime. It is fixed with salting, AQE skew handling, or better keys. See the PySpark section.
SLA / SLO
A Service Level Agreement is a commitment about a service's behavior (e.g. "data ready by 6 a.m."), and a Service Level Objective is the internal target that supports it. In DE they anchor pipeline freshness and reliability expectations. See the Data Quality and Real DE Work sections.
Small files problem
A performance pathology where a dataset is split into very many tiny files, so metadata overhead and per-file I/O dominate and reads slow dramatically. It is fixed by compaction/OPTIMIZE and better partitioning. See the File Formats and Delta Lake sections.
Snowpipe
Snowflake's serverless, continuous data-ingestion service that loads files into tables shortly after they land in stage storage, rather than on a manual batch. It enables near-real-time loading. See the Snowflake section.
Spill
When a Spark task's data exceeds available memory and is written to local disk to avoid an out-of-memory error, at a large speed cost. Frequent spill signals too-large partitions or too-small executors. See the PySpark section.
Star schema
A dimensional model with one central fact table connected to surrounding dimension tables, forming a star shape. It is denormalized for fast, intuitive analytical queries and is the default warehouse pattern. See the Modeling section.
Stream (Snowflake)
A Snowflake object that tracks change data (a CDC-like offset) on a table so you can consume only what changed since last read, typically paired with a task for incremental processing. See the Snowflake section.
Streaming
Processing data continuously as unbounded events arrive, producing low-latency results, as opposed to finite batch runs. It introduces challenges like state, watermarks, and exactly-once. Contrast with batch in the Architectures section.
Surrogate key
A system-generated, meaningless unique identifier (often an integer or hash) used as a dimension's primary key instead of a natural business key. It stays stable across source changes and enables SCD Type 2 versioning. See the Modeling section.

T–Z

Task (Snowflake)
A scheduled or triggered unit of work in Snowflake that runs SQL (often consuming a stream) on a schedule or after another task, enabling in-warehouse pipelines. Tasks chain into simple DAGs. See the Snowflake section.
Throughput
The volume of data or number of events a system processes per unit time (e.g. records/second). It is distinct from latency (time per item); systems often trade one against the other. See the Kafka and System Design sections.
Time travel
Querying a table as it existed at a past version or timestamp, supported by keeping historical file versions and a transaction log. It enables audits, rollbacks, and reproducible reads. See the Delta Lake and Snowflake sections.
Tombstone
A marker recording that a row or file has been deleted, kept temporarily so deletions propagate and time travel/compaction behave correctly before the data is physically purged. In Delta, VACUUM eventually removes tombstoned files. See the Delta Lake and Kafka (log compaction) sections.
Topic (Kafka)
A named, partitioned, append-only log to which producers write and from which consumer groups read. Partitions within a topic provide ordering (per partition) and parallelism. See the Kafka section.
Transaction log
An ordered, durable record of every change committed to a store, used to guarantee atomicity, enable recovery, and support features like CDC and time travel. Delta's _delta_log and a database's WAL are examples. See the Delta Lake and Databases sections.
UDF (User-Defined Function)
A custom function you register to use within SQL or Spark when built-in functions are insufficient. In Spark, Python UDFs can be slow due to serialization overhead, so native or pandas/vectorized UDFs are preferred. See the PySpark and SQL sections.
Upsert
An operation that inserts a row if its key is new and updates it if the key already exists — "update or insert". It is implemented with MERGE and is fundamental to incremental loads and CDC application. See the SQL and Delta Lake sections.
VACUUM
A Delta Lake command that permanently deletes data files no longer referenced by the transaction log beyond a retention window, reclaiming storage. Setting the retention too short can break time travel and concurrent readers. See the Delta Lake section.
VARIANT
A semi-structured data type (notably in Snowflake) that stores JSON-like nested data in a single column, queryable with path expressions without a fixed schema. It bridges structured and semi-structured data. See the Snowflake section.
WAL (Write-Ahead Log)
A durability mechanism where changes are first appended to a sequential log before being applied to the main data, so a crash can be recovered by replaying the log. It underlies ACID durability and CDC. See the Databases section.
WAP (Write-Audit-Publish)
A data-quality pattern where new data is written to a staging/branch location, audited against quality checks, and only published (made visible to consumers) if it passes. It prevents bad data from reaching users. See the Data Quality section.
Warehouse (Snowflake compute)
In Snowflake, a "virtual warehouse" is a named cluster of compute that executes queries, sized T-shirt style and billed while running, independent of storage. Separating compute from storage lets many warehouses share one dataset. See the Snowflake section.
Watermark
In streaming, a moving threshold on event time that declares how late data may still be accepted; events older than the watermark are treated as too late and dropped or handled specially. It bounds the state a streaming aggregation must keep. See the PySpark and Kafka sections.
Window function
A SQL function that computes a value across a set of rows related to the current row (a "window") without collapsing them, using OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...). It powers ranking, running totals, and deduplication. See the SQL section.
Z-ORDER
A Delta optimization that co-locates related rows across multiple columns using a space-filling curve, so multi-column filters skip more files (better data-skipping) than single-column ordering. Applied via OPTIMIZE ZORDER BY. See the Delta Lake section.
Zero-copy clone
Creating an instant, independent logical copy of a table (or database) that shares the original's underlying storage until either side changes, using metadata pointers. It enables cheap dev/test environments and snapshots. See the Snowflake and Delta shallow-clone material.
Zone (landing/raw/curated)
A logical area of a data lake reflecting data maturity — e.g. landing/raw for untouched ingested data and curated/trusted for cleaned, governed data — often mapped onto the medallion bronze/silver/gold layers. Zones organize access and lifecycle policies. See the Lakes & Architectures section.
